topics.title: Introduction to Multimedia - Components of Multimedia
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECNT742 - MULTIMEDIA COMPRESSION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Introduction to Multimedia - Components of Multimedia

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Multimedia** is the integration of multiple forms of digital media — **text, graphics, audio, images, animation, and video** — into a single, interactive, computer-based application that is delivered electronically to a user through a unified presentation framework.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context (PECNT742 – Multimedia Compression), multimedia is treated as a **data-intensive, time-sensitive, multi-modal information system** where each media type has its own:

- **Representation model** (how it is encoded in binary),
- **Perceptual characteristics** (how the human sensory system interprets it),
- **Compression requirements** (lossless vs. lossy techniques).

> [!NOTE]
> **Related Terminology (Board-Favourite Definitions):**
> - **Media (singular of *medium*)** — A carrier or channel through which information is transmitted or stored (e.g., text, sound).
> - **Hypermedia** — A non-linear, link-based organization of multimedia elements allowing user-driven navigation (e.g., the World Wide Web).
> - **Multimedia System** — The complete hardware + software infrastructure required to capture, store, process, transmit, and present multimedia content.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — A Student's First Read

> [!TIP]
> **Analogy: The Modern Smartphone Home Screen**
>
> Imagine tapping on your phone's home screen. You see:
> - A **text notification** ("3 new messages"),
> - A **background image** (your wallpaper),
> - A **sound alert** (the notification tone),
> - A **short video preview** (incoming video message),
> - An **animated icon** (a refreshing app loader),
> - A **2D vector graphic** (the app icon).
>
> All of these together form a **multimedia experience**. Each element is a *medium*, and the way they are *combined, synchronized, and presented* is what we call multimedia.
>
> **Compression** is the reason your phone can store 1,00,000 photos and 200 hours of video — it squeezes the same information into a smaller "suitcase" without losing what truly matters to your eyes and ears.

---

## 1.3 Why Multimedia Matters — Engineering Relevance

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Human brain processes visuals 60,000× faster than text.** (3M Corporation study — standard KTU reference).
> - Modern digital ecosystems (OTT, IoT, telemedicine, AR/VR) are all *multimedia-native*.
> - For a Multimedia Compression engineer, understanding *what* the components are is the **prerequisite** to understanding *how* to compress them.

---

## 1.4 Distinguishing Multimedia from Related Terms

| Term | Meaning | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Unimedia** | Single medium content | A plain `.txt` document |
| **Multimedia** | Combination of $\geq 2$ media types | A PPT slide with audio narration |
| **Hypermedia** | Multimedia + non-linear links | A webpage with clickable hyperlinks |
| **Interactive Multimedia** | User controls the flow | A video game |
| **Virtual Reality (VR)** | Immersive, simulated multimedia | A VR headset experience |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Six Core Components of Multimedia

> [!NOTE]
> **Mnemonic for Exam**: **"TIGAVA"** — **T**ext, **I**mages, **G**raphics, **A**udio, **V**ideo, **A**nimation.

### 2.1.1 Text
- **Definition:** The fundamental alphanumeric medium — the *raw* form of data in computing.
- **Representation:** ASCII (**7-bit**, 128 characters), Extended ASCII (**8-bit**, 256 characters), Unicode (UTF-8, UTF-16) for global languages.
- **Storage:** Plain text size = (Number of characters) $\times$ (bytes per character).
- **Compression Relevance:** Text is the easiest to compress losslessly — techniques like **Huffman Coding, LZW, and Run-Length Encoding (RLE)** are standard.

> **Example:** A 1,000-character ASCII file = $1000 \times 1$ byte = **1000 bytes (1 KB)**.

---

### 2.1.2 Images (Still Images / Raster)
- **Definition:** A 2D grid (matrix) of **pixels** (picture elements), each with a colour value.
- **Common Formats:** BMP (uncompressed), JPEG/JFIF (lossy), PNG (lossless), GIF, TIFF, WebP.
- **Resolution:** Width $\times$ Height in pixels (e.g., 1920 $\times$ 1080 = **Full HD**).
- **Colour Depth:** Bits per pixel (bpp) — 1 (monochrome), 8 (grayscale), 24 (true colour), 32 (RGBA).

---

### 2.1.3 Graphics (Vector / Object-based)
- **Definition:** Images defined by **mathematical equations, coordinates, and attributes** rather than pixel grids.
- **Common Formats:** SVG, EPS, AI, CDR.
- **Key Advantage:** **Infinitely scalable** with **zero quality loss** — critical for logos, CAD, typography.
- **Equation basis:** Bezier curves, B-splines, polygons, parametric lines.

> **Comparison Point (Board Favourite):**
> *Image* stores WHAT is at each pixel. *Graphic* stores HOW to DRAW the shape.

---

### 2.1.4 Audio
- **Definition:** A continuous, 1D time-varying signal representing sound pressure waves, sampled and quantized for digital storage.
- **Sampling Theorem (Nyquist–Shannon):** $f_s \geq 2 \times f_{max}$ to avoid aliasing.
- **Standard Rates:** 8 kHz (telephony), 44.1 kHz (CD quality), 48 kHz (studio), 96 kHz (Hi-Res).
- **Quantization:** 8-bit, 16-bit, 24-bit.
- **Channels:** Mono (1), Stereo (2), 5.1, 7.1 surround.

---

### 2.1.5 Video
- **Definition:** A **temporal sequence of still images (frames)** displayed at a frame rate fast enough to create the illusion of motion (typically $\geq 24$ fps).
- **Frame Rate (fps):** 24 (cinema), 25 (PAL), 30 (NTSC), 60 (HD streaming), 120 (gaming/VR).
- **Standards:** MPEG-1, MPEG-2, MPEG-4 (H.264/AVC), H.265/HEVC, AV1, VVC (H.266).
- **Key Insight:** Video is the **most data-hungry** medium — this is why compression (the course itself!) is vital.

---

### 2.1.6 Animation
- **Definition:** The **illusion of motion** created by displaying a sequence of 2D/3D objects over time, governed by keyframes and tweening.
- **Two Types:**
  1. **2D Animation** — Frame-by-frame (e.g., traditional cartoons) OR vector-based (e.g., Adobe Animate, Flash).
  2. **3D Animation** — Object-based with virtual cameras, lighting, and physics (e.g., Pixar, Blender).
- **Frame Rate for Animation:** Typically **24–30 fps** for smooth perception.

---

## 2.2 Properties of a Multimedia System (KTU 2024 Module-1 Outcomes)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A *multimedia system* must satisfy four canonical properties — these are **direct exam questions**:
>
> 1. **Computer-Controlled** — Digital integration and presentation.
> 2. **Integrated** — All media types are stored, accessed, and presented through a **unified** framework.
> 3. **Interactive** — User can control the flow, pace, and content.
> 4. **Digital** — All media is represented in binary (discrete-time, discrete-amplitude form).

> **Optional 5th Property** (sometimes in textbooks): **Networked** — supports distributed access and streaming.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!TIP]
> **Master these formulas — they appear in 7-mark and 14-mark problems across KTU question papers.**

$$
\boxed{
\begin{aligned}
\textbf{1. Text File Size} &: \; S_T = N_c \times B_c \quad \text{(bytes)} \\
\textbf{2. Image File Size (uncompressed)} &: \; S_I = W \times H \times D \quad \text{(bits)} \\
&\; \text{or} \; S_I = \dfrac{W \times H \times D}{8} \quad \text{(bytes)} \\
\textbf{3. Colour Palette Size} &: \; C = 2^{D} \quad \text{(number of distinct colours)} \\
\textbf{4. Audio File Size (uncompressed)} &: \; S_A = f_s \times n \times c \times t \quad \text{(bits)} \\
\textbf{5. Video File Size (uncompressed)} &: \; S_V = W \times H \times D \times R \times t \quad \text{(bits)} \\
\textbf{6. Compression Ratio} &: \; CR = \dfrac{\text{Original Size}}{\text{Compressed Size}} \\
\textbf{7. Space Savings (\%)} &: \; SS(\%) = \left(1 - \dfrac{1}{CR}\right) \times 100 \\
\textbf{8. Nyquist Sampling Rate} &: \; f_s \geq 2 \cdot f_{max} \\
\textbf{9. Bit Rate} &: \; BR = f_s \times n \times c \quad \text{(bits/second)} \\
\textbf{10. SNR (Signal-to-Noise Ratio)} &: \; SNR_{dB} = 6.02 \cdot n + 1.76
\end{aligned}
}
$$

> **Legend / Units:**
> - $N_c$ = number of characters, $B_c$ = bytes per character.
> - $W, H$ = image width, height in pixels.
> - $D$ = colour depth in bits per pixel.
> - $f_s$ = sampling frequency (Hz), $n$ = bits per sample, $c$ = number of channels.
> - $R$ = frame rate (fps), $t$ = duration (seconds).

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Field | Application |
|---|---|
| **Telemedicine** | Compressed DICOM medical imaging over low-bandwidth networks |
| **OTT Streaming** (Netflix/Prime) | H.265/AV1 video compression for 4K HDR delivery |
| **Video Conferencing** (Zoom/Meet) | Real-time audio + video compression with low latency |
| **IoT & Surveillance** | Motion-JPEG + delta encoding for CCTV streams |
| **Gaming & VR** | Texture compression (BC/DXT), mesh compression, perceptual audio |
| **E-Learning (KTU NEP 2020)** | Compressed lecture videos, animations, interactive simulations |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Image Storage Size from Resolution & Colour Depth

**Problem Statement:**
> A 24-bit colour image has a resolution of 1024 $\times$ 768 pixels. Calculate its **uncompressed storage size** in bytes, KB, and MB.

### Step-by-Step Solution

> **Step 1 — Identify the given parameters**
> - Width: $W = 1024$ pixels
> - Height: $H = 768$ pixels
> - Colour depth: $D = 24$ bits/pixel
> - Number of pixels (total): $P = W \times H$

> **Step 2 — Calculate total number of pixels**
> $$
> P = 1024 \times 768
> $$
> $$
> \begin{aligned}
> P &= 1024 \times 768 \\
> &= 786\,432 \text{ pixels}
> \end{aligned}
> $$

> **Step 3 — Calculate total size in bits**
> Each pixel stores $D$ bits, so:
> $$
> S_{bits} = P \times D
> $$
> $$
> \begin{aligned}
> S_{bits} &= 786\,432 \times 24 \\
> &= 18\,874\,368 \text{ bits}
> \end{aligned}
> $$

> **Step 4 — Convert bits to bytes (divide by 8)**
> $$
> S_{bytes} = \dfrac{S_{bits}}{8}
> $$
> $$
> \begin{aligned}
> S_{bytes} &= \dfrac{18\,874\,368}{8} \\
> &= 2\,359\,296 \text{ bytes}
> \end{aligned}
> $$

> **Step 5 — Convert to KB and MB**
> $$
> \begin{aligned}
> S_{KB} &= \dfrac{2\,359\,296}{1024} = 2304 \text{ KB} \\
> S_{MB} &= \dfrac{2304}{1024} = 2.25 \text{ MB}
> \end{aligned}
> $$

> **Final Answer:**
> $$
> \boxed{S_{image} = 2.25 \text{ MB (uncompressed)}}
> $$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Uncompressed Audio Storage

**Problem Statement:**
> A 5-minute audio file is recorded in stereo (2 channels) at 44.1 kHz with 16-bit samples. Compute its size in MB and the bit rate in kbps.

### Step-by-Step Solution

> **Step 1 — Identify given parameters**
> - $f_s = 44\,100$ Hz
> - $n = 16$ bits/sample
> - $c = 2$ channels
> - $t = 5$ minutes $= 300$ seconds

> **Step 2 — Compute the bit rate**
> $$
> BR = f_s \times n \times c
> $$
> $$
> \begin{aligned}
> BR &= 44\,100 \times 16 \times 2 \\
> &= 1\,411\,200 \text{ bits/second} \\
> &= 1411.2 \text{ kbps}
> \end{aligned}
> $$

> **Step 3 — Compute the total audio file size in bits**
> $$
> S_A = f_s \times n \times c \times t
> $$
> $$
> \begin{aligned}
> S_A &= 44\,100 \times 16 \times 2 \times 300 \\
> &= 423\,360\,000 \text{ bits}
> \end{aligned}
> $$

> **Step 4 — Convert to MB**
> $$
> S_{MB} = \dfrac{S_A}{8 \times 1024 \times 1024}
> $$
> $$
> \begin{aligned}
> S_{MB} &= \dfrac{423\,360\,000}{8 \times 1\,048\,576} \\
> &= \dfrac{423\,360\,000}{8\,388\,608} \\
> &\approx 50.46 \text{ MB}
> \end{aligned}
> $$

> **Final Answer:**
> $$
> \boxed{BR = 1411.2 \text{ kbps}, \quad S_A \approx 50.46 \text{ MB}}
> $$

> [!NOTE]
> This is precisely why a 3-minute song in WAV form occupies ~30 MB, while the same song as **MP3 (128 kbps)** is only ~2.8 MB — a **compression ratio** of approximately **10:1**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Uncompressed Video Storage (Worst-Case Estimate)

**Problem Statement:**
> A 10-minute 1080p (1920 $\times$ 1080), 24-bit colour, 30 fps video is captured uncompressed. Find its storage size in GB.

### Step-by-Step Solution

> **Step 1 — Identify parameters**
> - $W = 1920$, $H = 1080$, $D = 24$, $R = 30$ fps, $t = 600$ s.

> **Step 2 — Per-frame size**
> $$
> S_{frame} = W \times H \times D \text{ bits}
> $$
> $$
> \begin{aligned}
> S_{frame} &= 1920 \times 1080 \times 24 \\
> &= 49\,766\,400 \text{ bits} \approx 6.22 \text{ MB/frame}
> \end{aligned}
> $$

> **Step 3 — Per-second size (bit rate)**
> $$
> BR = S_{frame} \times R
> $$
> $$
> \begin{aligned}
> BR &= 49\,766\,400 \times 30 \\
> &= 1\,492\,992\,000 \text{ bps} \approx 1.49 \text{ Gbps}
> \end{aligned}
> $$

> **Step 4 — Total size**
> $$
> S_V = BR \times t
> $$
> $$
> \begin{aligned}
> S_V &= 1\,492\,992\,000 \times 600 \\
> &= 895\,795\,200\,000 \text{ bits} \\
> &= 111.97 \times 10^9 \text{ bytes} \approx 104.3 \text{ GB}
> \end{aligned}
> $$

> **Final Answer:**
> $$
> \boxed{S_V \approx 104.3 \text{ GB (uncompressed)}}
> $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Implication for Compression (Module-1 hook to the rest of PECNT742):**
> A 10-minute 4K HDR YouTube video is only ~1.5 GB on disk because of H.265/HEVC compression achieving a **CR $\approx$ 70:1**. *This is exactly the engineering motivation for this course.*

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Compression Ratio & Space Savings

**Problem Statement:**
> A 200 MB raw image is compressed to 25 MB. Calculate the **Compression Ratio (CR)** and **Space Savings percentage**.

### Step-by-Step Solution

> **Step 1 — Compression Ratio**
> $$
> CR = \dfrac{\text{Original Size}}{\text{Compressed Size}}
> $$
> $$
> \begin{aligned}
> CR &= \dfrac{200}{25} \\
> &= 8
> \end{aligned}
> $$

> **Step 2 — Space Savings**
> $$
> SS(\%) = \left(1 - \dfrac{1}{CR}\right) \times 100
> $$
> $$
> \begin{aligned}
> SS(\%) &= \left(1 - \dfrac{1}{8}\right) \times 100 \\
> &= \left(1 - 0.125\right) \times 100 \\
> &= 87.5\%
> \end{aligned}
> $$

> **Final Answer:**
> $$
> \boxed{CR = 8:1, \quad SS = 87.5\%}
> $$

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Component-Wise Compression Strategies

| Component | Data Type | Spatial Redundancy | Temporal Redundancy | Psycho-visual Redundancy | Typical Compression |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Text** | Symbolic | None | None | Low | Huffman, LZW, LZ77 |
| **Image** | 2D Pixel Grid | High (DCT, DWT) | None | High (JPEG quant.) | JPEG, JPEG2000, WebP |
| **Graphics** | Vector | Low (math-based) | None | Low | SVGZ (gzip), FLIF |
| **Audio** | 1D Waveform | None (transform domain) | None | High (masking) | MP3 (AAC), Opus, FLAC |
| **Video** | 3D (x,y,t) | High (I-frame) | Very High (P/B-frames) | Very High | H.264, H.265, AV1, VVC |
| **Animation** | 2D/3D | Medium | Medium | High | Lottie (vector), GIF, APNG |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Figure 1 — The Six Core Components of Multimedia (TIGAVA)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    MM[Multimedia System]
    MM --> TXT[Text]
    MM --> IMG[Images / Raster]
    MM --> GRX[Graphics / Vector]
    MM --> AUD[Audio]
    MM --> VID[Video]
    MM --> ANI[Animation]

    TXT --> TXT1["ASCII Unicode UTF-8 UTF-16"]
    IMG --> IMG1["BMP JPEG PNG TIFF WebP"]
    GRX --> GRX1["SVG EPS AI CDR Bezier Curves"]
    AUD --> AUD1["WAV MP3 AAC FLAC Opus 8kHz 44.1kHz 48kHz"]
    VID --> VID1["MPEG-2 MPEG-4 H.264 H.265 AV1 VVC"]
    ANI --> ANI1["2D Frame Tweening 3D Keyframe Physics"]

    IMG --> SUB1[Most Compressed]
    VID --> SUB2[Most Data Intensive]
```

---

## 4.2 Figure 2 — Multimedia System Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    CAPT[Capture Stage]
    STORE[Storage Stage]
    PROC[Processing Stage]
    DIST[Distribution Stage]
    PRES[Presentation Stage]
    USER[End User]

    CAPT --> |Digitization| STORE
    STORE --> |Retrieval| PROC
    PROC --> |Compression and Encoding| DIST
    DIST --> |Streaming or Transfer| PRES
    PRES --> |Multimodal Sensory Output| USER
    USER --> |Feedback and Interaction| PROC
```

> **Explanation of Stages:**
> 1. **Capture** — Analog-to-Digital Conversion (ADC) for audio/video; scanners for images; keyboards for text.
> 2. **Storage** — Files, databases, cloud storage. Total capacity = $\sum$ (component sizes from Section 3).
> 3. **Processing** — Editing, filtering, **compression (lossy/lossless)**.
> 4. **Distribution** — CD/DVD, LAN, Internet, 5G/6G.
> 5. **Presentation** — Display, speakers, haptic devices.
> 6. **User Feedback** — Closes the loop for *interactive* multimedia.

---

## 4.3 Figure 3 — Compression Decision Flow (Module-1 → Module-2 Bridge)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START([Raw Multimedia Input])
    Q1{Is Loss of Information Acceptable?}
    START --> Q1
    Q1 -->|Yes Lossy| Q2{Target Perceptual Quality?}
    Q1 -->|No Lossless| Q3[Use Huffman LZW FLAC PNG]

    Q2 -->|High Quality| Q4[Transform Coding DCT DWT Quantization]
    Q2 -->|Low Latency| Q5[Predictive Coding Delta DPCM]
    Q2 -->|High Compression| Q6[Deep Learning Autoencoders]

    Q4 --> OUT([Compressed Output])
    Q5 --> OUT
    Q6 --> OUT
    Q3 --> OUT
```

> This figure visually links **Module 1 (Components)** to **Module 2 onwards (Compression Techniques)** — the core theme of PECNT742.

---

## 4.4 Figure 4 — Data Dominance Hierarchy of Media

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    L0[Data Size Hierarchy]
    L0 --> L1[Text Smallest]
    L0 --> L2[Graphics Vector]
    L0 --> L3[Audio Mono to Stereo]
    L0 --> L4[Images Resolution Dependent]
    L0 --> L5[Animation Frame Dependent]
    L0 --> L6[Video Largest Spatial plus Temporal]

    L6 --> NOTE[Video Dominates Bandwidth and Storage]
    NOTE --> N1[Example: 10 min 4K Video is approximately 100 GB Uncompressed]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Never confuse "Image" and "Graphics".** A raster *image* stores pixels; *vector graphics* store equations. Marks are deducted in Q&A when students interchange them.
> - When asked for "components of multimedia", write **TIGAVA** (all six) — do not omit Animation.
> - In numerical problems, **unit consistency** is critical: $1 \text{ KB} = 1024$ bytes, $1 \text{ MB} = 1024$ KB, $1 \text{ GB} = 1024$ MB. A common error is using $10^6$ instead of $2^{20}$.
> - For audio/video, **always state sampling rate, bit depth, and channels** before substituting values.

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**Define multimedia. List its six main components.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Definition:**
> Multimedia is the computer-controlled, integrated, interactive presentation of text, images, audio, video, graphics, and animation using digital technology.
>
> **Six Components (TIGAVA):**
> 1. **T**ext
> 2. **I**mages
> 3. **G**raphics
> 4. **A**udio
> 5. **V**ideo
> 6. **A**nimation

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate between image and graphics.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Image | Graphics |
|---|---|---|
| Representation | Pixel matrix (raster) | Mathematical equations (vector) |
| Resolution | Fixed | Resolution-independent |
| File Size | Large (depends on resolution) | Small (depends on complexity) |
| Scaling | Loses quality (pixelation) | No quality loss |
| Examples | JPEG, PNG, BMP | SVG, EPS |
| Compression Suitability | Transform coding (DCT/DWT) | Lossless (gzip/SVGZ) |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

> **KTU Pattern:** Each Part-B question has two sub-parts — typically **(a) 7 marks** (descriptive/analytical) and **(b) 7 marks** (numerical/proof).

---

### **🔹 Question A (14 Marks)**

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme, Module 1, Choice 1]**
>
> **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **functional architecture of a multimedia system**. Describe the role of each stage. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A still image has dimensions 2048 $\times$ 1536 pixels with 24-bit colour depth. Calculate the **(i)** total storage in MB, **(ii)** number of distinct colours, and **(iii)** the new size if compressed to 2 MB — find the Compression Ratio and Space Savings. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution (a):**

**Multimedia System Architecture — Five Stages:**

1. **Capture / Acquisition Stage** — Converts real-world signals (light, sound) to digital form.
   - Tools: Cameras, microphones, scanners, sensors, keyboards.
2. **Storage / Database Stage** — Stores raw and processed media in databases, file systems, or cloud.
   - Storage requirement = $\sum$ (component sizes) — see Section 3 formulas.
3. **Processing / Editing Stage** — Editing, filtering, **compression**, encryption.
4. **Distribution / Network Stage** — Local network, Internet, 5G, broadcast.
5. **Presentation / Display Stage** — Visual (monitor/VR), audio (speakers), haptic (controllers).

**Bidirectional User Feedback** makes it *interactive* — a key KTU property.

**Block Diagram Reference:** See **Section 4, Figure 2** of these notes.

#### **Model Solution (b):**

> **Given:** $W = 2048$, $H = 1536$, $D = 24$ bits.
>
> **(i) Storage in MB:**
> $$
> \begin{aligned}
> S_{bits} &= 2048 \times 1536 \times 24 \\
> &= 75\,497\,472 \text{ bits} \\
> S_{MB} &= \dfrac{75\,497\,472}{8 \times 1024 \times 1024} \\
> &\approx 9.0 \text{ MB}
> \end{aligned}
> $$

> **(ii) Number of distinct colours:**
> $$
> C = 2^D = 2^{24} = 16\,777\,216 \text{ colours (16.7 M true-colour)}
> $$

> **(iii) Compression Ratio & Space Savings:**
> Compressed size = 2 MB
> $$
> \begin{aligned}
> CR &= \dfrac{9.0}{2.0} = 4.5 : 1 \\
> SS(\%) &= \left(1 - \dfrac{1}{4.5}\right) \times 100 \\
> &\approx 77.78\%
> \end{aligned}
> $$

---

### **🔹 Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme, Module 1, Choice 2]**
>
> **(a)** Explain the **properties of a multimedia system** in detail. Why is "interactivity" considered a defining property? **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A 4-minute stereo audio clip is sampled at 48 kHz with 16-bit resolution. Compute the **(i)** bit rate, **(ii)** total file size in MB, and **(iii)** the bit rate if the same audio is compressed to 192 kbps (MP3) — calculate the Compression Ratio. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution (a):**

**Four Canonical Properties:**

1. **Computer-Controlled** — All media handled digitally via CPU/GPU pipelines.
2. **Integrated** — Unified representation; text, audio, video share the same timeline/framework.
3. **Interactive** — The user can navigate, pause, select, and alter the flow of content.
4. **Digital** — All media is in binary/discrete form for storage and processing.

**Why Interactivity Matters:**
- It distinguishes multimedia from passive media (e.g., a movie reel).
- Enables applications: e-learning, video games, simulations, kiosks, VR.
- A non-interactive system is merely "multiform media," not true multimedia.

#### **Model Solution (b):**

> **Given:** $f_s = 48\,000$ Hz, $n = 16$ bits, $c = 2$ channels, $t = 4 \times 60 = 240$ s.
>
> **(i) Bit rate:**
> $$
> \begin{aligned}
> BR &= 48\,000 \times 16 \times 2 \\
> &= 1\,536\,000 \text{ bps} = 1536 \text{ kbps}
> \end{aligned}
> $$

> **(ii) Total file size:**
> $$
> \begin{aligned}
> S_A &= 1\,536\,000 \times 240 \\
> &= 368\,640\,000 \text{ bits} \\
> S_{MB} &= \dfrac{368\,640\,000}{8 \times 1\,048\,576} \\
> &\approx 43.95 \text{ MB}
> \end{aligned}
> $$

> **(iii) Compression ratio (1536 kbps → 192 kbps):**
> $$
> \begin{aligned}
> CR &= \dfrac{1536}{192} = 8 : 1 \\
> SS(\%) &= 87.5\%
> \end{aligned}
> $$
> Compressed size $\approx 43.95 / 8 \approx 5.49$ MB.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 1**

- **Multimedia** = Multi + Media; integration of $\geq 2$ digital media types.
- **Six components (TIGAVA):** Text, Images, Graphics, Audio, Video, Animation.
- **Text** — Simplest; lossless compression (Huffman, LZW).
- **Image** — 2D pixel grid; $S = W \times H \times D$ bits; $C = 2^D$ colours.
- **Graphics** — Vector/mathematical; lossless storage; **infinitely scalable**.
- **Audio** — 1D signal; $S = f_s \times n \times c \times t$; Nyquist theorem $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$.
- **Video** — Sequence of frames; **most data-intensive**; $S = W \times H \times D \times R \times t$.
- **Animation** — 2D/3D; keyframes + tweening; 24–30 fps typical.
- **4 Properties:** Computer-Controlled, Integrated, Interactive, Digital.
- **Compression Ratio (CR)** = Original / Compressed; $SS(\%) = (1 - 1/CR) \times 100$.
- **Uncompressed 4K 10-min video $\approx$ 104 GB** — motivates H.264/H.265/AV1 compression (the rest of PECNT742).
- **Unit conversion:** $1 \text{ KB} = 1024$ bytes; $1 \text{ MB} = 1024$ KB; $1 \text{ GB} = 1024$ MB.
- **Mnemonics:** TIGAVA (components); KTU standard: $W \times H \times D \times R \times t$ (video size).
- **Difference to remember:** Image = pixels; Graphics = equations; Audio = 1D samples; Video = 2D + time.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Graphics/Image Data Types
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECNT742 - MULTIMEDIA COMPRESSION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Graphics/Image Data Types

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the context of **Multimedia Compression (PECNT742)**, *Graphics/Image Data Types* refer to the standardized classification of visual data based on the source of origin (synthetic vs. natural), the internal representation model (raster vs. vector), the bit-depth (1-bit, 8-bit, 24-bit, etc.), and the color model (RGB, CMYK, YCbCr, HSV).

According to the KTU 2024 scheme, multimedia visual data is broadly partitioned into two primary domains:

- **Images (Natural / Captured):** Two-dimensional pixel arrays obtained from real-world scenes via optical sensors (cameras, scanners, satellites, MRI).
- **Graphics (Synthetic / Computer-Generated):** Visuals constructed mathematically from geometric primitives, equations, drawing commands, or rendering pipelines (CGI, CAD, charts, fonts).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition:** "A *bitmap* (or raster) image is a matrix of picture elements (pixels), each holding a color value. A *vector* graphic is a set of drawing instructions describing shapes geometrically."

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of a **photograph** and a **Microsoft Word SmartArt diagram** showing the same logo:

| The Photograph (Image) | The SmartArt Logo (Graphic) |
|------------------------|------------------------------|
| Saved as millions of tiny colored dots (pixels). | Saved as mathematical instructions: *“draw a circle of radius 50 at point (x, y) filled with red.”* |
| If you zoom in, you see blocky squares. | If you zoom in, it remains perfectly sharp at any size. |
| Hard to edit individual elements. | Easy to move, resize, or recolor each shape. |

This is the fundamental difference. A *photograph is data-intensive but realistic*, while a *graphic is compact and infinitely scalable*. Compression algorithms differ drastically between the two.

## 1.3 Key Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> - **Pixel (Picture Element):** The smallest addressable unit of a raster image.
> - **Resolution:** Measured in **PPI (Pixels Per Inch)** for screens and **DPI (Dots Per Inch)** for printing.
> - **Standard Reference Resolutions:** VGA = $640 \times 480$, HD = $1280 \times 720$, FHD = $1920 \times 1080$, 4K UHD = $3840 \times 2160$, 8K UHD = $7680 \times 4320$.
> - **Color Depth (bpp):** Bits used to represent the color of a single pixel (e.g., 1, 4, 8, 16, 24, 30, 36, 48).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Hierarchical Classification of Image Data Types

### A. By Internal Representation Model

1. **Raster (Bitmap) Images**
   - A finite, fixed grid of pixels arranged in rows and columns.
   - Each pixel stores a discrete color value (quantized).
   - Quality degrades when scaled up → *pixelation* and *aliasing*.
   - Examples: JPEG, PNG, BMP, TIFF, GIF.

2. **Vector Graphics**
   - Stored as a list of drawing commands: points, lines, curves (Bézier), polygons, and fill rules.
   - Resolution-independent; scales without loss.
   - Examples: SVG, EPS, PDF, AI, CDR.

3. **Hybrid (Compound) Formats**
   - Combine raster + vector + text (e.g., PDF, PostScript, Flash/SWF).
   - Used in publishing, print, and animation pipelines.

### B. By Color Depth (Bits Per Pixel — bpp)

| Type | Bit-Depth (bpp) | Colors Represented | Common Use |
|------|-----------------|---------------------|------------|
| Binary / Monochrome | **1 bpp** | $2^1 = 2$ | Fax, B/W documents, bitmaps |
| Indexed / 4-bit | 4 bpp | $2^4 = 16$ | Old GUI icons, CGA displays |
| Indexed / 8-bit | 8 bpp | $2^8 = 256$ | GIF, PNG-8, paletted game art |
| High Color (16-bit) | 16 bpp | $2^{16} = 65{,}536$ | Old displays, XGA |
| True Color (24-bit) | 24 bpp | $2^{24} \approx 16.7\,\text{M}$ | JPEG, PNG-24, digital photos |
| Deep Color (30-bit) | 30 bpp | $2^{30} \approx 1.07\,\text{B}$ | HDR displays, professional video |
| Deep Color (36-bit) | 36 bpp | $2^{36} \approx 68.7\,\text{B}$ | Medical imaging (DICOM) |
| Deep Color (48-bit) | 48 bpp | $2^{48} \approx 281.5\,\text{T}$ | Professional photography (TIFF) |

### C. By Source / Acquisition Type

- **Synthetic / Computer-Generated Graphics** (vector-dominant, low entropy, large smooth regions).
- **Natural / Photographic Images** (raster-dominant, high entropy, complex textures).
- **Hybrid (Mixed) Content** (e.g., a poster with a photo and overlaid text — common in print & web).

## 2.2 Why Does This Classification Matter in Compression?

The classification dictates the **compression strategy** chosen:

- **Natural Images (24-bit, high entropy, random noise-like content):**
  - Lossy transform coding → **DCT-based JPEG**, **DWT-based JPEG 2000**.
  - Exploits psycho-visual redundancy and spatial frequency decorrelation.

- **Graphics / Synthetic Images (sharp edges, flat colors, limited palette):**
  - Lossless dictionary/run-length coding → **LZW (GIF, TIFF)**, **DEFLATE (PNG, ZIP)**.
  - Exploits spatial redundancy (long runs of identical pixels).

- **Paletted 8-bit Images (e.g., GIF, retro games):**
  - Indexed-color compression with palette sorting, often combined with LZW.

- **Vector Graphics:**
  - Often compressed as text (gzip / DEFLATE for SVGz).
  - No pixel-based compression is applied at all.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All equations below are the **high-yield, board-favorite formulas** for Module 1 of PECNT742.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units |
|---|----------|---------|-------|
| 1 | Uncompressed File Size (bits) | $S_{bits} = W \times H \times b$ | bits |
| 2 | Uncompressed File Size (bytes) | $S_{bytes} = \dfrac{W \times H \times b}{8}$ | Bytes |
| 3 | Uncompressed File Size (KB) | $S_{KB} = \dfrac{W \times H \times b}{8 \times 1024}$ | KB |
| 4 | Total Distinct Colors | $N_{colors} = 2^{b}$ | dimensionless |
| 5 | Palette Memory (for 8-bit indexed) | $M_{palette} = 2^{8} \times 24 = 6144$ | bits = 768 Bytes |
| 6 | Image Aspect Ratio | $AR = \dfrac{W}{H}$ | ratio |
| 7 | Diagonal Resolution (pixels) | $D_{px} = \sqrt{W^{2} + H^{2}}$ | pixels |
| 8 | Physical Print Size | $L_{in} = \dfrac{\text{pixels}}{\text{DPI}}$ | inches |
| 9 | Storage with Compression Ratio $C_r$ | $S_{compressed} = \dfrac{S_{uncompressed}}{C_r}$ | bits |
| 10 | Compression Ratio | $C_r = \dfrac{S_{uncompressed}}{S_{compressed}}$ | dimensionless |
| 11 | Bits-per-pixel for RGB sub-sampled (4:2:0) | $b_{420} = 4.5$ | bpp |
| 12 | Compression Efficiency | $\eta = 1 - \dfrac{1}{C_r}$ | dimensionless |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In the KTU 2024 exam, **always state units explicitly** (bits, bytes, KB, MB). A missing unit is a guaranteed ½-mark deduction.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Medical Imaging (DICOM, 12–16 bpp grayscale):** Lossless archival mandated; uses JPEG-LS / JPEG 2000.
- **Satellite / Remote Sensing (16-bit multispectral):** Quantization-aware compression to preserve spectral signatures.
- **Web/Streaming (sRGB, 8-bit/channel):** Use perceptual coding (JPEG/WebP/AVIF).
- **Print & Publishing (CMYK 32 bpp, 300 DPI):** Hybrid pipelines (PDF → rasterization → CMYK separation).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Uncompressed Image File Size

The most fundamental derivation in Module 1 is the **uncompressed file-size formula** for a raster image.

### Step 1 — Start with a Single Pixel
A single pixel requires $b$ bits to encode its color value, where $b$ is the bit-depth (e.g., $b = 24$ for true-color RGB).

### Step 2 — Count the Pixels in a Row
A horizontal row of an image contains $W$ pixels (image width).

### Step 3 — Count All Pixels in the Image
The image has $H$ rows (image height). Therefore, the total number of pixels is:

$$
N_{px} = W \times H
$$

### Step 4 — Multiply Pixels by Bit-Depth
Total raw bits in the image:

$$
S_{bits} = N_{px} \times b = W \times H \times b
$$

### Step 5 — Convert to Bytes
Since $1$ byte $= 8$ bits:

$$
S_{bytes} = \dfrac{W \times H \times b}{8}
$$

### Step 6 — Convert to Kilobytes / Megabytes

$$
S_{KB} = \dfrac{W \times H \times b}{8 \times 1024} \quad , \quad S_{MB} = \dfrac{W \times H \times b}{8 \times 1024 \times 1024}
$$

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Style)

**Problem:** A photograph has dimensions $1920 \times 1080$ pixels with 24-bit true color. Calculate (a) total pixels, (b) uncompressed file size in MB, (c) compressed size if JPEG achieves a compression ratio of 12:1.

### Part (a) — Total Pixels

$$
N_{px} = W \times H = 1920 \times 1080
$$

$$
N_{px} = 2{,}073{,}600 \text{ pixels} \approx 2.07 \text{ megapixels}
$$

### Part (b) — Uncompressed Size in MB

$$
S_{bytes} = \dfrac{1920 \times 1080 \times 24}{8}
$$

Let us simplify step by step:

$$
1920 \times 1080 = 2{,}073{,}600
$$

$$
2{,}073{,}600 \times 24 = 49{,}766{,}400 \text{ bits}
$$

$$
49{,}766{,}400 \div 8 = 6{,}220{,}800 \text{ bytes}
$$

$$
6{,}220{,}800 \div 1024 = 6075 \text{ KB}
$$

$$
6075 \div 1024 \approx 5.93 \text{ MB}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** A single uncompressed 1080p photograph occupies **~5.93 MB**. A 1 GB SD card stores only ~168 such photos — proving why compression is essential.

### Part (c) — Compressed Size

$$
S_{compressed} = \dfrac{S_{uncompressed}}{C_r} = \dfrac{5.93}{12} \approx 0.494 \text{ MB} \approx 494 \text{ KB}
$$

### Compression Efficiency

$$
\eta = 1 - \dfrac{1}{C_r} = 1 - \dfrac{1}{12} = 1 - 0.0833 = 0.9167 \approx 91.67\,\%
$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Total Distinct Colors from Bit-Depth

Each pixel can take one of $N$ possible values. With $b$ bits per pixel, the number of distinct combinations is:

$$
N_{colors} = 2^{b}
$$

**Examples:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
b = 1 &\Rightarrow N = 2^{1} = 2 \text{ (monochrome)} \\
b = 4 &\Rightarrow N = 2^{4} = 16 \text{ (CGA)} \\
b = 8 &\Rightarrow N = 2^{8} = 256 \text{ (GIF)} \\
b = 16 &\Rightarrow N = 2^{16} = 65{,}536 \text{ (High Color)} \\
b = 24 &\Rightarrow N = 2^{24} = 16{,}777{,}216 \text{ (True Color)}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Diagonal Resolution & Physical Print Size

A monitor advertises “Full HD” ($1920 \times 1080$). The **diagonal pixel count** is:

$$
D_{px} = \sqrt{W^{2} + H^{2}} = \sqrt{1920^{2} + 1080^{2}}
$$

$$
D_{px} = \sqrt{3{,}686{,}400 + 1{,}166{,}400} = \sqrt{4{,}852{,}800}
$$

$$
D_{px} \approx 2202.9 \text{ pixels (diagonal)}
$$

For a **24-inch** diagonal monitor, the **PPI** is:

$$
\text{PPI} = \dfrac{D_{px}}{L_{diag}} = \dfrac{2202.9}{24} \approx 91.8 \text{ PPI}
$$

For **photo-quality printing at 300 DPI**, the maximum physical print size in inches is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_{in} &= \dfrac{1920}{300} = 6.4 \text{ in} \\
H_{in} &= \dfrac{1080}{300} = 3.6 \text{ in}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Choosing the Right Data Type

| Content Type | Best Representation | Recommended Bit-Depth | Preferred Compression |
|--------------|--------------------|------------------------|------------------------|
| B/W fax, text scan | 1-bit bitmap | 1 bpp | CCITT Group 3/4, JBIG2 |
| Cartoon, logo | 8-bit indexed | 8 bpp | LZW (GIF), DEFLATE (PNG) |
| Color photograph | 24-bit RGB | 24 bpp | JPEG, WebP, AVIF, JPEG 2000 |
| Medical scan | 16-bit grayscale | 12–16 bpp | JPEG-LS, JPEG 2000 lossless |
| CAD / Engineering | Vector | n/a | SVG, DXF, PDF |
| HDR photograph | 30/48-bit | 30–48 bpp | HDR-specific (Radiance, EXR) |
| Print artwork | CMYK 32-bit | 32 bpp | TIFF (LZW/ZIP), PDF |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level Classification of Image / Graphics Data Types

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Visual Data in Multimedia] --> B[Image Type]
    A --> C[Graphic Type]
    B --> B1[Raster Bitmap]
    B --> B2[Photographic Natural]
    C --> C1[Vector Synthetic]
    C --> C2[Hybrid Compound]
    B1 --> B1a[1-bit Mono]
    B1 --> B1b[4-bit Indexed]
    B1 --> B1c[8-bit Indexed]
    B1 --> B1d[16-bit HighColor]
    B1 --> B1e[24-bit TrueColor]
    B1 --> B1f[30 to 48-bit DeepColor]
    B2 --> B2a[8-bit Grayscale]
    B2 --> B2b[16-bit Grayscale]
    B2 --> B2c[24-bit RGB]
    B2 --> B2d[48-bit RGB]
    C1 --> C1a[2D Vector SVG]
    C1 --> C1b[3D Vector CAD]
    C2 --> C2a[PDF]
    C2 --> C2b[PostScript EPS]
    C2 --> C2c[SWF Flash]
```

## 4.2 Functional Block Diagram: Image Acquisition & Storage Pipeline

```mermaid
graph LR
    P1[Real World Scene] --> P2[Optical Sensor Camera]
    P2 --> P3[Analog Signal]
    P3 --> P4[Sampling]
    P4 --> P5[Quantization]
    P5 --> P6[Pixel Matrix Bitmap]
    P6 --> P7[Color Model RGB or YCbCr]
    P7 --> P8[Compression Engine]
    P8 --> P9[Encoded Bitstream]
    P9 --> P10[Storage or Transmission]
    P6 --> Q1[Resolution W x H]
    P7 --> Q2[Bit Depth b bpp]
    P5 --> Q3[Quantization Levels 2 to power b]
```

## 4.3 Block Architecture: Vector vs Raster Processing

```mermaid
graph TD
    R[Raster Pipeline] --> R1[Input Pixel Matrix]
    R1 --> R2[Transform DCT or DWT]
    R2 --> R3[Quantization]
    R3 --> R4[Entropy Coding Huffman or Arithmetic]
    R4 --> R5[Bitstream]

    V[Vector Pipeline] --> V1[Input Geometric Primitives]
    V1 --> V2[Parser Reads Commands]
    V2 --> V3[Renderer Rasterizes on Demand]
    V3 --> V4[Output Pixels at Any Resolution]

    R5 --> S1[Compressed Image File]
    V4 --> S2[Reconstructed Vector Graphic]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The **raster pipeline** is *lossy by design* (e.g., JPEG), while the **vector pipeline** is *mathematically lossless* because the source representation is never destroyed — only re-rendered on demand.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]** Differentiate between *Raster* and *Vector* graphics with one suitable example for each.

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Raster (Bitmap) | Vector |
|---------|-----------------|--------|
| Representation | Pixel matrix | Geometric primitives (points, lines, curves) |
| Resolution | Fixed, resolution-dependent | Resolution-independent, infinitely scalable |
| File size | Large, grows with resolution | Small, grows with complexity |
| Best suited for | Photographs, scanned images | Logos, fonts, CAD, icons |
| Common formats | JPEG, PNG, BMP, GIF | SVG, EPS, AI, PDF |
| Typical compression | DCT/DWT, LZW, RLE | Text-based, DEFLATE |
| Limitation | Pixelation on zoom | Cannot represent photorealistic detail efficiently |
| Example | A digital camera photo | A company logo in SVG |

*(1 mark for each distinguishing point, 1 mark for example — total 3 marks)*

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** Explain any three color depths used in image representation and state the number of colors each can represent.

**Model Answer:**

- **1-bit (Monochrome / Binary):** Each pixel is either black or white.
  - Number of colors: $2^{1} = 2$ colors.
  - Used in fax machines, B/W document scanning, bitmaps.

- **8-bit (Indexed Color / Grayscale):** Either a 256-entry palette is referenced, or 256 shades of gray are represented.
  - Number of colors: $2^{8} = 256$ shades.
  - Used in GIF, PNG-8, grayscale medical images.

- **24-bit (True Color):** Three independent 8-bit channels for Red, Green, and Blue.
  - Number of colors: $2^{24} = 16{,}777{,}216 \approx 16.7$ million.
  - Used in JPEG, PNG-24, digital photography, web images.

*(1 mark per correct bit-depth, 1 mark total for the color count formula usage — total 3 marks)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — Essay Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### ▶ QUESTION A — *(14 Marks: 7 + 7)*

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, Module 1]** *(a)* Classify the different **graphics/image data types** used in multimedia systems. Explain with neat diagrams the difference between *synthetic graphics* and *natural images*. *(b)* An image of dimension $1024 \times 768$ pixels is captured with 24-bit true color. Calculate (i) the total number of pixels, (ii) the uncompressed file size in KB, (iii) the number of distinct colors that can be represented, and (iv) the file size after applying a JPEG compression of ratio 10:1.

#### (a) Model Solution — Classification & Synthetic vs Natural (7 marks)

**Classification of Image / Graphics Data Types:**

1. **By Source:**
   - *Natural Images* (captured by sensors, photographs, scans).
   - *Synthetic Graphics* (computer-generated, geometric, text).
   - *Hybrid* (mixed content like posters, web pages).

2. **By Representation Model:**
   - *Raster (Bitmap)* — fixed pixel grid.
   - *Vector* — mathematical drawing commands.

3. **By Color Depth:**
   - 1-bit monochrome, 4/8-bit indexed, 16-bit high color, 24-bit true color, 30–48-bit deep color.

**Synthetic vs Natural Images (Diagram):**

```
   SYNTHETIC GRAPHIC                  NATURAL IMAGE
   ┌─────────────────┐                ┌─────────────────┐
   │  ▢ shape A       │                │ ░░▒▒▓▓██▓▓▒▒░░  │
   │    drawn as a    │                │ random pixel     │
   │    circle of r=5 │                │ intensities from │
   │    at (x,y),     │                │ a 24-bit RGB     │
   │    fill = red    │                │ camera sensor     │
   └─────────────────┘                └─────────────────┘
   - low entropy                       - high entropy
   - sharp edges                       - smooth gradients
   - few colors                        - millions of colors
   - lossless coding optimal           - lossy transform coding optimal
```

**Key Differences Table:**

| Property | Synthetic Graphic | Natural Image |
|----------|-------------------|---------------|
| Origin | Mathematically constructed | Optically captured |
| Color count | Low (≤ 256) | Very high (millions) |
| Entropy | Low | High |
| Edges | Perfectly sharp | Soft / noisy |
| Compression | Lossless (LZW, DEFLATE) | Lossy (DCT, DWT) |
| Example | SVG logo, CAD blueprint | Digital photograph |

*(3 marks for classification, 4 marks for the comparison table & explanation — total 7 marks)*

#### (b) Model Solution — Numerical Computation (7 marks)

**Given:** $W = 1024$, $H = 768$, $b = 24$ bpp, $C_r = 10:1$.

**(i) Total Pixels:**

$$
N_{px} = W \times H = 1024 \times 768
$$

$$
1024 \times 768 = 786{,}432 \text{ pixels}
$$

**(ii) Uncompressed File Size in KB:**

$$
S_{bytes} = \dfrac{W \times H \times b}{8} = \dfrac{786{,}432 \times 24}{8}
$$

$$
786{,}432 \times 24 = 18{,}874{,}368 \text{ bits}
$$

$$
18{,}874{,}368 \div 8 = 2{,}359{,}296 \text{ bytes}
$$

$$
2{,}359{,}296 \div 1024 = 2304 \text{ KB} \approx 2.25 \text{ MB}
$$

**(iii) Number of Distinct Colors:**

$$
N_{colors} = 2^{b} = 2^{24} = 16{,}777{,}216 \approx 16.7 \text{ million}
$$

**(iv) Compressed File Size with $C_r = 10:1$:**

$$
S_{compressed} = \dfrac{S_{uncompressed}}{C_r} = \dfrac{2304 \text{ KB}}{10} = 230.4 \text{ KB}
$$

*(1.5 marks each sub-part, with 0.5 marks for unit mention — total 7 marks)*

---

### ▶ QUESTION B — *(14 Marks: 7 + 7)* — *INTERNAL CHOICE*

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, Module 1]** *(a)* With neat sketches, explain the **internal structure of a raster image** including the concept of pixel, scan line, frame buffer, and color depth. How does *bit depth* affect the visual quality and storage requirement? *(b)* A $640 \times 480$ VGA image is stored as 8-bit indexed color. Calculate (i) the palette size in bytes, (ii) the size of pixel data in bytes, (iii) total file size in KB including the palette, and (iv) the total file size if converted to 24-bit true color.

#### (a) Model Solution — Raster Image Internal Structure (7 marks)

**Internal Structure Diagram:**

```
  FRAME BUFFER (Memory Layout)
  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │ Row 0 (Scan Line 0): [P00][P01][P02] ... [P0,W-1]│
  │ Row 1 (Scan Line 1): [P10][P11][P12] ... [P1,W-1]│
  │ ...                                              │
  │ Row H-1:        [PH-1,0] ... [PH-1,W-1]          │
  └──────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
  
  Each Pi,j = b bits stored consecutively
  Total memory = W × H × b bits
```

**Key Terms:**

- **Pixel (Picture Element):** The smallest addressable color sample, located at coordinate $(i, j)$.
- **Scan Line:** A single horizontal row of $W$ pixels. Images are stored scan-line by scan-line in memory (raster order).
- **Frame Buffer:** A contiguous region of RAM/VRAM holding the entire image, indexed row-major.
- **Bit Depth (bpp):** Number of bits used per pixel.

**Effect of Bit Depth:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
b = 1 &: 2 \text{ colors — visible posterization} \\
b = 4 &: 16 \text{ colors — strong banding} \\
b = 8 &: 256 \text{ colors — good for graphics} \\
b = 16 &: 65{,}536 \text{ colors — minor banding} \\
b = 24 &: 16.7\text{M colors — perceptually continuous}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Storage vs Quality Trade-off:** Doubling bit-depth **doubles storage** but reduces *color banding* (visible steps in gradients). Each extra bit exponentially increases the color count.

*(3 marks for diagram & terminology, 2 marks for bit-depth effect, 2 marks for storage/quality trade-off — total 7 marks)*

#### (b) Model Solution — Indexed Color Numerical Computation (7 marks)

**Given:** $W = 640$, $H = 480$, 8-bit indexed color.

**(i) Palette Size in Bytes:**

The 8-bit indexed format uses a $2^{8} = 256$-entry palette, each entry holding a 24-bit RGB color (8 bits per channel):

$$
M_{palette} = 2^{8} \text{ entries} \times 24 \text{ bits} = 256 \times 24 = 6144 \text{ bits}
$$

$$
M_{palette} = \dfrac{6144}{8} = 768 \text{ bytes}
$$

**(ii) Pixel Data Size in Bytes:**

$$
S_{pixels} = \dfrac{W \times H \times b}{8} = \dfrac{640 \times 480 \times 8}{8}
$$

$$
640 \times 480 = 307{,}200 \text{ pixels}
$$

$$
307{,}200 \times 8 = 2{,}457{,}600 \text{ bits} = 307{,}200 \text{ bytes}
$$

**(iii) Total File Size in KB (including palette):**

$$
S_{total} = M_{palette} + S_{pixels} = 768 + 307{,}200 = 307{,}968 \text{ bytes}
$$

$$
S_{total} = \dfrac{307{,}968}{1024} \approx 300.75 \text{ KB}
$$

**(iv) Total File Size if Converted to 24-bit True Color:**

$$
S_{24bit} = \dfrac{W \times H \times 24}{8} = \dfrac{640 \times 480 \times 24}{8}
$$

$$
S_{24bit} = 640 \times 480 \times 3 = 921{,}600 \text{ bytes}
$$

$$
S_{24bit} = \dfrac{921{,}600}{1024} = 900 \text{ KB}
$$

**Storage Comparison:** True color requires $\dfrac{900}{300.75} \approx 2.99\times$ more storage, but offers $2^{24} = 16.7$ million colors versus only 256.

*(1.5 marks each sub-part with units — total 6 marks; 1 mark for comparison & interpretation — total 7 marks)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Forgetting units.** A “size of 2304” with no KB/MB label → ½ to 1 mark lost. Always write `KB`, `MB`, or `bytes`.
> 2. **Mixing bits and bytes.** Dividing by 8 only *once* and leaving the result in bits is a frequent blunder.
> 3. **Using $2^{b}$ with $b = 8$ but writing 256 vs. 65,536 interchangeably.** Match the bit-depth exactly.
> 4. **Not stating assumptions** in essay questions (e.g., “assuming RGB 8 bits per channel”). Examiners deduct when assumptions are unstated.
> 5. **Confusing PPI with DPI.** PPI = screen pixels, DPI = print dots. Use the correct term for the correct context.
> 6. **Skipping the palette overhead** in indexed-color questions. KTU board explicitly tests whether you remember the 768-byte palette.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Graphics/Image Data Types**

- **Two fundamental data types:** *Raster* (pixel grid) and *Vector* (geometric commands).
- **Rasters are resolution-dependent; vectors are resolution-independent.**
- **Bit-depth (bpp)** controls both the number of colors and the file size linearly.
- **Total colors formula:** $N_{colors} = 2^{b}$.
- **Uncompressed file size formula:** $S = \dfrac{W \times H \times b}{8}$ bytes.
- **Standard resolutions to memorize:** VGA (640×480), HD (1280×720), FHD (1920×1080), 4K (3840×2160), 8K (7680×4320).
- **8-bit indexed images carry a 768-byte palette** in addition to pixel data.
- **24-bit true color** = 8 bits R + 8 bits G + 8 bits B = 16.7 million colors.
- **Synthetic graphics** favor *lossless* compression (LZW, DEFLATE) due to low entropy.
- **Natural photographs** favor *lossy transform* compression (DCT in JPEG, DWT in JPEG 2000).
- **Compression ratio** $C_r = \dfrac{S_{original}}{S_{compressed}}$; efficiency $\eta = 1 - \dfrac{1}{C_r}$.
- **PPI** = pixels per inch on screen; **DPI** = dots per inch in print; these are *not* the same concept.
- **Hybrid formats** (PDF, EPS) combine raster, vector, and text in a single container.
- **Medical/scientific images** mandate *lossless* archival (DICOM + JPEG-LS / JPEG 2000 lossless).
- **Always state units** — bits vs bytes vs KB vs MB. Unlabeled answers lose marks.
- **Always simplify step-by-step** in numerical problems; KTU examiners reward visible intermediate steps.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Popular File Formats
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECNT742 - MULTIMEDIA COMPRESSION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Popular File Formats

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Multimedia Compression**, a **File Format** refers to the standardized way in which multimedia data (text, image, audio, video) is encoded, structured, and stored on a digital medium. A file format defines the organization of bits/bytes within a file so that an application (decoder/player) can correctly interpret and reconstruct the original content. 

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PECNT742)** terminology, popular multimedia file formats are broadly classified as:

1. **Image File Formats** – e.g., JPEG, PNG, BMP, GIF, TIFF, WebP
2. **Audio File Formats** – e.g., WAV, MP3, AAC, FLAC, OGG
3. **Video File Formats** – e.g., MP4, AVI, MKV, MOV, FLV, WebM
4. **Container (Wrapper) Formats** – e.g., MKV, MP4, AVI (which hold compressed video/audio streams)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** A **codec** (Coder–Decoder) defines *how* the data is compressed/decompressed, while a **container** defines *how* the compressed data, audio, and metadata are bundled together. Example: An `.mp4` file may contain H.264 (codec) video + AAC (codec) audio inside an MP4 container.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of a multimedia file like a **sealed courier parcel**:
- The **container** is the outer box (the envelope/box structure).
- The **codec** is the language used to encode the message inside.
- The **metadata** is the shipping label (sender, receiver, dimensions).

Just as two different courier companies (DTDC, FedEx) can deliver the same item in differently shaped boxes, two file formats (e.g., `.avi` and `.mp4`) can carry the same video using different packaging rules.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Definition Callout:**
> A *file format* = **Header (signature bytes)** + **Compressed/Encoded Payload** + **Trailer/Metadata**.

## 1.3 Why File Formats Matter in Multimedia Compression

- **Interoperability** – allow cross-platform playback.
- **Compression efficiency** – certain formats favor lossy vs. lossless compression.
- **Streaming readiness** – formats like MP4/H.264 are streaming-optimized.
- **Quality vs. Size trade-off** – the format dictates acceptable distortion.

Standard metrics the student must memorize:
- **Lossless formats**: PNG, BMP, WAV, FLAC
- **Lossy formats**: JPEG, MP3, AAC, MPEG-4 video
- **Bits per pixel (bpp)** and **Bits per second (bps)** are the standard compression quality metrics.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Structural Anatomy of a Multimedia File

Every multimedia file, regardless of type, is composed of three logical layers:

1. **File Header / Signature**
   - Contains magic bytes (e.g., `FF D8 FF` for JPEG, `RIFF` for WAV/AVI).
   - Specifies file version, dimensions, codec, bit-depth, sampling rate.
2. **Data Payload (Compressed Stream)**
   - Holds the actual pixel, audio sample, or video frame data.
   - Encoded using the specific algorithm (JPEG-DCT, MP3-MDCT, H.264, etc.).
3. **Trailer / Metadata Block**
   - Holds EXIF (camera info), XMP, time-stamps, subtitles, chapters.

## 2.2 Classification Logic of Popular File Formats

- **Image Formats**:
  - *JPEG* – DCT-based lossy, ideal for photographs.
  - *PNG* – Lossless, supports transparency (alpha channel).
  - *GIF* – 8-bit indexed color, supports animation.
  - *BMP* – Uncompressed bitmap (rarely used today).
  - *TIFF* – High-quality, used in printing/scanning.
  - *WebP* – Modern lossy/lossless format from Google.

- **Audio Formats**:
  - *WAV* – Uncompressed PCM audio, high fidelity.
  - *MP3* (MPEG-1 Layer III) – Lossy perceptual codec.
  - *AAC* – Advanced Audio Coding (better than MP3 at same bitrate).
  - *FLAC* – Free Lossless Audio Codec.
  - *OGG* – Open container with Vorbis codec.

- **Video Formats**:
  - *MP4* (MPEG-4 Part 14) – Universal container, used in streaming.
  - *AVI* (Audio Video Interleave) – Legacy Microsoft format.
  - *MKV* (Matroska) – Open-source flexible container.
  - *MOV* – Apple QuickTime format.
  - *WebM* – Royalty-free container by Google for VP8/VP9.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Definition | Unit / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| **Compression Ratio (CR)** | $CR = \dfrac{\text{Original Size}}{\text{Compressed Size}}$ | Dimensionless (≥ 1) |
| **Compression Saving (%)** | $S\% = \left(1 - \dfrac{C}{N}\right) \times 100$ | Where $N$=original, $C$=compressed |
| **Bits per Pixel (bpp)** | $bpp = \dfrac{\text{Total Bits}}{\text{Width} \times \text{Height}}$ | Used for image quality |
| **Bits per Second (bps)** | $bps = \dfrac{\text{Sample Size (bits)} \times \text{Sample Rate} \times \text{Channels}}{\text{Compression Ratio}}$ | Used for audio/video |
| **Audio File Size** | $\text{Size} = \dfrac{\text{Bitrate (bps)} \times \text{Duration (s)}}{8}$ | Result in **bytes** |
| **PSNR (Peak Signal-to-Noise Ratio)** | $PSNR = 10 \cdot \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{L^2}{MSE}\right)$ | $L$ = max pixel value (e.g., 255), unit **dB** |
| **Mean Squared Error** | $MSE = \dfrac{1}{MN}\sum_{i=1}^{M}\sum_{j=1}^{N}\!\left[I(i,j) - K(i,j)\right]^2$ | Used in lossy quality evaluation |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Streaming Services** (Netflix, YouTube) → MP4 + H.264/H.265 (HEVC).
- **Broadcast Television** → MPEG-2 transport streams.
- **Medical Imaging** → DICOM (specialized TIFF-based).
- **Digital Forensics** → RAW + metadata preservation.
- **Web Delivery** → WebP, AVIF for efficient bandwidth usage.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Compression Ratio and Storage Savings

Let the original uncompressed multimedia data occupy $N$ bytes, and the compressed version occupy $C$ bytes.

**Step 1** — Define Compression Ratio:

$$CR = \frac{N}{C}$$

**Step 2** — Express the redundant (saved) bytes:

$$\text{Saved Bytes} = N - C$$

**Step 3** — Express as a percentage of the original:

$$S\% = \frac{N - C}{N} \times 100$$

**Step 4** — Substitute $C = N/CR$ (from Step 1 rearranged as $C = N/CR$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
S\% &= \frac{N - \dfrac{N}{CR}}{N} \times 100 \\
&= \left(1 - \frac{1}{CR}\right) \times 100
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5** — Engineering Interpretation:
- $CR = 1 \Rightarrow S\% = 0\%$ (no compression).
- $CR = 10 \Rightarrow S\% = 90\%$ (90 % storage saved).
- $CR = \infty \Rightarrow S\% = 100\%$ (theoretical maximum).

## 3.2 Derivation: Audio File Size from Bitrate

For an audio file compressed with constant bitrate (CBR):

**Step 1** — Define the basic relationship:

$$\text{Bitrate (bps)} = \frac{\text{Total Bits}}{\text{Duration (seconds)}}$$

**Step 2** — Solve for Total Bits:

$$\text{Total Bits} = \text{Bitrate} \times \text{Duration}$$

**Step 3** — Convert bits to bytes:

$$\text{File Size (bytes)} = \frac{\text{Bitrate} \times \text{Duration}}{8}$$

**Step 4** — Convert to megabytes (MB):

$$\text{File Size (MB)} = \frac{\text{Bitrate (kbps)} \times \text{Duration (s)}}{8 \times 1024}$$

**Numerical Illustration (KTU-Style)**:
A 3-minute MP3 song is encoded at **128 kbps**. Calculate the file size.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Size (MB)} &= \frac{128 \,\text{kbps} \times (3 \times 60)\,\text{s}}{8 \times 1024} \\
&= \frac{128 \times 180}{8192} \\
&= \frac{23040}{8192} \\
&\approx 2.81 \text{ MB}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Lossy vs. Lossless Formats

| Property | Lossy (JPEG, MP3, MP4) | Lossless (PNG, FLAC, BMP) |
|---|---|---|
| **Compression Ratio** | High (10:1 to 100:1) | Low (2:1 to 3:1 typical) |
| **Quality after decode** | Approximate (perceptual) | Identical (bit-perfect) |
| **Use Case** | Streaming, web, mobile | Archival, medical, editing |
| **Algorithm Family** | DCT, MDCT, Quantization | Huffman, LZW, RLE, Arithmetic |
| **PSNR Range** | 30–50 dB | ∞ (infinite, mathematically lossless) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Architecture: Generic Multimedia File Structure

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Multimedia File"] --> B["Header Block"]
    A --> C["Metadata Block"]
    A --> D["Compressed Payload"]
    A --> E["Trailer Block"]

    B --> B1["Magic Bytes / Signature"]
    B --> B2["Format Version"]
    B --> B3["Codec Identifier"]

    C --> C1["EXIF / XMP / Tags"]
    C --> C2["Time Stamps"]
    C --> C3["Chapters / Subtitles"]

    D --> D1["Audio Stream"]
    D --> D2["Video Stream"]
    D --> D3["Image / Frame Data"]

    E --> E1["Index Table"]
    E --> E2["Checksum / CRC"]
```

## 4.2 Classification of Popular File Formats

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Root["Popular File Formats"] --> Img["Image Formats"]
    Root --> Aud["Audio Formats"]
    Root --> Vid["Video Formats"]
    Root --> Con["Container Formats"]

    Img --> Img1["JPEG - Lossy DCT"]
    Img --> Img2["PNG - Lossless"]
    Img --> Img3["GIF - Animated"]
    Img --> Img4["BMP - Uncompressed"]
    Img --> Img5["WebP - Modern"]

    Aud --> Aud1["WAV - PCM Uncompressed"]
    Aud --> Aud2["MP3 - Perceptual Lossy"]
    Aud --> Aud3["AAC - Advanced Audio"]
    Aud --> Aud4["FLAC - Lossless"]
    Aud --> Aud5["OGG - Open Container"]

    Vid --> Vid1["MP4 - MPEG-4"]
    Vid --> Vid2["AVI - Legacy MS"]
    Vid --> Vid3["MKV - Matroska"]
    Vid --> Vid4["MOV - QuickTime"]
    Vid --> Vid5["WebM - VP8 VP9"]

    Con --> Con1["MP4 Container"]
    Con --> Con2["MKV Container"]
    Con --> Con3["AVI Container"]
```

## 4.3 Compression vs. Quality Trade-off Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start["Raw Multimedia Data"] --> Choice{"User Requirement?"}
    Choice -->|Faithful reproduction| LL["Lossless Compression"]
    Choice -->|Smallest size acceptable| LS["Lossy Compression"]

    LL --> LL1["PNG / FLAC / BMP / TIFF"]
    LL --> LL2["Algorithm: Huffman, LZW, RLE"]
    LL --> LL3["Output: Identical to original"]

    LS --> LS1["JPEG / MP3 / MP4 / WebP"]
    LS --> LS2["Algorithm: DCT, MDCT, Quantization"]
    LS --> LS3["Output: Perceptually similar"]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between a container format and a codec. Give one example of each.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Codec (Coder–Decoder)** is the algorithm used to compress and decompress the raw media data (e.g., H.264, AAC, MP3). It defines *how* the encoding is done.
- **Container Format** is the wrapper that bundles one or more compressed streams (audio, video, subtitles) along with synchronization metadata (e.g., MP4, MKV, AVI).
- **Example**: H.264 is the codec, while `.mp4` is the container that wraps H.264 video with AAC audio.
- *Award 1 mark each for correct definition of codec, container, and example.*

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**List any three popular image file formats and state whether each is lossy or lossless.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
1. **JPEG** – Lossy (DCT-based compression).
2. **PNG** – Lossless (uses DEFLATE / LZ77).
3. **GIF** – Lossless (uses LZW; 8-bit indexed color).
4. *(Alternatively accepted: BMP — lossless/uncompressed, TIFF — lossless, WebP — supports both).*

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the internal structure of a typical multimedia file with a neat block diagram. List the components stored in each block. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** With the help of formulas, derive the relationship between compression ratio and storage savings. A 5 MB image is compressed to 800 KB. Find the compression ratio and percentage saving. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution

### Part (a) — Internal Structure of a Multimedia File (7 Marks)

A multimedia file is logically divided into **four blocks**:

1. **File Header / Signature Block** (1 Mark)
   - Contains magic bytes that identify the format (e.g., `FF D8 FF` for JPEG, `RIFF` for WAV).
   - Specifies version, codec, dimensions, bit-depth.

2. **Metadata Block** (2 Marks)
   - Holds descriptive data such as EXIF (camera info, GPS), XMP tags, copyright info.
   - For video: subtitles, chapter markers, time-stamps.

3. **Compressed Payload / Data Block** (2 Marks)
   - Contains the actual compressed bitstream (audio samples, image pixels, video frames).
   - Encoded using the chosen algorithm (DCT, MDCT, Huffman, etc.).

4. **Trailer / Index Block** (2 Marks)
   - Provides seek indexes (for random access), CRCs, and end-of-file markers.

> [!NOTE]
> Examiner's Tip: Always draw the diagram first, then label each block with its contents. Marks are awarded separately for diagram and explanation.

---

### Part (b) — Compression Ratio & Storage Savings (7 Marks)

**Derivation (4 Marks):**

Let $N$ = original size, $C$ = compressed size.

$$
\begin{aligned}
CR &= \frac{N}{C} \\[4pt]
S\% &= \frac{N - C}{N} \times 100 = \left(1 - \frac{1}{CR}\right) \times 100
\end{aligned}
$$

**Numerical Calculation (3 Marks):**

Given:
- $N = 5\,\text{MB} = 5120\,\text{KB}$
- $C = 800\,\text{KB}$

$$
\begin{aligned}
CR &= \frac{5120}{800} = 6.4 \\[4pt]
S\% &= \left(1 - \frac{1}{6.4}\right) \times 100 \\
&= (1 - 0.15625) \times 100 \\
&= 84.375\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result**: Compression Ratio = **6.4 : 1**, Storage Saving = **84.375 %**.

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` *(Alternative Choice)*

**(a)** Compare lossy and lossless compression techniques. Provide two examples of file formats for each category and justify when each should be used. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A WAV audio file uses 16-bit samples, a 44.1 kHz sampling rate, and is stereo (2 channels). If the song duration is 4 minutes, calculate: (i) the uncompressed file size in MB, (ii) the file size after MP3 compression at 128 kbps. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution

### Part (a) — Lossy vs. Lossless Compression (7 Marks)

| Aspect | Lossy | Lossless |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** (1 Mark) | Discards perceptually irrelevant data | Preserves all original information |
| **Compression Ratio** (1 Mark) | High (10:1 to 100:1) | Moderate (2:1 to 3:1) |
| **Examples** (2 Marks) | JPEG (image), MP3 (audio), MP4 (video) | PNG (image), FLAC (audio), BMP (image) |
| **Use Cases** (2 Marks) | Web streaming, mobile delivery, broadcasting | Medical imaging, archival, professional editing |
| **Quality Metric** (1 Mark) | PSNR, SSIM, subjective MOS | Bit-exact equality, CRC verification |

---

### Part (b) — Audio File Size Calculations (7 Marks)

**(i) Uncompressed WAV size (3 Marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Bitrate} &= 16 \,\text{bits} \times 44100 \,\text{Hz} \times 2 \,\text{channels} \\
&= 1411200 \,\text{bps} \\[4pt]
\text{Total Bits} &= 1411200 \times (4 \times 60) = 338688000 \,\text{bits} \\[4pt]
\text{Size (MB)} &= \frac{338688000}{8 \times 1024 \times 1024} \\
&= \frac{338688000}{8388608} \\
&\approx 40.37 \,\text{MB}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) MP3 compressed size at 128 kbps (4 Marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Size (MB)} &= \frac{128 \,\text{kbps} \times (4 \times 60)\,\text{s}}{8 \times 1024} \\
&= \frac{128 \times 240}{8192} \\
&= \frac{30720}{8192} \\
&\approx 3.75 \,\text{MB}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Comparison**: Compression reduced the file from **40.37 MB → 3.75 MB**, achieving a compression ratio of approximately **10.77 : 1**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit mismatch** — Confusing kbps (kilobits per second) with KBps (kilobytes per second). Always divide by **8** when converting bits → bytes.
> 2. **MB vs MiB confusion** — Using $10^6$ (decimal MB) instead of $1024 \times 1024$ (binary) loses 1 mark.
> 3. **Container vs Codec blur** — Stating "MP4 is a codec" instead of "MP4 is a container; H.264 is the codec" results in 0 marks for that sub-part.
> 4. **Skipping units in formulas** — Always write bps, Hz, KB, MB explicitly to gain full marks.
> 5. **Missing the diagram in structure-based questions** — Part (a) questions require a *neat labeled block diagram* (1–2 marks reserved solely for the diagram).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ A **file format** is a standardized encoding/storage convention, not the compression algorithm itself.
- ✅ Distinguish **Codec** (compression logic) from **Container** (packaging format).
- ✅ **Magic bytes / signature** in the header uniquely identify file formats (e.g., `FF D8 FF` for JPEG).
- ✅ **Compression Ratio** $CR = N/C$ is the key metric; **Storage Saving** is $(1 - 1/CR) \times 100$.
- ✅ **Lossy formats** (JPEG, MP3, MP4) trade quality for size; **Lossless formats** (PNG, FLAC, BMP) preserve exact data.
- ✅ **PSNR** (in dB) is the standard quality metric for lossy formats; higher PSNR → better quality.
- ✅ **Audio file size** depends on bit depth, sampling rate, channels, and duration.
- ✅ **Modern web formats** (WebP, AVIF, WebM) are optimized for streaming and bandwidth efficiency.
- ✅ **KTU-favorite formats** to remember: JPEG, PNG, MP3, WAV, MP4, AVI, MKV.
- ✅ Always include **units** (KB, MB, kbps, Hz) in every numerical answer to avoid mark deductions.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Concepts of Video - Analog Video and Digital Video (Definitions Only)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECNT742 - MULTIMEDIA COMPRESSION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Concepts of Video - Analog Video and Digital Video (Definitions Only)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Analog Video — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Analog Video** is a method of representing moving visual information as a **continuous electrical signal** that varies in amplitude over time, where the instantaneous voltage level is directly proportional to the brightness (luminance) and color (chrominance) of the corresponding point on the original scene at that moment.

In analog video, the continuous 2-D image signal is converted into a **1-D time-varying waveform** through a process called **raster scanning**. The signal contains three embedded components:

- **Luminance (Y)** — the brightness information.
- **Chrominance (C)** — the color information.
- **Synchronization (Sync) pulses** — timing markers that tell the receiver when to start a new line (horizontal sync) or a new frame (vertical sync).

> [!NOTE]
> Standard analog broadcast systems include **NTSC** (525 lines, 30 fps — used in USA/Japan), **PAL** (625 lines, 25 fps — used in Europe/India), and **SECAM** (625 lines, 25 fps — used in France/Russia).

## 1.2 Digital Video — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Digital Video** is a discrete representation of moving visual information in which each frame of the video is sampled spatially into **pixels** and temporally into a sequence of **frames**, with each pixel's color and brightness encoded as a finite set of binary numbers.

A digital video stream is therefore a **three-dimensional discrete signal** characterized by:

- **Spatial resolution** — number of pixels per frame (e.g., 1920 × 1080).
- **Temporal resolution** — number of frames per second / fps (e.g., 24, 25, 30, 60).
- **Color depth / bit depth** — number of bits used to represent each color sample (e.g., 8-bit, 10-bit, 12-bit).
- **Chroma subsampling format** — e.g., 4:2:0, 4:2:2, 4:4:4.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are recording your friend's birthday party:

- **Analog Video (VHS / old camcorder)** is like painting a portrait on a long continuous scroll. The brush never lifts — every shade of color and motion bleeds smoothly into the next. The scroll can stretch, scratch, or fade, and each copy is slightly worse than the original. There is no concept of "frames" — it is one unbroken, flowing ribbon of light.

- **Digital Video (Smartphone / DSLR)** is like a **flipbook**. The page is split into thousands of tiny squares (pixels), and you capture 30 such pages (frames) every second. Each square stores a precise number. The picture is sharp, can be copied infinitely without loss, but is not a perfectly smooth continuum — it is a **discrete approximation** of reality.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Hook:** *Analog = continuous flow of paint on canvas; Digital = numbered beads on a string.*

## 1.4 Standard Metrics Highlighted

- **Frame Rate (fps)**: Frames per second — typically **24, 25, 30, 50, 60**.
- **Interlaced vs Progressive**: Interlaced transmits odd then even lines (fields); progressive transmits full frames.
- **Aspect Ratio**: Width-to-height ratio (e.g., **4:3**, **16:9**).
- **Color Space**: **YUV / YCbCr** for video, **RGB** for displays.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why Analog Video Exists — The Operational Logic

Analog video was engineered around the constraints of **20th-century broadcast television**:

1. **Bandwidth Efficiency** — A single continuous waveform can be modulated onto an RF carrier and broadcast over the air.
2. **Hardware Compatibility** — Cathode Ray Tube (CRT) displays naturally accept an analog signal — electron beam intensity is a continuous voltage.
3. **Real-time delivery** — No need for storage; the signal is consumed as it arrives.

### 2.1.1 Decomposition of an Analog Video Signal

- **Composite Video**: Combines luminance + chrominance + sync into ONE signal (e.g., RCA yellow connector). Prone to artifacts.
- **Component Video**: Splits into separate channels (Y, Pb, Pr) — higher fidelity.
- **S-Video**: Separates luma (Y) and chroma (C) into two wires — better than composite.

## 2.2 Why Digital Video Exists — The Operational Logic

Digital video solved the **fundamental weaknesses** of analog:

1. **Lossless Copying** — Bits are bits; copying does not degrade quality.
2. **Error Correction** — Forward Error Correction (FEC) codes can detect and repair bit errors.
3. **Compression Friendliness** — Discrete samples can be transformed (DCT, wavelets) and entropy-coded.
4. **Storage & Random Access** — Easy to store, seek, and edit frame-by-frame.

### 2.2.1 Decomposition of a Digital Video Stream

A digital video is fully characterized by five orthogonal parameters:

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Value | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| Horizontal resolution | $W$ | 1920 px | Pixels per line |
| Vertical resolution | $H$ | 1080 px | Lines per frame |
| Frame rate | $f$ | 30 fps | Frames per second |
| Bit depth | $b$ | 8 bits | Bits per color sample |
| Chroma subsampling | — | 4:2:0 | Spatial color sampling pattern |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Parameter | Units | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|
| Uncompressed bit rate of digital video | $R = W \times H \times f \times b \times S$ | **bits per second (bps)** | $S$ = samples per pixel after subsampling |
| Samples per pixel (4:2:0) | $S = 1.5$ | dimensionless | 1 Y + 0.5 Cb + 0.5 Cr |
| Samples per pixel (4:4:4) | $S = 3$ | dimensionless | Full RGB/YUV per pixel |
| Samples per pixel (4:2:2) | $S = 2$ | dimensionless | Broadcast standard |
| Color depth levels | $L = 2^{b}$ | levels | e.g., 256 levels for 8-bit |
| Frame period | $T = \dfrac{1}{f}$ | **seconds** | Time between consecutive frames |
| Aspect Ratio | $\text{AR} = \dfrac{W}{H}$ | ratio | e.g., 16:9 $\Rightarrow$ 1.778 |
| Analog bandwidth (approx.) | $BW \approx \dfrac{W \times H \times f}{2}$ | **Hz** | For interlaced, multiply by ~0.75 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Favorite Equation:** A full HD (1920×1080), 30 fps, 8-bit, 4:2:0 video has an uncompressed bit rate of:
> 
> $R = 1920 \times 1080 \times 30 \times 8 \times 1.5 \approx 746{,}496{,}000 \text{ bps} \approx 746 \text{ Mbps}$

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Broadcasting** — Transition from analog (NTSC/PAL) to digital (DVB, ATSC) freed spectrum for HD and 4K.
- **Telemedicine & Surveillance** — Digital video allows frame-level analysis (motion detection, AI tagging).
- **Streaming** — Netflix, YouTube rely on digital video + compression (H.264, H.265, AV1).
- **Medical Imaging** — DICOM standard for digital video in endoscopy, ultrasound, MRI.
- **Cinema** — Digital Cinema Initiatives (DCI) standardize 2K/4K digital projection globally.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix & Key Derivations

> [!NOTE]
> This module's syllabus scope is **Definitions Only**. The following analytical derivations establish the *quantitative relationships* students must know to define the terms precisely in an exam answer. No step is skipped.

## 3.1 Derivation — Uncompressed Digital Video Bit Rate

The bit rate $R$ of an uncompressed digital video stream is the product of five independent quantities.

### Step 1 — Count of Pixels per Frame

Each frame is a 2-D grid of width $W$ and height $H$.

$$N_{\text{pixels}} = W \times H$$

**Logic:** Every pixel is one unique spatial sample point. For a Full HD frame, $1920 \times 1080 = 2{,}073{,}600$ pixels.

### Step 2 — Bits per Pixel

Each pixel contains one luma sample $Y$ and two chroma samples (Cb, Cr). With subsampling $S$:

$$N_{\text{bits/px}} = b \times S$$

**Logic:** $b$ is the bit depth; $S$ counts the *effective* number of color samples per pixel (3 for 4:4:4, 2 for 4:2:2, 1.5 for 4:2:0).

### Step 3 — Bits per Frame

Multiply the bits per pixel by the number of pixels:

$$B_{\text{frame}} = W \times H \times b \times S$$

**Logic:** Total information content of one still image.

### Step 4 — Bits per Second (Bit Rate)

Frames repeat at rate $f$ frames per second:

$$
\begin{aligned}
R &= B_{\text{frame}} \times f \\
  &= W \times H \times f \times b \times S
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** Bit rate = information per frame × number of frames per second.

### Step 5 — Worked Numerical Example (Standard KTU Style)

For **Standard Definition PAL** ($W=720$, $H=576$, $f=25$, $b=8$, $S=1.5$ for 4:2:0):

$$
\begin{aligned}
R &= 720 \times 576 \times 25 \times 8 \times 1.5 \\
  &= 720 \times 576 = 414{,}720 \text{ pixel-frames/s} \\
  &\times 25 = 10{,}368{,}000 \text{ pixel-samples/s} \\
  &\times 8 = 82{,}944{,}000 \text{ bits/s} \\
  &\times 1.5 = 124{,}416{,}000 \text{ bits/s} \\
  R &\approx 124.4 \text{ Mbps}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **For KTU 14-mark answers**, present the general formula first, then substitute numerical values in a clean aligned block, then state the final answer with units.

## 3.2 Derivation — Analog Video Bandwidth Estimate

For an analog video signal scanned at resolution $W \times H$ at $f$ fps, the **Nyquist sampling theorem** tells us the minimum sampling frequency needed to digitize it:

$$f_{s,\min} = 2 \times W \times H \times f$$

Since sampling frequency corresponds to the analog baseband bandwidth:

$$BW_{\min} \approx W \times H \times f$$

**Logic:** Each frame contributes $W \times H$ unique samples; sampling twice per cycle is Nyquist's rule.

For **interlaced** analog video (two fields per frame), the effective line rate is half, so the bandwidth is approximately:

$$BW_{\text{interlaced}} \approx \frac{W \times H \times f}{2}$$

## 3.3 Conceptual Comparison Matrix (Definitional Distinctions)

| Dimension | Analog Video | Digital Video |
|---|---|---|
| Signal nature | Continuous in time **and** amplitude | Discrete in time **and** amplitude |
| Representation | Voltage waveform | Binary number sequence |
| Sampling | Not sampled inherently | Spatially + temporally sampled |
| Storage | Magnetic tape (VHS, Betamax) | Digital file (MP4, MKV, AVI) |
| Quality over copies | Degrades each generation | Identical up to bit-exact |
| Editing | Hard — physical cutting | Easy — frame-accurate software |
| Bandwidth need | High (continuous spectrum) | Lower (compressed) |
| Standards | NTSC, PAL, SECAM | CIF, QCIF, HD, 4K, 8K |
| Error sensitivity | Gradual degradation | Catastrophic if uncorrected |
| Sync mechanism | Embedded sync pulses | Frame/sequence headers |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
