# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]** Define Analog Video. Mention any two analog broadcast standards with their line counts and frame rates.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Analog video is a continuous time-varying electrical signal in which the voltage at any instant is proportional to the brightness and color of the corresponding point of the scene being captured. It is generated by raster scanning and contains luminance, chrominance, and synchronization information within a single waveform.

Two analog broadcast standards:

- **NTSC**: 525 lines per frame, **30 fps**, used in USA and Japan.
- **PAL**: 625 lines per frame, **25 fps**, used in Europe, India, and Australia.

*(1 mark definition + 1 mark standards list + 1 mark for line/fps accuracy = 3 marks)*

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]** Define Digital Video. List the four parameters that fully characterize a digital video stream.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Digital video is the discrete representation of moving visual information in which each frame is sampled spatially into pixels and temporally into a sequence of frames, with each sample encoded as a finite set of binary numbers.

Four characterizing parameters:

1. **Spatial resolution** ($W \times H$) — pixels per frame.
2. **Frame rate** ($f$) — frames per second.
3. **Bit depth** ($b$) — bits per color sample.
4. **Chroma subsampling** — e.g., 4:2:0, 4:2:2, 4:4:4.

*(1 mark definition + 1.5 marks parameters + 0.5 mark examples = 3 marks)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — Model 2024]*

**(a)** Define Analog Video in detail. With the help of a block diagram, explain the constituents of an analog video signal. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Define Digital Video. A 4K UHD digital video stream has parameters: $W = 3840$, $H = 2160$, $f = 60$ fps, $b = 10$ bits, chroma subsampling = 4:2:2. Calculate the uncompressed bit rate in Mbps. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — (a) **7 marks**

**Definition (2 marks):** Analog video is a continuous electrical signal representation of visual information, in which the instantaneous voltage level is proportional to the luminance and chrominance of the original scene. The signal is generated through raster scanning of the image, line by line, top to bottom, repeated at a fixed frame rate.

**Constituents of an analog video signal (5 marks):**

1. **Luminance (Y) component** — carries brightness information; computed as $Y = 0.299R + 0.587G + 0.114B$ for NTSC.
2. **Chrominance (C) component** — carries color information; transmitted as I/Q (NTSC) or U/V (PAL) modulating a subcarrier.
3. **Horizontal sync pulse** — signals the start of a new line; typically 4–5 µs wide.
4. **Vertical sync pulse** — signals the start of a new frame; occupies several horizontal line periods.
5. **Blanking intervals** — Horizontal and vertical blanking periods during which the beam is turned off so it can retrace.
6. **Equalizing pulses** — used in interlaced systems to maintain interlace alignment.

The composite waveform interleaves all the above into a single baseband signal that can be modulated onto an RF carrier for broadcast.

---

#### Model Solution — (b) **7 marks**

**Definition (2 marks):** Digital video is a discrete, sampled, and quantized representation of a moving visual scene, in which each frame is divided into a finite grid of pixels and each pixel's color values are represented by binary numbers.

**Numerical calculation (5 marks):**

Given: $W = 3840$, $H = 2160$, $f = 60$ fps, $b = 10$ bits, subsampling = 4:2:2 $\Rightarrow S = 2$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
R &= W \times H \times f \times b \times S \\
  &= 3840 \times 2160 \times 60 \times 10 \times 2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 1:** $3840 \times 2160 = 8{,}294{,}400$ pixels per frame.

**Step 2:** $\times 60 = 497{,}664{,}000$ pixel-frames per second.

**Step 3:** $\times 10 = 4{,}976{,}640{,}000$ bits per second.

**Step 4:** $\times 2 = 9{,}953{,}280{,}000$ bits per second.

**Final answer (3 marks for substitution and arithmetic):**

$$
R = 9{,}953{,}280{,}000 \text{ bps} \approx 9.95 \text{ Gbps}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert — Question A(b):**
> - Students frequently forget the **subsampling factor** $S$ and report the answer as ~5 Gbps (losing 2 marks).
> - Some students omit unit conversion and write "9953 Mbps" instead of "9.95 Gbps" (losing 1 mark).
> - Showing the **aligned substitution step** is mandatory — examiners award partial credit only if intermediate work is visible.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[Alternative choice]*

**(a)** Compare and contrast Analog Video and Digital Video along any seven dimensions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the concept of chroma subsampling in digital video. Differentiate between 4:4:4, 4:2:2, and 4:2:0 with neat sketches of sample grids. Calculate the uncompressed bit rate of a 1080p (1920×1080), 30 fps, 8-bit video under each subsampling scheme. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — (a) **7 marks**

**Comparison table (1 mark per valid pair, 7 marks):**

| Dimension | Analog Video | Digital Video |
|---|---|---|
| Signal nature | Continuous | Discrete |
| Storage medium | Magnetic tape | Digital file (HDD, SSD, cloud) |
| Quality degradation | Yes, with each copy/generation | No, bit-exact reproduction |
| Editing difficulty | Hard — physical cuts | Easy — software frame access |
| Bandwidth usage | Higher (continuous spectrum) | Lower (after compression) |
| Error handling | Graceful degradation | Catastrophic unless ECC applied |
| Equipment cost | Cheaper legacy hardware | Higher initial, lower long-term |
| Standards examples | NTSC, PAL, SECAM | CIF, HD, UHD, 4K, 8K |

**Conclusion (built into the table presentation):** Digital video is the modern choice for nearly all applications due to its compression friendliness, editability, and lossless reproduction, although analog still survives in niche legacy and broadcast contexts.

---

#### Model Solution — (b) **7 marks**

**Concept of Chroma Subsampling (2 marks):**
The human visual system is more sensitive to brightness (luma) than to color (chroma). Chroma subsampling exploits this by storing chroma samples at a lower spatial resolution than luma samples. The notation **$A:B:C$** describes the relative sampling rates of luma and chroma in a 2×2 block of pixels:

- $A$ = luma samples in the reference width (always 4).
- $B$ = chroma samples in the first row.
- $C$ = chroma samples in the second row.

**Three subsampling schemes (2 marks):**

- **4:4:4** — Full chroma resolution. Every pixel has its own Cb and Cr samples. $S = 3$.
- **4:2:2** — Half horizontal chroma resolution. $S = 2$.
- **4:2:0** — Quarter chroma resolution (half horizontal, half vertical). $S = 1.5$.

**Sample grid sketches (1 mark) — described textually:**

- 4:4:4: every cell has Y, Cb, Cr → "Y Cb Cr" repeated.
- 4:2:2: Cb and Cr shared between two horizontal neighbors.
- 4:2:0: Cb and Cr shared between a 2×2 block.

**Numerical calculations (2 marks):**

For $W = 1920$, $H = 1080$, $f = 30$, $b = 8$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{4:4:4} &= 1920 \times 1080 \times 30 \times 8 \times 3 = 1{,}492{,}992{,}000 \text{ bps} \approx 1.49 \text{ Gbps} \\
R_{4:2:2} &= 1920 \times 1080 \times 30 \times 8 \times 2 = 995{,}328{,}000 \text{ bps} \approx 995.3 \text{ Mbps} \\
R_{4:2:0} &= 1920 \times 1080 \times 30 \times 8 \times 1.5 = 746{,}496{,}000 \text{ bps} \approx 746.5 \text{ Mbps}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert — Question B(b):**
> - **Do NOT** confuse the subsampling notation with the number of bits. $4:2:0$ does NOT mean 4+2+0 = 6 samples — it is a positional ratio describing spatial sampling.
> - **Do NOT** use $S = 1$ for 4:2:0. The correct effective samples-per-pixel factor is **1.5** (one full luma + half a Cb + half a Cr per pixel, on average).
> - Always present results in **Mbps or Gbps** with the correct prefix — examiners deduct 0.5–1 mark for raw bps in a 14-mark question.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU PECNT742 Module 1 (Analog & Digital Video Definitions):**

- **Analog Video** = continuous, time-varying voltage signal carrying luminance + chrominance + sync.
- **Digital Video** = discrete sampled/quantized sequence of frames, each frame a 2-D pixel grid.
- **Three classical analog standards**: NTSC (525/30), PAL (625/25), SECAM (625/25).
- **Five digital video parameters**: $W$, $H$, $f$, $b$, subsampling $S$.
- **Master Formula**: $R = W \times H \times f \times b \times S$ (in bps).
- **Subsampling multipliers** (memorize!): 4:4:4 → $S = 3$, 4:2:2 → $S = 2$, 4:2:0 → $S = 1.5$.
- **Color depth levels**: $L = 2^{b}$ (e.g., 8-bit → 256 levels).
- **Frame period**: $T = 1/f$.
- **Interlaced vs Progressive**: interlaced uses 2 fields/frame; progressive uses 1 full frame.
- **Aspect Ratio**: $W/H$ (16:9 = 1.778).
- **Analog → Digital conversion pipeline**: Capture → Sample → Quantize → Color-convert → Encode → Compress → Store/Stream.
- **Why digital dominates**: lossless copying, easy editing, compression friendliness, error correction, random access.
- **Why analog still exists**: legacy broadcast infrastructure, certain scientific/medical instruments, niche artistic uses.
- **Common exam mistake**: forgetting the subsampling factor $S$ in the bit rate formula (loses 2 marks).
- **Common exam mistake**: writing "bits per second" without converting to Mbps or Gbps when the question expects SI prefixes.
- **Key takeaway line for any definition answer**: "Digital video is a **discrete**, **sampled**, and **quantized** representation of a moving visual scene."

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Digital Audio - Digitization of Sound
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECNT742 - MULTIMEDIA COMPRESSION > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Digital Audio - Digitization of Sound ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) **Digital Audio** is the technical representation of an acoustic sound wave as a sequence of discrete numerical samples, produced by the process of **Digitization of Sound** (also called Analog-to-Digital Conversion or ADC). The continuous-time, continuous-amplitude audio signal is converted into a discrete-time, discrete-amplitude binary stream that can be stored, transmitted, processed, and reproduced by digital systems. The digitization process consists of three fundamental sub-processes executed in sequence: 1. **Sampling** — Discretization of the time axis (continuous-time → discrete-time). 2. **Quantization** — Discretization of the amplitude axis (continuous-amplitude → discrete-amplitude). 3. **Encoding (Pulse Code Modulation, PCM)** — Conversion of quantized levels into binary bit-strings. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The term *Digitization of Sound* is a unified, indivisible concept in the KTU syllabus. Examiners expect students to address all three sub-processes (sampling, quantization, encoding) together and explain the inter-relationship between them. A fragmented answer addressing only sampling or only quantization will attract heavy mark deductions. > [!NOTE] > **Standard Reference Metrics Used Globally:** > - Human Audible Frequency Range: **20 Hz – 20 kHz** > - Standard CD-Quality Sampling Frequency: **44.1 kHz** (set by Sony/Philips Red Book standard, 1980) > - Standard DVD/Studio Sampling Frequency: **48 kHz** (professional audio/video standard) > - Telephone Quality Sampling Frequency: **8 kHz** (ITU-T G.711 standard) --- ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition Imagine you are sitting on a sea-cliff in Kerala, watching the ocean waves roll in. The actual ocean is a **continuous phenomenon** — every microsecond the water surface has a new height, and the wave has a continuous shape. Now, suppose a film director wants to "capture" this ocean in a movie. The director's camera is set to take **one photograph every second**. The still photographs (samples), when played back rapidly at 24 frames per second, recreate the *illusion* of a continuous, flowing ocean to the human eye. This is exactly how digital audio works: | Ocean Analogy | Digital Audio Equivalent | Role | |---|---|---| | Continuous ocean surface | Continuous sound pressure wave (analog) | **Source signal** | | One photo per second | Sampling at *fs* samples/second | **Sampling** | | Limited shutter colors (8-bit, 16-bit color depth) | Limited amplitude levels (8-bit, 16-bit) | **Quantization** | | Photographs stored as JPEG/PNG files | Binary PCM codes | **Encoding** | | Playing back film at 24 fps | D/A converter → Speaker vibration | **Reconstruction** | **Key Takeaway:** A digital audio file is *not* the actual sound — it is a **highly faithful, discrete numerical *representation*** of the sound, sufficient to fool the human ear into perceiving continuous audio upon playback. > [!TIP] > **Why digitize at all?** Analog audio (vinyl records, magnetic tapes) degrades with every copy, picks up noise, and cannot be processed by computers. Digital audio is **noise-immune, perfectly copyable, compressible, and streamable** — which is why music, telephony, broadcasting, and cinema all migrated to digital standards. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Digitization Pipeline — Step-by-Step Operational Logic The complete digitization of sound is a **strictly sequential, loss-tolerant pipeline**. Each stage introduces a specific type of error, and the entire quality of the final digital file is dictated by how each stage is parameterized. --- #### **Stage 1 — Anti-Aliasing (Pre-Filtering)** * **Why this stage?** Real-world audio signals contain frequencies far above the human hearing range (ultrasonics, RF interference, instrument harmonics above 20 kHz). If these are allowed to enter the sampler, they will *fold back* into the audible band as **aliasing distortion** — an irreversible artifact. * **How it works:** A **low-pass analog filter** (the *anti-aliasing filter*) with cutoff frequency *fc* = *fs* / 2 is placed *before* the sampler. It strips out all energy above half the sampling rate. * **Engineering Trade-off:** A sharp (steep) analog filter has a long impulse response and introduces phase distortion; a gentle filter leaks more aliasing. Professional ADC chips use **oversampling + digital filtering** to circumvent this. --- #### **Stage 2 — Sampling (Time Discretization)** * **What happens:** A switch (the sample-and-hold circuit) closes briefly at regular intervals *Ts* = 1 / *fs*, capturing the instantaneous amplitude of the analog waveform. * **Nyquist-Shannon Sampling Theorem (the cornerstone of all digital signal processing):** > A band-limited signal with maximum frequency component *fmax* can be **perfectly reconstructed** from its samples **if and only if** the sampling frequency satisfies *fs* ≥ 2 × *fmax*. * **Nyquist Rate** = 2 × *fmax* (the minimum theoretical rate to avoid aliasing). * **Nyquist Frequency** = *fs* / 2 (the highest frequency that can be unambiguously represented). --- #### **Stage 3 — Quantization (Amplitude Discretization)** * **What happens:** Each captured sample amplitude (a real-valued voltage) is rounded to the *nearest* of *L* pre-defined discrete amplitude levels. * **Quantization Step (Resolution):** *Δ* = (Vmax − Vmin) / *L* * **Number of Levels:** *L* = 2*n*, where *n* = bits per sample. * **Quantization Error:** *e* = *xq* − *x* (quantized value − true value), bounded in the interval [−*Δ*/2, +*Δ*/2]. * **Quantization Noise Power (uniform mid-tread quantizer):** *σq2* = *Δ*2 / 12 > [!IMPORTANT] > **Quantization is an IRREVERSIBLE, LOSSY operation.** Once an amplitude is rounded to the nearest level, the original value is mathematically lost. Unlike sampling (which can be lossless with sufficient *fs*), quantization always introduces **quantization noise**, and this is the primary limit on the dynamic range of a digital recording. --- #### **Stage 4 — Encoding (Binary Pulse Code Modulation)** * **What happens:** Each quantized level index (0 to *L*−1) is converted into an *n*-bit binary word. The output is a bitstream of **0s and 1s** — the actual digital audio data. * **Pulse Code Modulation (PCM)** is the canonical encoding scheme. Variants include **Linear PCM (LPCM)**, **Differential PCM (DPCM)**, **Adaptive DPCM (ADPCM)** — all are lossless once quantization is fixed. * **The bitstream is wrapped in a container** (WAV, AIFF, FLAC for uncompressed/lossless; MP3, AAC, OGG for lossy compressed). --- #### **Stage 5 — Reconstruction (D/A Conversion at Playback)** * Each PCM word is converted back to a voltage level. * A **reconstruction (low-pass) filter** smooths the staircase waveform into a continuous analog signal. * This analog signal drives the loudspeaker cone, which vibrates air to recreate the original sound. --- ### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes / Typical Values | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Sampling Period | *Ts* = 1 / *fs* | seconds | Time between two samples | | 2 | Nyquist Criterion | *fs* ≥ 2 *fmax* | Hz | Minimum for no aliasing | | 3 | Nyquist Frequency | *fN* = *fs* / 2 | Hz | Highest representable frequency | | 4 | Quantization Levels | *L* = 2*n* | dimensionless | *n* = bits per sample | | 5 | Quantization Step | *Δ* = (Vmax − Vmin) / *L* | Volts | Resolution of amplitude | | 6 | Quantization Noise Power | *σq2* = *Δ*2 / 12 | W (power) | Uniform distribution assumed | | 7 | **Signal-to-Quantization-Noise Ratio (SQNR)** | **SQNR = 6.02 *n* + 1.76** | **dB** | **★ Highest-priority KTU formula** | | 8 | Bit Rate (Mono) | *R* = *fs* × *n* | bits/second (bps) | Single-channel | | 9 | Bit Rate (Stereo) | *R* = *fs* × *n* × 2 | bps | Two channels | | 10 | Audio File Size | *S* = *fs* × *n* × *c* × *t* | bits | *c* = channels, *t* = duration in seconds | | 11 | SNR for *m*-bit ADC | SNRdB ≈ 6.02 *m* | dB | Approximation (high-bit case) | | 12 | Alias Frequency | *falias* = ∣ *f* − *k fs* ∣ | Hz | Lowest non-negative result | > [!WARNING] > **Pipe-Symbol Safety Note:** All absolute-value bars above (e.g., *falias* = |*f* − *k fs*|) are rendered using `\vert` for HTML compatibility. **In your exam scripts, write them as actual vertical bars or use the notation `mod(...)`** to avoid formatting issues. --- ### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility * **Telephony (8 kHz, 8-bit μ-law/A-law PCM):** Designed around the Nyquist rate of human speech (~4 kHz); produces a 64 kbps stream — the building block of the PSTN (Public Switched Telephone Network). * **CD Audio (44.1 kHz, 16-bit, Stereo):** Bit rate = 1,411.2 kbps; chosen to cover up to 22.05 kHz (above 20 kHz human limit), and 44.1 kHz is mathematically compatible with both NTSC (29.97 fps video) and PAL (25 fps video) frame rates. * **Studio / DVD / Blu-ray (48 kHz, 16/24-bit):** The professional standard; 48 kHz was chosen to be a multiple of standard video frame rates, simplifying A/V synchronization. * **Hi-Res Audio (96 kHz / 192 kHz, 24-bit):** Used in audiophile and mastering workflows; provides headroom for digital filtering without audible aliasing artifacts. * **Voice-over-IP (VoIP), Music Streaming (Spotify, Apple Music):** Built on lossy compression codecs (Opus, AAC, MP3) that exploit psychoacoustic models to reduce bit rate from 1.4 Mbps (CD) to as low as 32–320 kbps. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Quantization Step (Resolution) *Δ* We start with a continuous analog input voltage *x*(*t*) bounded in the range [*V*min, *V*max]. A uniform *n*-bit quantizer divides this range into *L* = 2*n* equally spaced intervals. The width of each quantization interval (the *step size* or *resolution*) is: $$ \begin{aligned} \Delta &= \frac{\text{Full-scale voltage range}}{\text{Number of discrete levels}} \\[4pt] \Delta &= \frac{V_{\max} - V_{\min}}{L} \\[4pt] \Delta &= \frac{V_{\max} - V_{\min}}{2^{n}} \end{aligned} $$ **Logic step (line 1→2):** The total analog range is *V*max − *V*min; the quantizer must partition this into *L* equal sub-intervals. **Logic step (line 2→3):** Substitute *L* = 2*n* for an *n*-bit quantizer. **Numerical Example (KTU-style):** For a 16-bit ADC with full-scale range of 10 V: $$ \begin{aligned} \Delta &= \frac{10 - (-10)}{2^{16}} = \frac{20}{65536} \\[4pt] \Delta &= 3.05176 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{V} \approx 0.305\ \text{mV} \end{aligned} $$ This is the *smallest voltage difference the ADC can resolve* — a crucial specification in instrumentation. --- ### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Quantization Noise Power *σq2* Assume the quantization error *e* is uniformly distributed in [−*Δ*/2, +*Δ*/2] (a standard assumption for high-resolution quantizers with input signals that exercise all levels). The **probability density function** of *e* is: $$ p(e) = \begin{cases} \dfrac{1}{\Delta}, & -\dfrac{\Delta}{2} \leq e \leq +\dfrac{\Delta}{2} \\[6pt] 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases} $$ The mean-square value of *e* (which equals its variance since the mean is zero) is: $$ \begin{aligned} \sigma_{q}^{2} &= E[e^{2}] = \int_{-\Delta/2}^{+\Delta/2} e^{2}\, p(e)\, de \\[4pt] &= \int_{-\Delta/2}^{+\Delta/2} e^{2}\, \frac{1}{\Delta}\, de \\[4pt] &= \frac{1}{\Delta} \left[ \frac{e^{3}}{3} \right]_{-\Delta/2}^{+\Delta/2} \\[4pt] &= \frac{1}{3\Delta} \left[ \left(\frac{\Delta}{2}\right)^{3} - \left(-\frac{\Delta}{2}\right)^{3} \right] \\[4pt] &= \frac{1}{3\Delta} \cdot 2 \cdot \frac{\Delta^{3}}{8} \\[4pt] &= \frac{\Delta^{2}}{12} \end{aligned} $$ **Result:** *σq2* = *Δ*2 / 12 (watts, assuming 1 Ω normalization). **Logic step (1→2):** Definition of mean-square (expected value) for a continuous random variable. **Logic step (2→3):** Insert the uniform PDF *p*(*e*) = 1/*Δ*. **Logic step (3→4):** Evaluate the antiderivative *e*3/3. **Logic step (4→5):** Apply limits −*Δ*/2 and +*Δ*/2. **Logic step (5→6):** Note that the cubic is an odd function, so the difference is *2* × (*Δ*/2)3. **Logic step (6→7):** Simplify the constants. --- ### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Signal-to-Quantization-Noise Ratio (SQNR) The **signal power** for a full-scale sinusoidal input of amplitude *V*pp/2 (where *V*pp is peak-to-peak = *L* × *Δ*) is: $$ \begin{aligned} P_{\text{signal}} &= \frac{V_{\text{rms}}^{2}}{R} = \frac{1}{R} \left( \frac{V_{\text{pp}}}{2\sqrt{2}} \right)^{2} = \frac{V_{\text{pp}}^{2}}{8R} \end{aligned} $$ Substituting *V*pp = 2*n* · *Δ*: $$ P_{\text{signal}} = \frac{(2^{n}\, \Delta)^{2}}{8R} = \frac{2^{2n}\, \Delta^{2}}{8R} $$ The quantization noise power (assuming 1 Ω load, i.e., *R* = 1): $$ P_{\text{noise}} = \frac{\Delta^{2}}{12} $$ The **SQNR in linear scale** is the ratio: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{SQNR}_{\text{linear}} &= \frac{P_{\text{signal}}}{P_{\text{noise}}} = \frac{2^{2n}\, \Delta^{2}/8}{\Delta^{2}/12} = \frac{2^{2n} \cdot 12}{8} = \frac{3}{2} \cdot 2^{2n} \end{aligned} $$ Converting to **decibels** using SQNRdB = 10 log10(SQNRlinear): $$ \begin{aligned} \text{SQNR}_{\text{dB}} &= 10 \log_{10}\!\left( \frac{3}{2} \cdot 2^{2n} \right) \\[4pt] &= 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{3}{2}\right) + 10 \log_{10}\!\left(2^{2n}\right) \\[4pt] &= 10 \cdot (0.1761) + 2n \cdot 10 \log_{10}(2) \\[4pt] &= 1.7609 + 2n \cdot (3.0103) \\[4pt] &\approx 1.76 + 6.02\, n \end{aligned} $$ **Final Result (the single most important KTU formula):** $$ \boxed{\ \text{SQNR (dB)} \approx 6.02\, n + 1.76\ } $$ **Logic step (1→2):** Decompose the logarithm of a product. **Logic step (2→3):** 10 log10(3/2) ≈ 1.76 dB; 10 log10(2) ≈ 3.01 dB. **Logic step (3→4):** Multiply 2 × 3.01 = 6.02; this is the "6 dB per bit" rule of thumb (every additional bit adds ~6 dB of dynamic range). > [!IMPORTANT] > **The 6 dB-per-bit rule** is the most-asked derivative in KTU exams. Memorize SQNR = 6.02*n* + 1.76 dB. For 16-bit CD audio, SQNR ≈ 98.08 dB; for 24-bit studio audio, SQNR ≈ 146.24 dB (beyond the threshold of human hearing at ~120 dB SPL). --- ### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Audio Bit Rate and File Size for a Recording For a recording with sampling frequency *fs*, *n* bits per sample, *c* channels, and duration *t* seconds: $$ \begin{aligned} R\ (\text{bps}) &= f_{s} \times n \times c \\[4pt] S\ (\text{bits}) &= f_{s} \times n \times c \times t \end{aligned} $$ **Worked Example — CD-Quality Stereo, 4-minute song:** $$ \begin{aligned} f_{s} &= 44{,}100\ \text{Hz} \\ n &= 16\ \text{bits/sample} \\ c &= 2\ (\text{stereo}) \\ t &= 4 \times 60 = 240\ \text{seconds} \end{aligned} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} S &= 44100 \times 16 \times 2 \times 240 \\[4pt] &= 44100 \times 7680 \\[4pt] &= 338{,}688{,}000\ \text{bits} \\[4pt] &= \frac{338{,}688{,}000}{8}\ \text{bytes} \\[4pt] &= 42{,}336{,}000\ \text{bytes} \approx 40.37\ \text{MiB} \end{aligned} $$ This matches the real-world file size of a 4-minute CD-quality WAV track. --- ### 3.5 Derivation 5 — Aliasing Frequency Calculation If a signal contains a frequency component *f* that violates the Nyquist criterion (i.e., *f* > *fs*/2), the sampled signal will appear to contain an *alias* frequency: $$ f_{\text{alias}} = \left\vert f - k\, f_{s} \right\vert $$ where *k* is chosen as the integer that yields the smallest non-negative result. **Example:** Sample at *fs* = 8 kHz (telephony). A real input frequency *f* = 9 kHz is present. $$ \begin{aligned} f_{\text{alias}} &= \vert 9000 - 1 \cdot 8000 \vert = 1000\ \text{Hz} \end{aligned} $$ A 9 kHz tone (above the audible 4 kHz telephone band) reappears as a 1 kHz tone (audible) — this is **aliasing distortion**, and the anti-aliasing filter is precisely what prevents it. > [!WARNING] > **Aliasing is irreversible.** Once high-frequency content folds into the baseband during sampling, no downstream filter can remove it. Prevention at the anti-aliasing stage is the *only* solution. --- ### 3.6 Analytical Summary Matrix (Quality vs. Bit Depth) | Bit Depth *n* | SQNR (dB) | Approx. Dynamic Range | Typical Use Case | |---|---|---|---| | 8-bit | 49.9 dB | 6× | Legacy games, low-quality speech | | 12-bit | 74.0 dB | ~5000× | Telephony (A-law companded) | | 16-bit | 98.1 dB | ~80,000× | CD Audio, consumer music | | 20-bit | 122.2 dB | ~1.3 M× | DVD-Audio | | 24-bit | 146.2 dB | ~20 M× | Studio recording, mastering | > [!TIP] > **Every additional bit doubles the quantization resolution *Δ* precision** and adds **6.02 dB** of dynamic range. The human ear's threshold of hearing-to-pain spans ~120 dB, so 20-bit (122 dB) is theoretically more than sufficient — but engineers use 24-bit for *headroom* (to safely capture transients without clipping). ## 4. For traditional signal-shape illustrations (sinusoids with sample points, staircase waveforms), please refer to the standard textbook figures for "Pulse Code Modulation" — such time-domain plots cannot be accurately rendered by Mermaid. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap > **Exam Blueprint Reference:** As per KTU 2024 Scheme regulations, the End-Semester Examination (ESE) for PECNT742 follows a 3-mark Part A (10 questions, answer any 5) and a 14-mark Part B pattern (Module-level internal choice, typically one full 14-mark question from Module 1). The questions below are modeled on actual KTU valuation patterns. --- ### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) #### **Question A1** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024] **State and explain the Nyquist-Shannon sampling theorem. Why is it called the cornerstone of digital signal processing?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The Nyquist-Shannon sampling theorem states that **a band-limited signal with maximum frequency component *f*max can be perfectly reconstructed from its discrete samples if and only if the sampling frequency *f*s ≥ 2 *f*max**. The minimum sampling rate *f*s = 2 *f*max is called the **Nyquist rate**, and the maximum reconstructable frequency *f*s/2 is the **Nyquist frequency**. It is the cornerstone of digital signal processing because it provides the **theoretical foundation** that allows any analog signal (audio, video, biomedical) to be represented digitally without loss of information — provided the rate is met. Failure to satisfy the criterion causes **aliasing**, an irreversible distortion. **(3 marks)** --- #### **Question A2** [KTU University Exam – May 2024] **Define quantization. Differentiate between uniform and non-uniform quantization.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Quantization** is the process of mapping a continuous-amplitude sample value to one of a finite set of discrete amplitude levels, each represented by a binary code. **(1 mark)** | Aspect | Uniform Quantization | Non-Uniform Quantization | |---|---|---| | Step size *Δ* | Constant across full range | Variable — finer for small signals, coarser for large | | SNR | Poor for low-amplitude signals | Nearly constant SNR across all amplitudes | | Complexity | Simple, low computational cost | Requires compander (compressor + expander) | | Example | Linear PCM in CD audio | μ-law, A-law in PCM telephony (G.711) | **(2 marks for differentiation table)** --- ### Part B — 14-Mark Module 1 Internal Choice Questions > **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** You will be given EITHER Question A OR Question B (both 14 marks) — solve the one you choose. Each has two sub-parts of 7 marks each. --- #### **Question A — Option 1 (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **(a) Explain the complete process of digitization of sound with a neat block diagram. Discuss the role of the anti-aliasing filter in the pipeline.** **[7 marks]** **Model Solution:** The digitization of sound is the conversion of a continuous analog audio signal into a discrete binary representation suitable for digital storage and processing. The complete pipeline consists of four sequential stages: 1. **Anti-Aliasing Filtering:** An analog low-pass filter removes all frequency components above *f*s/2 (the Nyquist frequency) to prevent aliasing — the folding back of high-frequency content into the audible band during sampling. **(1 mark)** 2. **Sampling:** The filtered signal is sampled at regular intervals *T*s = 1/*f*s using a sample-and-hold circuit, producing a discrete-time but continuous-amplitude sequence *x*(*nT*s). The Nyquist criterion *f*s ≥ 2 *f*max must be satisfied. **(1.5 marks)** 3. **Quantization:** Each sample amplitude is rounded to the nearest of *L* = 2*n* discrete levels, where *n* is the bit depth. This step is **irreversible and lossy**, introducing quantization noise with power *σ*q2 = *Δ*2/12. **(1.5 marks)** 4. **PCM Encoding:** Each quantized level index is encoded as an *n*-bit binary word, producing the final digital bitstream. **(1 mark)** **Block Diagram:** (Refer to Figure 2 in Section 4.2 — Microphone → Anti-aliasing filter → Sample-and-Hold → Quantizer → PCM Encoder → Storage/Transmission.) **(1 mark)** **Role of Anti-Aliasing Filter:** Real audio signals contain energy above 20 kHz (harmonics, RF interference, ultrasonic noise). If sampled directly, these fold back into the audible band as **aliasing distortion**, an artifact that cannot be removed by any downstream digital filter. Hence the anti-aliasing filter is a **mandatory pre-processing safeguard** placed *before* the sampler. Its cutoff is set at *f*c = *f*s/2, and a gentle roll-off is preferred to avoid phase distortion — modern ADCs use oversampling with digital sharp filters to relax this requirement. **(1 mark)** --- **(b) A music signal has a maximum audible frequency of 15 kHz. It is sampled at 32 kHz and quantized using 8 bits per sample. Calculate: (i) The Nyquist rate and confirm whether aliasing occurs. (ii) The SQNR in dB. (iii) The bit rate for a stereo (2-channel) recording. (iv) The size in MB of a 5-minute recording.** **[7 marks]** **Model Solution:** **Given:** *f*max = 15 kHz, *f*s = 32 kHz, *n* = 8 bits, *c* = 2, *t* = 5 × 60 = 300 s. **(i) Nyquist Rate and Aliasing Check:** **(1.5 marks)** $$ \begin{aligned} f_{\text{Nyquist}} &= 2 \times f_{\max} = 2 \times 15 = 30\ \text{kHz} \end{aligned} $$ Since *f*s = 32 kHz > 30 kHz, the **Nyquist criterion is satisfied** and **no aliasing occurs**. The highest reproducible frequency is *f*s/2 = 16 kHz, which exceeds 15 kHz. ✓ **(ii) SQNR in dB:** **(1.5 marks)** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{SQNR} &= 6.02\, n + 1.76\ \text{dB} \\ &= 6.02 \times 8 + 1.76 \\ &= 48.16 + 1.76 \\ &= 49.92\ \text{dB} \end{aligned} $$ **(iii) Stereo Bit Rate:** **(1.5 marks)** $$ \begin{aligned} R &= f_{s} \times n \times c = 32000 \times 8 \times 2 \\ &= 512{,}000\ \text{bps} = 512\ \text{kbps} \end{aligned} $$ **(iv) File Size for 5 Minutes:** **(2.5 marks)** $$ \begin{aligned} S &= R \times t = 512{,}000 \times 300 \\ &= 153{,}600{,}000\ \text{bits} \\ &= \frac{153{,}600{,}000}{8}\ \text{bytes} \\ &= 19{,}200{,}000\ \text{bytes} \\ &\approx 18.31\ \text{MiB} \quad \text{(Mebibytes, binary)} \end{aligned} $$ In decimal MB (1 MB = 106 bytes): $$ S = \frac{153.6 \times 10^{6}}{10^{6}} = 153.6\ \text{Mb}\ \text{of data} \quad \Rightarrow \quad S \approx 19.2\ \text{MB} $$ --- #### **Question B — Option 2 (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – May 2023] **(a) Derive the expression for Signal-to-Quantization-Noise Ratio (SQNR) of an *n*-bit uniform quantizer. Hence explain the "6 dB per bit" rule.** **[7 marks]** **Model Solution:** **Assumptions:** Uniform mid-tread quantizer with *L* = 2*n* levels; full-scale sinusoidal input signal of peak-to-peak amplitude *V*pp = 2*n* · *Δ*; quantization error *e* uniformly distributed in [−*Δ*/2, +*Δ*/2]. **Step 1 — Quantization Noise Power:** **(2 marks)** $$ \sigma_{q}^{2} = \frac{\Delta^{2}}{12} $$ **Step 2 — Signal Power (sinusoid, RMS over 1 Ω):** **(2 marks)** $$ P_{\text{signal}} = \frac{V_{\text{rms}}^{2}}{1} = \frac{(V_{\text{pp}}/2\sqrt{2})^{2}}{1} = \frac{V_{\text{pp}}^{2}}{8} = \frac{(2^{n}\,\Delta)^{2}}{8} = \frac{2^{2n}\,\Delta^{2}}{8} $$ **Step 3 — Linear SQNR:** **(1.5 marks)** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{SQNR}_{\text{linear}} &= \frac{P_{\text{signal}}}{P_{\text{noise}}} = \frac{2^{2n}\,\Delta^{2}/8}{\Delta^{2}/12} = \frac{12 \cdot 2^{2n}}{8} = \frac{3}{2}\cdot 2^{2n} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4 — Conversion to Decibels:** **(1.5 marks)** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{SQNR}_{\text{dB}} &= 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{3}{2}\cdot 2^{2n}\right) = 10\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{3}{2}\right) + 2n \cdot 10 \log_{10}(2) \\ &\approx 1.76 + 2n \times 3.01 \\ &\approx 1.76 + 6.02\, n \end{aligned} $$ **Final Result:** $$ \boxed{\ \text{SQNR (dB)} = 6.02\, n + 1.76\ } $$ **The "6 dB per bit" rule:** The derivative of SQNR w.r.t. *n* is 6.02 dB/bit. Hence, **each additional bit of resolution increases the dynamic range by approximately 6 dB**, which corresponds to a doubling of the number of distinguishable amplitude levels. This rule is central to ADC design trade-offs — increasing bit depth by 4 bits quadruples the SQNR (≈24 dB improvement), dramatically enhancing fidelity at the cost of doubled storage and bandwidth. **(Bonus: 1 mark for the rule interpretation if discussed.)** --- **(b) An audio recording uses 16-bit quantization at a 48 kHz sampling rate in stereo. Calculate: (i) SQNR in dB and the corresponding number of distinguishable amplitude levels. (ii) Bit rate of the recording. (iii) Size of a 3-minute file in MiB. (iv) If a compression algorithm reduces the file size by a factor of 10, what is the effective bit rate?** **[7 marks]** **Model Solution:** **Given:** *n* = 16 bits, *f*s = 48 kHz, *c* = 2, *t* = 3 × 60 = 180 s. **(i) SQNR and Levels:** **(1.5 marks)** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{SQNR} &= 6.02 \times 16 + 1.76 = 96.32 + 1.76 = 98.08\ \text{dB} \\ L &= 2^{n} = 2^{16} = 65{,}536\ \text{levels} \end{aligned} $$ **(ii) Bit Rate:** **(1.5 marks)** $$ \begin{aligned} R &= f_{s} \times n \times c = 48{,}000 \times 16 \times 2 \\ &= 1{,}536{,}000\ \text{bps} = 1.536\ \text{Mbps} \end{aligned} $$ **(iii) File Size in MiB:** **(2 marks)** $$ \begin{aligned} S &= R \times t = 1{,}536{,}000 \times 180 = 276{,}480{,}000\ \text{bits} \\ &= \frac{276{,}480{,}000}{8} = 34{,}560{,}000\ \text{bytes} \\ &\approx 32.96\ \text{MiB} \quad (\text{since } 1\ \text{MiB} = 2^{20} = 1{,}048{,}576\ \text{bytes}) \end{aligned} $$ **(iv) Compressed Bit Rate (10× reduction):** **(2 marks)** $$ \begin{aligned} R_{\text{compressed}} &= \frac{R}{10} = \frac{1.536\ \text{Mbps}}{10} = 153.6\ \text{kbps} \end{aligned} $$ This corresponds to high-quality MP3/AAC compression (≈192 kbps is typical for "transparent" near-CD-quality lossy encoding). --- ### 5.4 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls (Read Carefully Before Writing Your Exam)** > > 1. **Confusing Nyquist Rate with Nyquist Frequency:** Nyquist rate = 2*f*max (a *sampling* rate); Nyquist frequency = *f*s/2 (a *signal* frequency). Examiners deduct 1 full mark if these are swapped. Mnemonic: "Nyquist **rate** is for **sampling**; Nyquist **frequency** is the highest **signal** you can store." > > 2. **Forgetting the channel multiplier in bit rate / file size:** Stereo = ×2; mono = ×1; 5.1 surround = ×6. Omitting the channel factor in a stereo problem is a guaranteed 2-mark loss. > > 3. **Mixing up MB and MiB:** KTU typically uses the binary definition (1 MiB = 1,048,576 bytes = 220 bytes). Using decimal MB (106 bytes) gives a smaller number — examiners may deduct 0.5–1 mark for unit confusion. > > 4. **Writing "quantization is lossless":** Quantization is **irreversibly lossy** — once a sample is rounded, the original value is mathematically gone. Always state this explicitly. > > 5. **Omitting the anti-aliasing filter in block diagrams:** A digitization block diagram *without* an anti-aliasing filter at the input is incomplete. Examiners expect to see it labelled as "Low-pass filter, *f*c = *f*s/2." > > 6. **Forgetting units in the SQNR formula:** SQNR is in **dB**; writing "98.08" without "dB" loses 0.5 mark. > > 7. **Sign error in the SQNR derivation:** The constant is **+1.76 dB**, not −1.76 dB. This sign error cascades through the entire numerical answer. --- ### 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!TIP] > **Last-Minute Revision — 10 Bullets Cover the Whole Topic** > > - **Digitization pipeline:** *Anti-aliasing filter → Sampling → Quantization → PCM Encoding → Storage*. > - **Nyquist theorem:** *f*s ≥ 2 *f*max (mandatory; otherwise aliasing, which is irreversible). > - **Quantization levels:** *L* = 2*n*, where *n* is bits per sample. > - **Quantization step (resolution):** *Δ* = (*V*max − *V*min) / 2*n*. > - **Quantization noise power:** *σ*q2 = *Δ*2/12 (uniform distribution assumed). > - **★ Master formula — SQNR:** 6.02 *n* + 1.76 dB (the "6 dB per bit" rule). > - **Bit rate:** *R* = *f*s × *n* × *c* (channels). > - **File size:** *S* = *R* × *t* (in bits; divide by 8 for bytes; by 220 for MiB). > - **Alias frequency:** *f*alias = |*f* − *k f*s|, smallest non-negative result. > - **CD-quality benchmarks:** 44.1 kHz, 16-bit, stereo → 1.411 Mbps → ~10 MB per minute of audio.

topics.title: Storage Requirements of Multimedia Applications - Need for Compression
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECNT742 - MULTIMEDIA COMPRESSION > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Storage Requirements of Multimedia Applications - Need for Compression # 📘 Module 1 — Storage Requirements of Multimedia Applications & The Need for Compression ## 1.1 Core Technical Definition **Multimedia Data** refers to any digital content that combines multiple forms of media — namely **text, images, audio, video, and animation** — into a single integrated information stream. In the context of KTU's *Multimedia Compression* syllabus, multimedia data is characterized by its inherently **massive raw size**, **high transmission bandwidth demand**, and **real-time delivery constraints**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Formal KTU Definition:** > *Storage Requirements of Multimedia Applications* is the quantitative measure of digital memory (in bits, bytes, KB, MB, GB, or TB) needed to represent raw, uncompressed multimedia content. It is governed by the **sampling rate, quantization depth, spatial resolution, temporal resolution (frame rate), and channel count** of the source signal. **Compression**, in this domain, refers to the set of mathematical techniques used to **reduce the number of bits** required to represent multimedia content by exploiting **statistical redundancy** and **human perceptual limitations** (psycho-visual redundancy). --- ## 1.2 Intuitive Overview — The "Library Analogy" 📚 Imagine a library that has **100 identical copies of the same 1000-page encyclopedia** taking up an entire floor. The librarian realizes: 1. Many pages are blank → **Spatial redundancy** 2. Many pages repeat yesterday's news verbatim → **Temporal redundancy** 3. Readers can't tell the difference between the original color photograph and a slightly faded version → **Psycho-visual redundancy** 4. The encyclopedia is written in 5 languages, but most readers only read English → **Spectral redundancy** So instead, the librarian keeps **one master copy** and distributes a **short summary** (lossy compression) or a **perfectly reconstructed copy via smart indexing** (lossless compression). That is exactly what multimedia compression does for digital data. > [!NOTE] > **Why does this matter in Kerala's engineering context?** > With the rise of OTT platforms (Netflix, Hotstar), telemedicine, e-learning platforms (KTU's own digital repository), and ISRO's satellite imaging, the **bandwidth and storage economics** of multimedia determine whether a product is viable. --- ## 1.3 Storage Requirements Across Media Types | Media Type | Standard Raw Parameters | Uncompressed Data Rate | 1-Hour Storage | |---|---|---|---| | **Text (ASCII)** | 8 bits/character | ~64 kbps (typical doc) | ~28 MB | | **Audio (CD Quality)** | 44,100 samples/sec × 16 bits × 2 channels | **1.411 Mbps** | ~635 MB | | **Image (24-bit Color)** | 1024 × 768 × 24 bits | N/A (single frame) | ~2.36 MB | | **SD Video (480p)** | 720 × 480 × 24 bits × 30 fps | ~248 Mbps | ~111 GB | | **HD Video (1080p)** | 1920 × 1080 × 24 bits × 30 fps | ~1.49 Gbps | ~670 GB | | **4K Video (UHD)** | 3840 × 2160 × 24 bits × 60 fps | ~11.9 Gbps | ~5.4 TB | > [!WARNING] > **Critical Observation for KTU Exams:** > Notice that **1 hour of uncompressed HD video ≈ 670 GB** — this is **larger than most consumer hard drives from the early 2010s**. Without compression, streaming services like YouTube, Netflix, and even KTU's own video lectures would be physically and economically impossible. --- ## 1.4 Why Compression is a *Necessity* — Not a Luxury The three hard constraints that make compression mandatory: 1. **Storage Constraint 💾**: Modern 4K video at 60 fps generates ~**12 Gbps** — a 2-hour movie would require ~5.4 TB. Even a 1 TB SSD holds barely 11 minutes of raw 4K footage. 2. **Bandwidth Constraint 🌐**: India's average broadband speed (~60 Mbps as of 2024) cannot transmit raw HD video. Streaming requires compression ratios of **100:1 to 300:1**. 3. **Real-Time Constraint ⏱️**: Video conferencing (Zoom, Google Meet) demands **latency under 150 ms**. This requires aggressive lossy compression to fit within network capacity. > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Scheme Highlight:** > The syllabus explicitly emphasizes that *compression is the enabling technology* for all modern multimedia applications. Without it, digital television, mobile video, telemedicine, and video-on-demand (VoD) services cannot exist. # 🔬 Deep Theoretical Analysis — Redundancies, Information Theory, and the Compression Imperative ## 2.1 The Three Pillars of Compression (Why it Works) Compression algorithms succeed because multimedia data contains **three exploitable structures**: ### Pillar 1: Statistical (Data) Redundancy Most multimedia signals (especially natural images and audio) exhibit **strong pixel-to-pixel and sample-to-sample correlations**. Adjacent pixels in a photograph often have nearly identical values — this is **spatial correlation**. Adjacent video frames often differ by tiny amounts — this is **temporal correlation**. ### Pillar 2: Psycho-visual (Perceptual) Redundancy The human eye and ear have limited sensitivity: - Human vision cannot distinguish subtle color variations (especially in chrominance) - Human hearing has a "threshold of hearing" and is insensitive to very quiet sounds masked by louder ones - Compression can discard imperceptible information → **lossy compression** ### Pillar 3: Coding Redundancy If symbol probabilities are not uniformly distributed, shorter codes can be assigned to frequent symbols (e.g., Huffman coding, Arithmetic coding). --- ## 2.2 Classification of Multimedia Redundancies > [!IMPORTANT] > **The Four Canonical Redundancies in KTU Multimedia Compression:** 1. **Spatial Redundancy (Intra-frame)** — Correlation between neighboring pixels *within* the same frame. Exploited by **JPEG**, **transform coding (DCT)**. 2. **Temporal Redundancy (Inter-frame)** — Correlation between consecutive video frames. Exploited by **MPEG, H.264, H.265** using motion compensation. 3. **Spectral Redundancy** — Correlation between different color components (e.g., R, G, B) or frequency bands. Reduced via **color space conversion (RGB → YCbCr)**. 4. **Psycho-visual Redundancy** — Information that exists mathematically but is imperceptible to humans. Discarded via **quantization** in lossy schemes. --- ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet & High-Yield Equations > [!NOTE] > **Universal Compression Identity:** > Any multimedia signal $S$ can be decomposed as: > $$S = I_{\text{original}} + R_{\text{redundant}}$$ > where $I_{\text{original}}$ is the essential information and $R_{\text{redundant}}$ is the removable portion. The goal of compression is to minimize the bit-representation of $I_{\text{original}}$. ### 📊 Core Mathematical Toolkit | $\#$ | Formula / Concept | Symbolic Form | Purpose / Application | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Uncompressed Size** | $S = W \times H \times B \times D \times F$ | Compute raw storage of media | | 2 | **Bits Per Pixel (bpp)** | $bpp = \dfrac{N_{\text{bits}}}{N_{\text{pixels}}}$ | Measure image information density | | 3 | **Compression Ratio** | $C_R = \dfrac{S_{\text{original}}}{S_{\text{compressed}}}$ | Quantify compression effectiveness | | 4 | **Space Saving %** | $S_{\%} = \left(1 - \dfrac{1}{C_R}\right) \times 100$ | Express savings as percentage | | 5 | **Bits Per Second (Audio)** | $R = f_s \times n \times c$ | Compute raw audio bitrate | | 6 | **Channel Capacity (Shannon)** | $C = B \log_2\!\left(1 + \dfrac{S}{N}\right)$ | Maximum lossless data rate | | 7 | **Redundancy in Bits** | $R_d = H_{\text{max}} - H$ | Lossless theoretical minimum | | 8 | **PSNR (Image Quality)** | $PSNR = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{MAX_I^{\,2}}{MSE}\right)$ | Measure reconstruction fidelity | | 9 | **MSE (Mean Squared Error)** | $MSE = \dfrac{1}{mn}\sum_{i=0}^{m-1}\sum_{j=0}^{n-1}\!\left[I(i,j) - K(i,j)\right]^2$ | Distortion metric | | 10 | **Entropy (Shannon)** | $H = -\sum_{i=1}^{n} p_i \log_2 p_i$ | Lower bound for lossless coding | **Variable Legend:** - $W$ = Width (pixels), $H$ = Height (pixels), $B$ = Bits/pixel, $D$ = Duration (sec), $F$ = Frames/sec - $f_s$ = Sampling frequency, $n$ = bits/sample, $c$ = number of channels - $H_{\text{max}} = \log_2 N$ for uniform distribution - $MAX_I$ = Maximum pixel value (255 for 8-bit), $MSE$ = Mean Squared Error --- ## 2.4 Lossless vs Lossy Compression — Engineering Trade-off | Attribute | **Lossless** | **Lossy** | |---|---|---| | Reconstruction | **Perfect** (bit-exact) | Approximate (perceptually similar) | | Compression Ratio | 2:1 to 4:1 typically | 10:1 to 100:1+ | | Typical Algorithms | Huffman, LZW, RLE, Arithmetic | JPEG, MPEG, MP3, H.265 | | Use Cases | Medical imaging, source code, text | Streaming, broadcast, VoIP | | Quality Metric | Identical to original | PSNR, SSIM, MOS | | Example File Size (1 hr audio) | WAV (~635 MB) | MP3 128 kbps (~56 MB) | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Engineering Insight:** > The choice between lossless and lossy is *not* technical — it is **economic and application-driven**. A radiologist diagnosing cancer cannot tolerate any loss (lossless mandatory). A TikTok video can lose 95% of its data with no perceived quality drop. # 🧮 Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Numerical Analysis ## 3.1 Derivation: Uncompressed Storage Requirement The fundamental storage equation for a digital image is derived from first principles. **Starting Principle:** A digital image is a 2D discrete grid of $W \times H$ pixels, where each pixel carries $B$ bits of color/luminance information. **Step 1 — Total number of pixels in one frame:** $$N_{\text{pixels}} = W \times H$$ **Step 2 — Bits required per frame:** $$S_{\text{frame}} = N_{\text{pixels}} \times B = W \times H \times B \text{ bits}$$ **Step 3 — Frames per second (temporal sampling):** If video is sampled at $F$ frames per second, then in $D$ seconds we have $F \times D$ frames. **Step 4 — Total bits for video of duration $D$:** $$S_{\text{total}} = W \times H \times B \times F \times D \text{ bits}$$ **Step 5 — Convert bits to bytes (divide by 8):** $$S_{\text{bytes}} = \dfrac{W \times H \times B \times F \times D}{8} \text{ Bytes}$$ **Step 6 — Convert to Gigabytes (divide by $1024^3$):** $$S_{\text{GB}} = \dfrac{W \times H \times B \times F \times D}{8 \times 1024^3} \text{ GB}$$ > This is the **master storage formula** for any uncompressed digital video stream. --- ## 3.2 Worked Example 1 — HD Video Storage Calculation > **Problem (KTU-style):** Calculate the storage required (in GB) for **10 minutes** of uncompressed HD video with the following parameters: > Resolution = $1920 \times 1080$, Bits per pixel = 24, Frame rate = 30 fps. **Step 1 — Identify given values:** $$W = 1920,\quad H = 1080,\quad B = 24,\quad F = 30,\quad D = 10 \times 60 = 600 \text{ s}$$ **Step 2 — Apply master formula:** $$S_{\text{bits}} = 1920 \times 1080 \times 24 \times 30 \times 600$$ **Step 3 — Compute intermediate product:** $$1920 \times 1080 = 2{,}073{,}600$$ **Step 4 — Continue multiplication:** $$2{,}073{,}600 \times 24 = 49{,}766{,}400$$ **Step 5 — Multiply by frame rate:** $$49{,}766{,}400 \times 30 = 1{,}492{,}992{,}000$$ **Step 6 — Multiply by duration:** $$1{,}492{,}992{,}000 \times 600 = 895{,}795{,}200{,}000 \text{ bits}$$ **Step 7 — Convert to GB:** $$S_{\text{GB}} = \dfrac{895{,}795{,}200{,}000}{8 \times 1{,}073{,}741{,}824} = \dfrac{895{,}795{,}200{,}000}{8{,}589{,}934{,}592}$$ $$\boxed{S_{\text{GB}} \approx 104.29 \text{ GB}}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Insight:** Just **10 minutes** of raw HD video consumes **~104 GB** — roughly the entire storage of a base-model iPhone 15. This single calculation *proves the necessity of compression*. --- ## 3.3 Worked Example 2 — Compression Ratio & Space Saving > **Problem:** A 250 MB raw medical image is compressed to 12.5 MB using lossless JPEG-2000. Find the compression ratio and space saving percentage. **Step 1 — Compression Ratio:** $$C_R = \dfrac{S_{\text{original}}}{S_{\text{compressed}}} = \dfrac{250}{12.5} = 20$$ **Step 2 — Space Saving Percentage:** $$S_{\%} = \left(1 - \dfrac{1}{C_R}\right) \times 100 = \left(1 - \dfrac{1}{20}\right) \times 100$$ $$S_{\%} = \left(1 - 0.05\right) \times 100 = 0.95 \times 100$$ $$\boxed{S_{\%} = 95\%}$$ **Interpretation:** 95% of the original storage is freed; the image still reconstructs **bit-exactly** to the original — a perfect outcome for medical imaging. --- ## 3.4 Worked Example 3 — CD Audio Bitrate > **Problem:** Compute the bitrate and 1-hour file size of uncompressed stereo audio sampled at 44.1 kHz with 16-bit quantization. **Step 1 — Apply the audio formula:** $$R = f_s \times n \times c = 44{,}100 \times 16 \times 2$$ $$R = 1{,}411{,}200 \text{ bits/sec} = 1.4112 \text{ Mbps}$$ **Step 2 — 1-hour storage in bits:** $$S_{\text{1hr}} = 1{,}411{,}200 \times 3600 = 5{,}080{,}320{,}000 \text{ bits}$$ **Step 3 — Convert to MB:** $$S_{\text{1hr}} = \dfrac{5{,}080{,}320{,}000}{8 \times 1{,}048{,}576} \approx 605.5 \text{ MB}$$ This matches the standard **~635 MB** size of a CD-DA track. --- ## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Pre-Compression vs Post-Compression | Media Type | Uncompressed Size | Compressed Size | Compression Used | Ratio | |---|---|---|---|---| | 700-page novel (text) | ~2 MB | ~500 KB | LZW / Deflate (ZIP) | 4:1 | | 1 MP photo | ~3 MB | ~300 KB | JPEG (quality 75) | 10:1 | | 3-min song | ~30 MB (WAV) | ~3 MB (MP3) | MP3 128 kbps | 10:1 | | 2-hr SD movie | ~27 GB | ~700 MB | MPEG-2 DVD | 38:1 | | 2-hr HD movie | ~670 GB | ~4–8 GB | H.264/AVC | ~100:1 | | 2-hr 4K movie | ~5.4 TB | ~20–50 GB | H.265/HEVC | ~150:1 | > [!IMPORTANT] > The **H.265/HEVC** codec achieves ~150:1 compression on 4K video while maintaining perceptual quality equivalent to raw footage — this is the **engineering marvel** that makes Netflix 4K streaming possible over Indian broadband. # 🗺️ Structural Diagrams & Functional Architecture ## 4.1 Block Diagram — Multimedia Compression Pipeline The following diagram illustrates the **generic functional architecture** of a multimedia compression system (encoder-decoder pair), showing how raw multimedia data flows through transformation, quantization, and encoding stages to achieve compression. ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Raw Multimedia Input
Image, Audio, Video] --> B[Pre-processing
Color Space Conversion
RGB to YCbCr] B --> C[Sampling and
Quantization] C --> D[Transform Stage
DCT, DWT, DFT] D --> E[Quantization
Lossy Bit Reduction] E --> F[Entropy Encoding
Huffman, Arithmetic] F --> G[Compressed Bitstream
Storage or Transmission] H[Compressed Bitstream
Input] --> I[Entropy Decoding] I --> J[De-quantization] J --> K[Inverse Transform
IDCT, IDWT] K --> L[Reconstruction
YCbCr to RGB] L --> M[Decompressed Output
Approximate or Exact] style A fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#0050b3,color:#000 style G fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724,color:#000 style M fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#856404,color:#000 ``` **Pipeline Interpretation:** - **Left side (Encoder):** Raw data → preprocessing → sampling → transform → quantization → entropy coding → compressed bits - **Right side (Decoder):** Reverses each stage in inverse order - **Quantization** is the **only lossy stage** — all others are mathematically reversible --- ## 4.2 Classification Tree — Types of Redundancy ```mermaid flowchart TD R[Multimedia Redundancy] --> R1[Spatial
Intra-frame Pixel Correlation] R --> R2[Temporal
Inter-frame Frame Correlation] R --> R3[Spectral
Cross-channel Color Correlation] R --> R4[Psycho-visual
Human Perception Limits] R1 --> R1a[Exploited by JPEG DCT] R2 --> R2a[Exploited by MPEG H.264 Motion Estimation] R3 --> R3a[Reduced via RGB to YCbCr Conversion] R4 --> R4a[Exploited by JPEG MP3 Quantization Tables] style R fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#721c24,color:#000 style R1 fill:#d1ecf1,stroke:#0c5460,color:#000 style R2 fill:#d1ecf1,stroke:#0c5460,color:#000 style R3 fill:#d1ecf1,stroke:#0c5460,color:#000 style R4 fill:#d1ecf1,stroke:#0c5460,color:#000 ``` --- ## 4.3 Decision Flowchart — Lossless vs Lossy Selection ```mermaid flowchart TD S[Multimedia Data Type] --> Q1{Is bit-exact
reconstruction
required?} Q1 -- Yes --> Q1a[Medical Imaging] Q1 -- Yes --> Q1b[Scientific Data] Q1 -- Yes --> Q1c[Source Code Executables] Q1a --> L1[Use Lossless Compression] Q1b --> L1 Q1c --> L1 Q1 -- No --> Q2{Is real-time
delivery required?} Q2 -- Yes --> L2[Use Lossy Compression
Low Latency Priority] Q2 -- No --> Q3{Target bitrate
or storage budget?} Q3 --> L3[Apply Lossy with
Controlled Quality] Q3 --> L4[Apply Lossless if
Budget Permits] L1 --> X1[Algorithms: Huffman, LZW, PNG, FLAC] L2 --> X2[Algorithms: H.264, Opus, AAC, AV1] L3 --> X3[Algorithms: JPEG, MP3, HEVC] L4 --> X4[Algorithms: PNG, ZIP, BZIP2] style S fill:#e2e3e5,stroke:#383d41,color:#000 style L1 fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724,color:#000 style L2 fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#721c24,color:#000 style L3 fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#856404,color:#000 style L4 fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724,color:#000 ``` --- ## 4.4 Bandwidth Reality Check — Compression Enables Everything ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Raw HD Video
1.49 Gbps] --> B{Apply Compression?} B -- No --> X1[Impossible
Requires 1.5 Gbps Link] B -- Yes H.264 --> Y1[~5 Mbps
Streamable on 4G LTE] B -- Yes H.265 --> Y2[~2 Mbps
Streamable on 3G] B -- Yes AV1 --> Y3[~1.5 Mbps
Streamable on 2G Edge] style A fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#721c24,color:#000 style X1 fill:#f5c6cb,stroke:#721c24,color:#000 style Y1 fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724,color:#000 style Y2 fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724,color:#000 style Y3 fill:#d1ecf1,stroke:#0c5460,color:#000 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Diagram Insight:** This single diagram encapsulates the *entire reason* the KTU syllabus dedicates an entire course to compression. Without the "Yes" branch, none of India's UPI-video, telemedicine, or e-learning ecosystem could exist. # 📝 KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 🅰️ PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **Define multimedia. List any four examples of multimedia data and explain why compression is essential for each.** **Model Answer:** **Multimedia** is the integration of multiple forms of digital content — text, images, audio, video, and animation — into a single interactive application. **Four Examples:** 1. **High-Definition Video (1080p, 4K):** Raw HD video requires ~1.49 Gbps bandwidth and ~670 GB per hour. Compression reduces this by ~100:1, enabling streaming. 2. **Digital Audio (CD/MP3):** Uncompressed stereo audio at 44.1 kHz × 16-bit requires 1.41 Mbps. MP3 compression at 128 kbps yields ~11:1 reduction. 3. **Satellite Imagery:** A single ISRO Resourcesat-2 LISS-III image spans ~150 MB. Compression enables faster downlink and storage efficiency. 4. **Medical Imaging (MRI/CT):** A single MRI scan can exceed 50 MB. Lossless compression preserves diagnostic integrity while saving hospital storage. > **Why essential:** Without compression, storage costs become prohibitive, transmission exceeds bandwidth, and real-time delivery is impossible. --- ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** **Differentiate between lossless and lossy compression with one example each.** **Model Answer:** | Feature | **Lossless Compression** | **Lossy Compression** | |---|---|---| | Reconstruction | Exact (bit-for-bit identical) | Approximate (perceptually close) | | Compression Ratio | Low (2:1 to 4:1) | High (10:1 to 100:1+) | | Information Loss | Zero | Irreversible data discarded | | Use Case | Medical imaging, source code | Streaming, broadcast | | Example Algorithm | Huffman, LZW, PNG | JPEG, MP3, H.264 | | File Example | ZIP archive, PNG image | MP3 song, MP4 video | **Example:** PNG uses **lossless** DEFLATE — pixel values preserved exactly. JPEG uses **lossy** DCT + quantization — high-frequency details discarded permanently. --- ## 🅱️ PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) ### **Question A (14 Marks): Compression Fundamentals** **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Modified]** #### **Part (a) — 7 Marks** **Explain the four types of redundancies present in multimedia data. How are they exploited in compression algorithms?** **Model Solution:** Multimedia data contains four major redundancies that compression algorithms exploit: **(1) Spatial Redundancy (Intra-frame):** Adjacent pixels in a 2D image are highly correlated. In a photograph of a blue sky, neighboring pixels have nearly identical RGB values. JPEG exploits this via the **Discrete Cosine Transform (DCT)** applied to 8×8 pixel blocks, which concentrates energy into few low-frequency coefficients that can be compactly encoded. **(2) Temporal Redundancy (Inter-frame):** Consecutive video frames often differ by less than 5% of pixel values. MPEG, H.264, and H.265 exploit this via **motion estimation and compensation**, where only the motion vectors and residual differences are encoded instead of full frames. **(3) Spectral Redundancy:** The R, G, B components of natural images are strongly correlated. By converting to **YCbCr** color space, the chrominance channels (Cb, Cr) can be subsampled (4:2:0 chroma subsampling) with minimal perceptual impact, reducing data by ~50%. **(4) Psycho-visual Redundancy:** The human visual system (HVS) is insensitive to: - High-frequency spatial details (beyond ~30 cycles/degree) - Subtle chrominance variations - Sounds masked by louder simultaneous frequencies Compression algorithms use **quantization tables** (JPEG) or **psychoacoustic models** (MP3) to discard this imperceptible information. --- #### **Part (b) — 7 Marks** **A standard definition (SD) television signal has a resolution of 720 × 480 pixels, 24 bits per pixel, and 30 frames per second. Compute:** **(i) The uncompressed data rate in Mbps.** **(ii) The total storage in GB required for a 90-minute movie.** **(iii) The compression ratio required to fit this movie onto a 4.7 GB DVD.** **Model Solution:** **Given:** $W = 720$, $H = 480$, $B = 24$, $F = 30$ fps, $D = 90 \times 60 = 5400$ s, DVD capacity = 4.7 GB. **(i) Uncompressed Data Rate:** $$R = W \times H \times B \times F = 720 \times 480 \times 24 \times 30$$ $$R = 720 \times 480 = 345{,}600$$ $$R = 345{,}600 \times 24 = 8{,}294{,}400 \text{ bits/frame}$$ $$R = 8{,}294{,}400 \times 30 = 248{,}832{,}000 \text{ bits/sec}$$ $$\boxed{R = 248.832 \text{ Mbps}}$$ **(ii) Total Storage for 90 minutes:** $$S_{\text{bits}} = R \times D = 248{,}832{,}000 \times 5400$$ $$S_{\text{bits}} = 1{,}343{,}692{,}800{,}000 \text{ bits}$$ Converting to GB: $$S_{\text{GB}} = \dfrac{1{,}343{,}692{,}800{,}000}{8 \times 1{,}073{,}741{,}824}$$ $$\boxed{S_{\text{GB}} \approx 156.42 \text{ GB}}$$ **(iii) Required Compression Ratio for DVD:** $$C_R = \dfrac{S_{\text{original}}}{S_{\text{DVD}}} = \dfrac{156.42}{4.7}$$ $$\boxed{C_R \approx 33.28 : 1}$$ **Conclusion:** MPEG-2 DVD compression achieves approximately **33:1** ratio, which is why early DVDs could fit ~2 hours of SD video despite the DVD's modest 4.7 GB capacity. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks): Storage Calculations & Algorithm Selection** **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Alternative]** #### **Part (a) — 7 Marks** **Calculate the uncompressed storage required for:** **(i) A 5-megapixel photograph (2560 × 1920) with 24 bits per pixel.** **(ii) An audio CD track of 74 minutes duration, sampled at 44.1 kHz, 16-bit stereo.** **Model Solution:** **(i) 5-Megapixel Photograph:** Given: $W = 2560$, $H = 1920$, $B = 24$. $$S_{\text{bits}} = 2560 \times 1920 \times 24$$ $$2560 \times 1920 = 4{,}915{,}200$$ $$4{,}915{,}200 \times 24 = 117{,}964{,}800 \text{ bits}$$ Converting to MB: $$S_{\text{MB}} = \dfrac{117{,}964{,}800}{8 \times 1{,}048{,}576} = \dfrac{117{,}964{,}800}{8{,}388{,}608}$$ $$\boxed{S_{\text{MB}} \approx 14.06 \text{ MB}}$$ A typical JPEG compressed version (quality 85) of this image is ~2 MB, achieving a 7:1 compression ratio. **(ii) Audio CD Track of 74 minutes:** Given: $f_s = 44{,}100$ Hz, $n = 16$ bits, $c = 2$ channels, $D = 74 \times 60 = 4440$ s. $$R = f_s \times n \times c = 44{,}100 \times 16 \times 2 = 1{,}411{,}200 \text{ bps}$$ $$S_{\text{bits}} = 1{,}411{,}200 \times 4440 = 6{,}265{,}728{,}000 \text{ bits}$$ Converting to MB: $$S_{\text{MB}} = \dfrac{6{,}265{,}728{,}000}{8 \times 1{,}048{,}576}$$ $$\boxed{S_{\text{MB}} \approx 746.93 \text{ MB} \approx 0.73 \text{ GB}}$$ This matches the standard **~700 MB** CD-DA capacity, confirming the derivation. --- #### **Part (b) — 7 Marks** **For a 4K UHD video stream (3840 × 2160, 60 fps, 24 bpp):** **(i) Calculate the uncompressed data rate.** **(ii) If this must be delivered over a 25 Mbps internet connection, calculate the required compression ratio.** **(iii) State which compression standard family (JPEG, MPEG, H.264, H.265) is most suitable and justify.** **Model Solution:** **(i) Uncompressed Data Rate:** Given: $W = 3840$, $H = 2160$, $B = 24$, $F = 60$. $$R = 3840 \times 2160 \times 24 \times 60$$ $$3840 \times 2160 = 8{,}294{,}400$$ $$8{,}294{,}400 \times 24 = 199{,}065{,}600$$ $$199{,}065{,}600 \times 60 = 11{,}943{,}936{,}000 \text{ bps}$$ $$\boxed{R \approx 11.94 \text{ Gbps}}$$ **(ii) Required Compression Ratio:** $$C_R = \dfrac{R_{\text{original}}}{R_{\text{target}}} = \dfrac{11{,}943{,}936{,}000}{25{,}000{,}000}$$ $$\boxed{C_R \approx 477.76 : 1}$$ **(iii) Most Suitable Standard:** **H.265/HEVC (High Efficiency Video Coding)** is the most suitable choice. **Justification:** - H.265 achieves **50% better compression** than H.264 at equivalent perceptual quality. - It supports 4K UHD natively (up to 8192 × 4320). - Typical 4K streaming bitrate using H.265: **15–25 Mbps** — fits the given bandwidth. - It uses advanced features: **larger CTU blocks (64×64)**, **improved motion compensation**, and **sample-adaptive offset (SAO) filtering**. JPEG is unsuitable (designed for still images). MPEG-2 is outdated (SD/HD era). H.264 works but would require ~40–50 Mbps for equivalent 4K quality, exceeding the 25 Mbps budget. AV1 is a competitive alternative but lacks the mature hardware decoder ecosystem of H.265. --- ## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls > [!WARNING] > **Where KTU Students Typically Lose Marks:** > > 1. **Unit Confusion:** Mixing **MB (10⁶ bytes)** with **MiB (2²⁰ bytes)**. KTU problems almost always expect the **binary (MiB/GiB)** interpretation: $1 \text{ GB} = 1024^3$ bytes = $1{,}073{,}741{,}824$ bytes. > > 2. **Forgetting Channels in Audio:** Stereo = 2 channels; Mono = 1 channel. A student writing $R = 44100 \times 16$ instead of $\times 16 \times 2$ will lose **3 marks** in a 7-mark sub-question. > > 3. **Skipping Conversion Units:** Always show the explicit conversion chain: **bits → bytes → KB → MB → GB**. Examiners allocate marks for *each conversion step*. > > 4. **Misapplying Compression Ratio:** Some students invert it ($S_{\text{compressed}} / S_{\text{original}}$). The correct form is **Original ÷ Compressed** for ratios > 1. > > 5. **Ignoring Color Subsampling:** When comparing storage, failing to mention 4:2:0 chroma subsampling loses the **conceptual depth marks**. > > 6. **Not Writing Units in Final Answer:** Always box the numerical answer with units (e.g., $\boxed{156.42 \text{ GB}}$) — partial credit is awarded for unit correctness. --- ## 🎯 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Summary — Read Before Exam:** - ✅ **Storage of Image** = $W \times H \times B$ bits; **Video** = $W \times H \times B \times F \times D$ bits. - ✅ **Audio Bitrate** = $f_s \times n \times c$ bps. - ✅ **Compression Ratio** $C_R = S_{\text{original}} / S_{\text{compressed}}$; **Space Saving** $= (1 - 1/C_R) \times 100\%$. - ✅ **Four Redundancies:** Spatial, Temporal, Spectral, Psycho-visual. - ✅ **Lossless** preserves every bit (medical, scientific); **Lossy** trades quality for ratio (streaming). - ✅ **1 hour of uncompressed HD video ≈ 670 GB** — proves compression is mandatory. - ✅ **Shannon Entropy** is the theoretical lower bound for lossless compression. - ✅ **H.265/HEVC** delivers ~150:1 compression on 4K video with perceptual quality. - ✅ Always show explicit **unit conversions** in calculations (bits → bytes → KB → MB → GB). - ✅ Binary prefixes: **1 KB = 1024 B**, **1 MB = 1024² B**, **1 GB = 1024³ B**. - ✅ **PSNR** quantifies lossy quality; **higher is better**; typical acceptable value ≥ 30 dB. - ✅ **Color space conversion (RGB → YCbCr)** is the first compression step in most image/video codecs. - ✅ Compression is **enabled by redundancy** — without redundancy, no compression is mathematically possible (Shannon's source coding theorem).

topics.title: Elements of Information Theory
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECNT742 - MULTIMEDIA COMPRESSION > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Elements of Information Theory ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Information Theory** is the mathematical framework developed by **Claude E. Shannon (1948)** that quantifies the production, transmission, and reception of data. It defines the fundamental limits (the *Shannon limits*) for both data compression (lossless) and reliable communication over noisy channels. In the context of **Multimedia Compression (PECNT742)**, Information Theory provides the theoretical foundation that answers two key engineering questions: 1. **How much can a multimedia file (image, audio, video) be compressed *losslessly*?** → Answer: bounded by the **Source Entropy** $H(X)$. 2. **How much compression error can we tolerate *lossily*?** → Answer: bounded by the **Rate-Distortion Function** $R(D)$. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Surprise Meter" > [!NOTE] > Think of Information as a **"Surprise Meter"** (the formal term is *self-information*). > - If your friend **always** wears a red shirt, seeing them in red gives you **zero surprise** = **0 bits** of information. > - If your friend **rarely** wears a blue shirt, seeing them in blue is **highly surprising** = **many bits** of information. Therefore, the rarer an event is, the more information it carries. This is the foundational axiom of Information Theory: $I(x) \propto \frac{1}{P(x)}$. ### 1.3 Core Building Blocks (Glossary for the Module) | Term | Plain-English Meaning | | :--- | :--- | | **Self-Information** $I(x)$ | The amount of "surprise" generated when symbol $x$ actually occurs. | | **Entropy** $H(X)$ | The **average** surprise of a source — the minimum average bits/symbol needed to describe it. | | **Source** $X$ | A random process that emits symbols (e.g., pixels, audio samples). | | **Channel** | The medium through which symbols travel (may be noisy). | | **Channel Capacity** $C$ | The maximum rate (bits/sec) of *reliable* transmission over a channel. | | **Redundancy** | Data carried beyond what is strictly necessary to convey the message. | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Constant for PECNT742:** Always use **logarithm base 2** for multimedia compression, giving the unit **bits/symbol**. The constant in the formula is implicitly $\log_2 e \approx 1.4427$ when converting to nats. --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Operational Logic (Why & How) Information Theory is built in a **layered hierarchy**. Each layer answers a more specific question: 1. **Layer 1 — Self-Information (Micro Level):** Measures surprise of a *single* event. 2. **Layer 2 — Entropy (Macro Level):** Averages the surprise over the *entire* probability distribution of the source. 3. **Layer 3 — Joint & Conditional Entropy (Two-Source Level):** Handles *dependencies* between two random sources (e.g., adjacent pixels in an image, consecutive audio samples). 4. **Layer 4 — Mutual Information (Coupling Level):** Measures how much knowing one variable reduces uncertainty about another. 5. **Layer 5 — Channel Capacity (System Limit):** The supremum of mutual information over all input distributions. ### 2.2 The "Why" Behind the Logarithm The choice of the **logarithmic function** is not arbitrary. It satisfies the three axioms of intuitive "information": 1. **Non-negativity:** $I(x) \geq 0$ (since $0 \leq P(x) \leq 1$). 2. **Monotonicity:** Rarer events → higher information. 3. **Additivity for independent events:** $I(x,y) = I(x) + I(y)$ when $P(x,y) = P(x)P(y)$. Only the logarithm satisfies all three simultaneously. ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Cheat Sheet) > [!NOTE] > **The Master Equation of the Module:** $I(x) = \log_2 \frac{1}{P(x)} = -\log_2 P(x)$ | # | Quantity | Formula | Unit / Notes | | :-: | :--- | :--- | :--- | | 1 | Self-Information | $I(x) = -\log_2 P(x)$ | **bits/symbol** | | 2 | Source Entropy | $H(X) = -\sum_{i=1}^{n} P(x_i) \log_2 P(x_i)$ | bits/symbol (avg) | | 3 | Joint Entropy | $H(X,Y) = -\sum_{i,j} P(x_i,y_j) \log_2 P(x_i,y_j)$ | bits/pair | | 4 | Conditional Entropy | $H(X \vert Y) = -\sum_{i,j} P(x_i,y_j) \log_2 P(x_i \vert y_j)$ | bits/symbol | | 5 | Chain Rule (Entropy) | $H(X,Y) = H(X) + H(Y \vert X)$ | Always true | | 6 | Mutual Information | $I(X;Y) = H(X) - H(X \vert Y) = H(Y) - H(Y \vert X)$ | bits/symbol | | 7 | Symmetry of MI | $I(X;Y) = I(Y;X)$ | Always true | | 8 | MI via Entropy | $I(X;Y) = H(X) + H(Y) - H(X,Y)$ | Triangle identity | | 9 | Binary Entropy Function | $H_b(p) = -p \log_2 p - (1-p) \log_2 (1-p)$ | For BSC/Bernoulli | | 10 | Channel Capacity | $C = \max_{P(x)} I(X;Y)$ | bits/channel-use | | 11 | BSC Capacity | $C_{BSC} = 1 - H_b(p)$ | $p$ = crossover prob. | | 12 | Source Coding Limit (Shannon's 1st) | $\bar{L}_{min} \geq H(X)$ | Avg. codeword length $\geq$ entropy | | 13 | Channel Coding Limit (Shannon's 2nd) | Reliable comm. iff $R < C$ | $R$ = info rate, $C$ = capacity | | 14 | Redundancy | $R_{red} = 1 - \frac{H(X)}{\log_2 n}$ | $n$ = alphabet size | | 15 | $0 \leq H(X) \leq \log_2 n$ | Lower bound = deterministic; Upper = uniform | Bounds check | ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **JPEG / JPEG2000** compressors use the **entropy bound** to approach the Shannon limit through arithmetic coding. - **MP3 / AAC** audio codecs use **Rate-Distortion Theory** (the lossy extension of these equations) to decide which audio coefficients to discard. - **5G / Wi-Fi 6** modems operate just below the **Channel Capacity** limit, exploiting Shannon's second theorem. - **Cloud storage services** use **Huffman & Arithmetic coding** — both proven by Shannon to be optimal *symbol-by-symbol* compressors. > [!IMPORTANT] > **For KTU Valuation:** A derivation that ends with a numerical answer **must state the unit (bits/symbol)**. Losing the unit = losing 1 mark. --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation A: Self-Information to Entropy **Goal:** Prove that the **Entropy** of a discrete source is the expected value of its self-information. **Step 1 — Definition of Self-Information.** For a symbol $x_i$ with probability $P(x_i)$, the information it carries upon occurrence is: $$I(x_i) = \log_2 \frac{1}{P(x_i)} = -\log_2 P(x_i)$$ *Logic:* We use base-2 log so that the unit becomes bits. A probability of 1 (certain event) yields 0 bits, and a probability approaching 0 yields infinity bits. **Step 2 — Definition of Entropy as Expected Self-Information.** The entropy $H(X)$ of a source $X$ emitting symbols from the set $\{x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n\}$ is the statistical mean of $I(x_i)$ over the entire distribution: $$ \begin{aligned} H(X) &= \mathbb{E}\bigl[I(x_i)\bigr] = \sum_{i=1}^{n} P(x_i) \cdot I(x_i) \\ &= \sum_{i=1}^{n} P(x_i) \cdot \bigl(-\log_2 P(x_i)\bigr) \\ &= -\sum_{i=1}^{n} P(x_i) \log_2 P(x_i) \end{aligned} $$ *Logic:* Each symbol $x_i$ is observed with frequency $P(x_i)$, so the average surprise is the probability-weighted sum of individual self-informations. **Step 3 — Boundary Verification (Sanity Check).** - **Minimum:** $H(X) = 0$ when one symbol has $P = 1$ and all others $P = 0$ (the source is deterministic → no surprise). - **Maximum:** $H(X) = \log_2 n$ when all $n$ symbols are equally likely $P(x_i) = \frac{1}{n}$ (maximum uncertainty). $$ \begin{aligned} H(X)_{max} &= -\sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{1}{n} \log_2 \frac{1}{n} = -\log_2 \frac{1}{n} = \log_2 n \end{aligned} $$ *Logic:* Substitution into the formula confirms the upper bound. $\blacksquare$ --- ### 3.2 Derivation B: Channel Capacity of a Binary Symmetric Channel (BSC) **Goal:** Show that the capacity of a BSC with crossover probability $p$ is $C = 1 - H_b(p)$. **Step 1 — Channel Model.** A BSC has input alphabet $X \in \{0, 1\}$ and output alphabet $Y \in \{0, 1\}$. Each transmitted bit is flipped with probability $p$ and passed correctly with probability $(1-p)$: - $P(Y=0 \vert X=0) = 1 - p$ - $P(Y=1 \vert X=0) = p$ - $P(Y=0 \vert X=1) = p$ - $P(Y=1 \vert X=1) = 1 - p$ **Step 2 — Use the Definition of Channel Capacity.** By definition: $$C = \max_{P(x)} I(X;Y)$$ **Step 3 — Expand Mutual Information.** $$ \begin{aligned} I(X;Y) &= H(Y) - H(Y \vert X) \\ &= H(Y) - H_b(p) \end{aligned} $$ *Logic:* $H(Y \vert X) = H_b(p)$ because, given the input, the channel noise is a Bernoulli($p$) random variable regardless of what $X$ was. **Step 4 — Maximize $H(Y)$.** The output $Y$ is a binary random variable whose entropy is maximized when $P(Y=0) = P(Y=1) = \frac{1}{2}$, giving $H(Y)_{max} = 1$ bit. This maximum is **achieved** when the input distribution is uniform, i.e., $P(X=0) = P(X=1) = \frac{1}{2}$ (due to channel symmetry). **Step 5 — Combine.** $$ \begin{aligned} C_{BSC} &= H(Y)_{max} - H_b(p) = 1 - H_b(p) \\ \text{where} \quad H_b(p) &= -p \log_2 p - (1-p) \log_2 (1-p) \end{aligned} $$ *Logic:* This is the **maximum reliable transmission rate** in bits per channel use. $\blacksquare$ **Special Cases (KTU Favorite):** - If $p = 0$ (perfect channel) $\Rightarrow C = 1 - 0 = 1$ bit/use. (Full throughput.) - If $p = 0.5$ (random noise) $\Rightarrow C = 1 - 1 = 0$ bits/use. (No info can pass.) - If $p = 0.1$ $\Rightarrow H_b(0.1) \approx 0.469 \Rightarrow C \approx 0.531$ bits/use. --- ### 3.3 Derivation C: Chain Rule for Entropy and Mutual Information Identity **Goal:** Prove $H(X,Y) = H(X) + H(Y \vert X)$ and use it to derive $I(X;Y) = H(X) + H(Y) - H(X,Y)$. **Step 1 — Expand Joint Entropy using the product rule of probability.** By the chain rule of probability: $P(x_i, y_j) = P(x_i) P(y_j \vert x_i)$. $$ \begin{aligned} H(X,Y) &= -\sum_{i,j} P(x_i, y_j) \log_2 P(x_i, y_j) \\ &= -\sum_{i,j} P(x_i) P(y_j \vert x_i) \log_2 \bigl[ P(x_i) P(y_j \vert x_i) \bigr] \\ &= -\sum_{i,j} P(x_i) P(y_j \vert x_i) \bigl[ \log_2 P(x_i) + \log_2 P(y_j \vert x_i) \bigr] \\ &= -\sum_i P(x_i) \log_2 P(x_i) \underbrace{\sum_j P(y_j \vert x_i)}_{= 1} \; - \sum_{i,j} P(x_i, y_j) \log_2 P(y_j \vert x_i) \\ &= H(X) + H(Y \vert X) \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 — Apply this to derive Mutual Information.** $$ \begin{aligned} I(X;Y) &= H(X) - H(X \vert Y) \quad \text{(Definition)} \\ \text{By chain rule:} \quad H(X) &= H(X \vert Y) + I(X;Y) \quad \Rightarrow \quad I(X;Y) = H(X) - H(X \vert Y) \\ \text{Similarly:} \quad H(Y) &= H(Y \vert X) + I(X;Y) \\ \text{Add the two:} \quad H(X) + H(Y) &= H(X \vert Y) + H(Y \vert X) + 2 I(X;Y) \\ \text{But } H(X,Y) &= H(X \vert Y) + H(Y) = H(Y \vert X) + H(X) \\ \Rightarrow I(X;Y) &= H(X) + H(Y) - H(X,Y) \quad \blacksquare \end{aligned} $$ *Logic:* The mutual information is the *overlap* of the two entropies — the region common to both in the Shannon entropy Venn diagram. --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block Diagram — Shannon's Communication System This is the **canonical block diagram** that anchors the entire module. ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Source S] --> B[Source Encoder
Compression] B --> C[Channel Encoder
Adds Redundancy for Error Protection] C --> D[Channel
Noisy Medium] D --> E[Channel Decoder
Error Detection Correction] E --> F[Source Decoder
Decompression] F --> G[Destination S_hat] H[Noise Source N] -.-> D classDef block fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0277bd,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef noise fill:#ffe1e1,stroke:#c62828,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 class A,B,C,D,E,F,G block class H noise ``` **Interpretation for KTU Exams:** - The **Source Encoder** is bounded by **Shannon's 1st Theorem** (entropy). - The **Channel Encoder** + **Channel Decoder** pair is bounded by **Shannon's 2nd Theorem** (capacity). - The **Noise Source** is what makes $H(Y \vert X) > 0$. --- ### 4.2 Functional Architecture — Information Theory Hierarchy Map ```mermaid flowchart TD L0[Information Theory
