**(b) [7 Marks]** Compare Run Length Encoding and Shannon–Fano coding with respect to (i) the type of redundancy exploited, (ii) suitability for natural images, (iii) computational complexity, and (iv) the prefix-free property.

#### Model Solution for (a)

**Step 1 — Identify the runs:**

| Run | Symbol | Length |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 0 | 3 |
| 2 | 1 | 3 |
| 3 | 0 | 3 |
| 4 | 1 | 3 |
| 5 | 0 | 3 |
| 6 | 1 | 3 |
| 7 | 0 | 1 |

So $N = 19$ symbols and $R = 7$ runs.

**Step 2 — Compressed output:**

`3,0 | 3,1 | 3,0 | 3,1 | 3,0 | 3,1 | 1,0`

**Step 3 — Compute lengths:**

$$
L_{\text{input}} = 19 \times 1 = 19 \text{ bits}
$$

$$
L_{\text{RLE}} = 7 \times (4 + 1) = 35 \text{ bits}
$$

**Step 4 — Compression ratio and space saving:**

$$
\text{CR} = \frac{19}{35} \approx 0.543
$$

$$
\text{Space Saving} = \left(1 - \frac{35}{19}\right) \times 100\% \approx -84.21\%
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Negative space saving!** This example demonstrates that RLE can **inflate** data when runs are very short. The student should explicitly **comment on the negative CR** and explain why RLE is unsuitable for alternating-bit patterns.

#### Model Solution for (b)

| Criterion | Run Length Encoding | Shannon–Fano Coding |
|---|---|---|
| (i) Redundancy type | **Structural** — long runs of identical symbols | **Statistical** — unequal symbol probabilities |
| (ii) Natural images | **Poor** — natural images rarely have long runs of identical pixel values | **Good** — exploits the non-uniform histogram of pixel intensities |
| (iii) Computational complexity | **Very low** — $O(N)$ single-pass scan | **Moderate** — $O(n \log n)$ for sorting + recursive splits |
| (iv) Prefix-free property | **Inherently** prefix-free because each pair is self-delimited | **Guaranteed** by construction (binary tree leaves) |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often claim RLE is "faster than Shannon–Fano so it is better for real-time applications." That is true **only** for the data classes where RLE is effective. For general-purpose compression, the computational cost of Shannon–Fano is justified by the much higher compression ratio.

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 — Alternative Choice]`
**(a) [7 Marks]** A discrete source emits six symbols with the following probabilities:

| Symbol | S1 | S2 | S3 | S4 | S5 | S6 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $p$ | 0.40 | 0.20 | 0.15 | 0.10 | 0.08 | 0.07 |

Construct the **Shannon–Fano code** and determine the codeword for each symbol.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Compute the average code length $\bar{L}$, the source entropy $H$, the coding efficiency $\eta$, and the redundancy $R$. Comment on whether this code is optimal.

#### Model Solution for (a)

**Step 1 — Sort in descending order** (already sorted): S1, S2, S3, S4, S5, S6.

**Step 2 — First split:**

Cumulative sums: 0.40, 0.60, 0.75, 0.85, 0.93, 1.00.

Check the difference at each split point:
- After S1: $\vert 0.40 - 0.60 \vert = 0.20$
- After S2: $\vert 0.60 - 0.40 \vert = 0.20$
- After S3: $\vert 0.75 - 0.25 \vert = 0.50$

The minimum difference is 0.20, achieved at either S1 or S2. We choose to split **after S1** (convention: split after the *first* occurrence of the minimum).

- $\mathcal{G}_1 = \{S1\}$ → bit **0** → code **0**
- $\mathcal{G}_2 = \{S2, S3, S4, S5, S6\}$ → bit **1**

**Step 3 — Recurse on $\mathcal{G}_2$:**

Cumulative: 0.20, 0.35, 0.45, 0.53, 0.60.

- After S2: $\vert 0.20 - 0.40 \vert = 0.20$
- After S2 + S3: $\vert 0.35 - 0.25 \vert = 0.10$ ✓ (minimum)
- After S2 + S3 + S4: $\vert 0.45 - 0.15 \vert = 0.30$

Split **after S2 + S3**:
- $\{S2, S3\}$ → bit **0** (prefix "10")
- $\{S4, S5, S6\}$ → bit **1** (prefix "11")

**Step 4 — Recurse on $\{S2, S3\}$:**

- S2 → bit **0** → code **100**
- S3 → bit **1** → code **101**

**Step 5 — Recurse on $\{S4, S5, S6\}$:**

Cumulative: 0.10, 0.18, 0.27.

- After S4: $\vert 0.10 - 0.17 \vert = 0.07$ ✓ (minimum)
- After S4 + S5: $\vert 0.18 - 0.09 \vert = 0.09$

Split **after S4**:
- S4 → bit **0** → code **110**
- $\{S5, S6\}$ → bit **1** (prefix "111")

**Step 6 — Recurse on $\{S5, S6\}$:**

- S5 → bit **0** → code **1110**
- S6 → bit **1** → code **1111**

**Final Code Table:**

| Symbol | $p$ | Codeword | Length $l$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| S1 | 0.40 | 0 | 1 |
| S2 | 0.20 | 100 | 3 |
| S3 | 0.15 | 101 | 3 |
| S4 | 0.10 | 110 | 3 |
| S5 | 0.08 | 1110 | 4 |
| S6 | 0.07 | 1111 | 4 |

#### Model Solution for (b)

**Step 1 — Average code length:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\bar{L} &= (0.40 \times 1) + (0.20 \times 3) + (0.15 \times 3) + (0.10 \times 3) + (0.08 \times 4) + (0.07 \times 4) \\
&= 0.40 + 0.60 + 0.45 + 0.30 + 0.32 + 0.28 \\
&= 2.35 \text{ bits/symbol}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Entropy:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
H &= -[0.40 \log_2 0.40 + 0.20 \log_2 0.20 + 0.15 \log_2 0.15 + 0.10 \log_2 0.10 \\
&\quad + 0.08 \log_2 0.08 + 0.07 \log_2 0.07] \\
&= -[0.40(-1.3219) + 0.20(-2.3219) + 0.15(-2.7370) + 0.10(-3.3219) \\
&\quad + 0.08(-3.6439) + 0.07(-3.8365)] \\
&= 0.5288 + 0.4644 + 0.4106 + 0.3322 + 0.2915 + 0.2686 \\
&= 2.2961 \text{ bits/symbol}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Efficiency and redundancy:**

$$
\eta = \frac{2.2961}{2.35} \times 100\% \approx 97.71\%
$$

$$
R = 1 - 0.9771 = 0.0229 \quad (\text{or } 2.29\%)
$$

**Step 4 — Optimality comment:**

The Shannon–Fano code gives $\bar{L} = 2.35$ and $\eta \approx 97.71\%$. Comparing with the noiseless-coding bound:

$$
H \le \bar{L} < H + 1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad 2.2961 \le 2.35 < 3.2961
$$

The bound is satisfied. However, because Shannon–Fano is not always optimal, the **Huffman code** for the same distribution might yield a marginally lower $\bar{L}$ (e.g., 2.30 bits/symbol) and hence a slightly higher efficiency. The student should note that for this particular distribution, the Shannon–Fano split happened to align well with the optimal Huffman merge sequence, so the difference is small.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Valuation Mistakes:**
> 1. **Forgetting the negative sign** in the entropy formula — the most common error. Always write $-p_i \log_2 p_i$, *not* $p_i \log_2 p_i$.
> 2. **Using $\ln$ instead of $\log_2$** — entropy in information theory is **always** in base 2 (bits).
> 3. **Rounding intermediate results too early** — keep 4 decimal places until the final answer.
> 4. **Omitting the unit "bits/symbol"** — examiners explicitly look for units.
> 5. **Not drawing the code tree** — KTU evaluators award 2 marks for a clearly labeled tree.

---

## ✅ Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- 🔹 **RLE** is a **structural** lossless coder that replaces runs of identical symbols with `(count, symbol)` pairs. It is best for **highly repetitive data** (binary fax, simple graphics).
- 🔹 **RLE compression ratio** is $\text{CR} = \frac{N \cdot b_s}{R \cdot (b_c + b_s)}$. A **negative space saving** means the data has been **expanded**, not compressed.
- 🔹 **Shannon–Fano** is a **statistical, top-down, prefix-free, variable-length** lossless coding technique.
- 🔹 The **algorithm steps** are: sort by probability → split into two near-equal probability groups → assign 0/1 → recurse.
- 🔹 **Entropy** $H = -\sum p_i \log_2 p_i$ is the **theoretical lower bound** for any lossless code.
- 🔹 **Average code length** $\bar{L} = \sum p_i \cdot l_i$ is the actual performance of the constructed code.
- 🔹 **Efficiency** $\eta = \frac{H}{\bar{L}} \times 100\%$ — closer to 100% means closer to optimal.
- 🔹 Shannon–Fano is **near-optimal** but **not guaranteed optimal**; **Huffman** is the strict optimum.
- 🔹 Always **verify the prefix-free property** before declaring the code complete.
- 🔹 In KTU exams, **draw the code tree** — it earns easy marks and helps you verify lengths.
- 🔹 Watch out for **logarithm base confusion** — entropy is always $\log_2$ in information theory.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Static Huffman Coding
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECNT742 - MULTIMEDIA COMPRESSION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Static Huffman Coding

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Static Huffman Coding** is a **lossless, entropy-based, variable-length source coding technique** introduced by **David A. Huffman (1952)** in his seminal paper *"A Method for the Construction of Minimum-Redundancy Codes"*. It constructs an optimal **prefix code tree** using a *static* (pre-computed) probability/frequency distribution of the source symbols. The word *static* implies that the symbol probabilities are either **known a priori** or computed from a **single pass** over the data, and the resulting codebook is **fixed for the entire compression session**.

The code tree is built by repeatedly merging the **two lowest-probability nodes** until a single root remains. The output codes are then read off the branches of the resulting binary tree, with one branch labelled `0` and the other `1`.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — A Smart Librarian's Filing System**
>
> Imagine a librarian in Kerala arranging a shelf of frequently borrowed books. The most popular book (say, *Harry Potter*) is kept at **eye level** so it can be pulled out in **1 second** (short codeword). A rarely read research thesis is buried in the **topmost corner**, taking **5 seconds** to fetch (long codeword). Crucially, the librarian never places a short book *underneath* a long one in such a way that the short book's name is a *prefix* of the long book's name — otherwise, on retrieval, confusion would arise. This is exactly the philosophy of Huffman coding: **frequent symbols → short codes, rare symbols → long codes, with the prefix-free property guaranteed by the binary tree structure**.

## 1.3 Key Terminology Snapshot

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Source Symbol** | The basic unit of data (e.g., character, pixel value, audio sample) |
| **Codeword** | The binary bit-string assigned to a symbol |
| **Code Length ($l_i$)** | Number of bits in the codeword of symbol $s_i$ |
| **Prefix Code** | No codeword is a prefix of another codeword |
| **Entropy $H(S)$** | Lower bound on the average code length in bits/symbol |
| **Average Code Length $L_{avg}$** | Expected bits per symbol after coding |
| **Codeword Redundancy** | $L_{avg} - H(S)$, the "wasted" bits per symbol |
| **Coding Efficiency $\eta$** | Ratio $H(S) / L_{avg}$, ideally approaches 1 |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Examiner Insight:** Examiners consistently award full marks when students explicitly state the **prefix-free property** and the **entropy bound** $H(S) \le L_{avg} < H(S) + 1$ for any uniquely decodable code.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Algorithmic Foundation — The Underlying "Why"

The Huffman algorithm is a **greedy algorithm**. It works because of the following two key optimal-substructure lemmas, which are *guaranteed* to hold for the optimal tree:

1. **Lemma 1 (Sibling Property):** In an optimal Huffman tree, the two symbols with the **lowest probabilities** are siblings (children of the same internal node) located at the **deepest level** of the tree.
2. **Lemma 2 (Reduction):** Replacing the two lowest-probability symbols by a single combined symbol with summed probability produces a sub-problem whose optimal solution, when expanded, yields the optimal solution for the original problem.

These two lemmas mathematically justify why repeatedly picking the two smallest frequencies and merging them is **provably optimal**.

## 2.2 Operational Steps (Standard KTU Algorithm Outline)

The procedural breakdown is enumerated below. Students must be able to reproduce these steps *verbatim* in the exam for full marks.

- **Step 1 — Frequency Analysis:** Compute the occurrence count (or probability $p_i$) of every distinct source symbol in the input alphabet $S = \{s_1, s_2, \ldots, s_n\}$.
- **Step 2 — Leaf Node Creation:** Construct $n$ leaf nodes, each carrying a symbol and its associated frequency.
- **Step 3 — Priority Queue Initialization:** Insert all leaf nodes into a min-priority queue (min-heap) keyed by frequency.
- **Step 4 — Iterative Merging (the loop):**
  - Extract the two nodes with the **smallest frequencies**, say $f_a$ and $f_b$.
  - Create a new internal node whose weight is $f_a + f_b$.
  - Make the two extracted nodes the **left** and **right** children of this new node.
  - Insert the new node back into the priority queue.
- **Step 5 — Termination:** Repeat Step 4 until exactly **one node** remains in the queue. This is the **root** of the Huffman tree.
- **Step 6 — Code Assignment:** Traverse the tree from the root to each leaf; assign `0` to every left-branch edge and `1` to every right-branch edge (or vice versa). The accumulated bits form the codeword.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **high-yield reference** for solving numerical problems. Memorize these expressions — they appear in **almost every KTU university exam paper** on this module.

| Formula Name | Mathematical Expression | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Source Entropy** | $H(S) = - \displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{n} p_i \log_2 p_i$ bits/symbol | Theoretical minimum bits/symbol |
| **Average Code Length** | $L_{avg} = \displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{n} p_i \cdot l_i$ bits/symbol | Actual bits used after Huffman coding |
| **Kraft–McMillan Inequality** | $\displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{n} 2^{-l_i} \le 1$ | Tests prefix-code existence (always $\le 1$ for valid Huffman) |
| **Entropy Bound** | $H(S) \le L_{avg} < H(S) + 1$ | Shannon's noiseless coding theorem |
| **Coding Efficiency** | $\eta = \dfrac{H(S)}{L_{avg}}$ | Should approach 1; closer to 1 is better |
| **Redundancy** | $R = 1 - \eta = \dfrac{L_{avg} - H(S)}{L_{avg}}$ | Bits wasted per symbol |
| **Compression Ratio** | $C_R = \dfrac{\text{Original size}}{\text{Compressed size}}$ | Larger is better; $> 1$ means compression achieved |
| **Total Bits (Encoded)** | $B_{enc} = \displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{n} f_i \cdot l_i$ | Used for average length and CR computation |
| **Variance of Code Length** | $\sigma^2 = \displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{n} p_i (l_i - L_{avg})^2$ | Indicates spread of code lengths (not always required) |

## 2.4 Properties of the Huffman Code

- **Optimality:** For a given probability distribution, the Huffman code produces a code whose average length is the minimum among all *uniquely decodable* codes that are constructed symbol-by-symbol.
- **Prefix-Free (Instantaneous):** The code is uniquely decodable without lookahead; the decoder stops as soon as a leaf is reached.
- **Variable Length:** More probable symbols get shorter codewords; less probable ones get longer.
- **Non-Uniqueness:** Multiple optimal Huffman trees may exist when several symbols share the *same* probability (tie-breaking by the algorithm affects the tree shape, not $L_{avg}$).
- **Static Nature:** The probability distribution is computed **once** and the codebook is **frozen** — hence, it does not adapt to local changes in the data stream (this limitation is solved by *Adaptive Huffman*).
- **Bounded Performance:** The average length always lies within **one bit** of the entropy: $H(S) \le L_{avg} < H(S) + 1$.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> Huffman coding is the workhorse behind major multimedia standards:
> - **JPEG** (image compression): used after DCT and quantization to encode DC/AC coefficients.
> - **MPEG-1/2/4** (video compression): used for variable-length coding of motion vectors and DCT blocks.
> - **MP3** (audio compression): scale-factor bands and Huffman-coded quantized samples.
> - **DEFLATE algorithm** (ZIP, PNG, gzip): combines LZ77 with Huffman.
> - **Fax machines (T.4/T.6 standards)** and early **BMP/TIFF** image formats.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Canonical Worked Example (KTU Board Style)

Consider a discrete source $S$ with six symbols and the following frequency table obtained from a sample message of **100 symbols total**.

| Symbol | $s_1$ | $s_2$ | $s_3$ | $s_4$ | $s_5$ | $s_6$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Name** | A | B | C | D | E | F |
| **Frequency $f_i$** | 5 | 9 | 12 | 13 | 16 | 45 |
| **Probability $p_i = f_i / 100$** | 0.05 | 0.09 | 0.12 | 0.13 | 0.16 | 0.45 |

> **Total symbols** $N = 5 + 9 + 12 + 13 + 16 + 45 = 100$.

### 3.1.1 Tree Construction (Stage-by-Stage)

We follow the standard KTU step convention: at every stage, the two smallest weights are merged.

**Stage 1 — Initial list** (sorted ascending):

$$w_1^{(1)} = 5 \ (\text{A}), \quad w_2^{(1)} = 9 \ (\text{B}), \quad w_3^{(1)} = 12 \ (\text{C}), \quad w_4^{(1)} = 13 \ (\text{D}), \quad w_5^{(1)} = 16 \ (\text{E}), \quad w_6^{(1)} = 45 \ (\text{F})$$

**Stage 2 — Merge A(5) and B(9)**:

$$w_{AB} = 5 + 9 = 14$$

Updated list: {AB(14), C(12), D(13), E(16), F(45)} — re-sorted as {C(12), D(13), AB(14), E(16), F(45)}.

**Stage 3 — Merge C(12) and D(13)**:

$$w_{CD} = 12 + 13 = 25$$

Updated list: {AB(14), E(16), CD(25), F(45)} — re-sorted as {AB(14), E(16), CD(25), F(45)}.

**Stage 4 — Merge AB(14) and E(16)**:

$$w_{ABE} = 14 + 16 = 30$$

Updated list: {CD(25), ABE(30), F(45)} — already sorted.

**Stage 5 — Merge CD(25) and ABE(30)**:

$$w_{CDE\_ABE} = 25 + 30 = 55$$

Updated list: {F(45), CDE_ABE(55)}.

**Stage 6 — Merge F(45) and CDE_ABE(55) → ROOT**:

$$w_{root} = 45 + 55 = 100 \quad \checkmark \ (\text{matches total } N)$$

### 3.1.2 Codeword Assignment

Following the convention `0 = left branch`, `1 = right branch`, the codes extracted from the tree are:

| Symbol | Codeword | Length $l_i$ |
|---|---|---|
| F | `0` | 1 |
| C | `100` | 3 |
| D | `101` | 3 |
| A | `1100` | 4 |
| B | `1101` | 4 |
| E | `111` | 3 |

> [!NOTE]
> The sum of probabilities weighted by $2^{-l_i}$ must satisfy the Kraft inequality. Verification:
> $\displaystyle\sum 2^{-l_i} = 2^{-1} + 2^{-3} + 2^{-3} + 2^{-4} + 2^{-4} + 2^{-3} = 0.5 + 0.125 + 0.125 + 0.0625 + 0.0625 + 0.125 = 1.0$ — exactly satisfied, indicating a **complete, optimal** tree.

### 3.1.3 Derivation of Average Code Length $L_{avg}$

Using the defining formula:

$$L_{avg} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} p_i \cdot l_i$$

Substituting each term:

$$L_{avg} = (0.05)(4) + (0.09)(4) + (0.12)(3) + (0.13)(3) + (0.16)(3) + (0.45)(1)$$

Step-by-step evaluation:

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{avg} &= 0.20 + 0.36 + 0.36 + 0.39 + 0.48 + 0.45 \\
&= (0.20 + 0.36) + (0.36 + 0.39) + (0.48 + 0.45) \\
&= 0.56 + 0.75 + 0.93 \\
&= 2.24 \text{ bits/symbol}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Equivalent integer-form check** (using $B_{enc} = \sum f_i l_i$):

$$B_{enc} = 5(4) + 9(4) + 12(3) + 13(3) + 16(3) + 45(1) = 20 + 36 + 36 + 39 + 48 + 45 = 224 \text{ bits}$$

$$L_{avg} = \frac{B_{enc}}{N} = \frac{224}{100} = 2.24 \text{ bits/symbol} \quad \checkmark$$

### 3.1.4 Derivation of Source Entropy $H(S)$

Using the defining formula $H(S) = -\sum p_i \log_2 p_i$, we compute each term separately.

**Term 1 — $p_A = 0.05$**:

$$-p_A \log_2 p_A = -(0.05)\log_2(0.05) = (0.05)(4.3219) = 0.21610 \text{ bits}$$

**Term 2 — $p_B = 0.09$**:

$$-p_B \log_2 p_B = -(0.09)\log_2(0.09) = (0.09)(3.4739) = 0.31265 \text{ bits}$$

**Term 3 — $p_C = 0.12$**:

$$-p_C \log_2 p_C = -(0.12)\log_2(0.12) = (0.12)(3.0589) = 0.36707 \text{ bits}$$

**Term 4 — $p_D = 0.13$**:

$$-p_D \log_2 p_D = -(0.13)\log_2(0.13) = (0.13)(2.9434) = 0.38264 \text{ bits}$$

**Term 5 — $p_E = 0.16$**:

$$-p_E \log_2 p_E = -(0.16)\log_2(0.16) = (0.16)(2.6439) = 0.42302 \text{ bits}$$

**Term 6 — $p_F = 0.45$**:

$$-p_F \log_2 p_F = -(0.45)\log_2(0.45) = (0.45)(1.1520) = 0.51840 \text{ bits}$$

**Summing all six terms:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
H(S) &= 0.21610 + 0.31265 + 0.36707 + 0.38264 + 0.42302 + 0.51840 \\
&= (0.21610 + 0.31265) + (0.36707 + 0.38264) + (0.42302 + 0.51840) \\
&= 0.52875 + 0.74971 + 0.94142 \\
&= 2.21988 \text{ bits/symbol}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Rounded for KTU board use:** $H(S) \approx 2.220$ bits/symbol.

### 3.1.5 Derivation of Coding Efficiency $\eta$ and Redundancy $R$

**Efficiency:**

$$\eta = \frac{H(S)}{L_{avg}} = \frac{2.21988}{2.24} = 0.99102$$

Expressed as a percentage: $\eta \approx 99.10\%$.

**Redundancy (fractional):**

$$R = 1 - \eta = 1 - 0.99102 = 0.00898 \approx 0.90\%$$

**Redundancy (absolute, in bits/symbol):**

$$R_{abs} = L_{avg} - H(S) = 2.24 - 2.21988 = 0.02012 \text{ bits/symbol}$$

### 3.1.6 Verification of Shannon's Bound

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For any uniquely decodable code, Shannon's noiseless coding theorem states that $H(S) \le L_{avg} < H(S) + 1$.

**Lower bound check:**

$$H(S) = 2.220 \le L_{avg} = 2.24 \quad \checkmark$$

**Upper bound check:**

$$L_{avg} = 2.24 < H(S) + 1 = 3.220 \quad \checkmark$$

The Huffman code satisfies the theorem and is therefore *near-optimal* — within **2.01%** of the theoretical entropy limit.

### 3.1.7 Compression Ratio Computation

Assuming **8 bits/symbol** as the original fixed-length encoding:

$$B_{orig} = 100 \times 8 = 800 \text{ bits}$$

$$C_R = \frac{B_{orig}}{B_{enc}} = \frac{800}{224} \approx 3.57$$

**Percentage size reduction:** $(1 - 224/800) \times 100\% = 72\%$.

## 3.2 Decoding Process — Bit-by-Bit Tree Traversal

The decoder uses the **same pre-shared tree** (or, in the case of static Huffman, a *transmitted* codebook header). For the encoded bitstream `0 1100 100 111 101 ...`, the decoder proceeds as follows:

- Read first bit `0` → move to left child of root → **leaf reached → emit symbol F**.
- Read next bit `1` → move right → next bit `1` → move right → next bit `0` → move left → next bit `0` → **leaf A**.
- Continue similarly for the remaining bits.

The decoder **never needs to look ahead**, which is the defining property of a *prefix* (or *instantaneous*) code.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Algorithm Flow Diagram — Huffman Tree Construction

The following flowchart captures the procedural state transitions of the Huffman coding algorithm from input to encoded bitstream.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A1([START]) --> A2[Read input symbols]
    A2 --> A3[Compute frequency fi for each symbol]
    A3 --> A4[Create n leaf nodes with weight fi]
    A4 --> A5[Insert all nodes into min priority queue]
    A5 --> A6{Queue size greater than 1}
    A6 -->|YES| A7[Extract two nodes with minimum weights]
    A7 --> A8[Create new internal node with weight wA plus wB]
    A8 --> A9[Make extracted nodes left and right children of new node]
    A9 --> A10[Insert new internal node into priority queue]
    A10 --> A6
    A6 -->|NO| A11[Last remaining node is ROOT of Huffman tree]
    A11 --> A12[Assign 0 to left edges and 1 to right edges]
    A12 --> A13[Traverse root to leaf for each symbol to extract codewords]
    A13 --> A14[Build codebook table mapping symbol to codeword]
    A14 --> A15[Encode source message using codebook]
    A15 --> A16[Transmit codebook plus encoded bitstream]
    A16 --> A17([END])
```

## 4.2 Final Huffman Tree for the Worked Example

The following tree visualizes the optimal code tree constructed in Section 3. Each node displays its aggregated weight (frequency sum) in the format `WEIGHT`. Edge labels `0` and `1` denote the bit assigned to that branch.

```mermaid
graph TD
    R1((100))
    N1((F : 45))
    N2((55))
    N3((25))
    N4((30))
    N5((C : 12))
    N6((D : 13))
    N7((14))
    N8((E : 16))
    N9((A : 5))
    N10((B : 9))
    R1 --- R1a
    R1a((F : 45))
    R1 --- R1b
    R1b((55))
    R1a --- f1((F : 45))
    R1b --- R2a
    R2a((25))
    R1b --- R2b
    R2b((30))
    R2a --- c1((C : 12))
    R2a --- d1((D : 13))
    R2b --- R3a
    R3a((14))
    R2b --- e1((E : 16))
    R3a --- a1((A : 5))
    R3a --- b1((B : 9))
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the tree:** Starting from the root `R1` (weight 100), traversing the **left** edge leads to leaf `F` (weight 45) — codeword `0`. Traversing the **right** edge to internal node `R1b` (weight 55) and continuing right leads to `E` (weight 16) — codeword `111`. The full mapping table is given in Section 3.1.2.

## 4.3 Static vs. Adaptive (Dynamic) Huffman — Block Architecture

The block diagram below highlights where the *static* Huffman differs architecturally from its *adaptive* cousin. The static variant pre-computes a **frequency table** in a dedicated first pass.

```mermaid
graph LR
    SRC[Source Symbol Stream] --> P1[Pass 1: Frequency Counter]
    P1 --> FT[Frequency Table]
    FT --> CB[Codebook Builder]
    CB --> TREE[Huffman Tree]
    TREE --> CB2[Static Codebook]
    CB2 --> ENC[Encoder]
    SRC --> ENC
    ENC --> BIT[Encoded Bitstream]
    CB2 --> TX[Transmitted Alongside Bitstream]
    TX --> DEC[Decoder with Pre-shared Codebook]
    BIT --> DEC
    DEC --> OUT[Decoded Symbol Stream]
```

> **Key Architectural Difference:** In *adaptive* Huffman, the dashed "Pass 1" block is removed, and the frequency table is updated on the fly as symbols are encoded. This trades extra decoder complexity for the elimination of codebook transmission overhead.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model]**
**Define Huffman coding. State and justify the two important properties that the algorithm guarantees.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Huffman coding is a lossless, entropy-based compression algorithm that constructs an optimal **variable-length, prefix-free binary code** by repeatedly merging the two lowest-probability symbols in a bottom-up tree-building process.

**Property 1 — Optimality (1 mark):** Among all uniquely decodable (prefix) codes, the Huffman code yields the **minimum average code length** for a given symbol probability distribution. This follows from the greedy choice and optimal substructure properties.

**Property 2 — Prefix-Free (1 mark):** No codeword is a prefix of any other codeword. This is because every codeword corresponds to a leaf in the binary tree, and no leaf can lie on the internal-node-to-leaf path of another leaf. This guarantees **instantaneous decoding** without lookahead.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model]**
**Compare static and adaptive (dynamic) Huffman coding along three dimensions.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Dimension | Static Huffman | Adaptive (Dynamic) Huffman |
|---|---|---|
| **Codebook Generation** | Built **once** from a pre-computed frequency distribution (two-pass) | Built and **updated incrementally** during encoding (one-pass) |
| **Transmission Overhead** | Codebook (or tree) must be **transmitted** alongside the bitstream | No codebook transmission needed — decoder builds identical tree from observed symbols |
| **Adaptation to Local Statistics** | **Cannot** adapt; one codebook for entire stream | **Adapts** to symbol-frequency drift in the data |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfall Alert**
> - **Do NOT** confuse the *average code length* $L_{avg}$ with the *entropy* $H(S)$. They are different quantities: $H(S)$ is the theoretical lower bound; $L_{avg}$ is the *actual* achieved value.
> - **Do NOT** forget to write the **units** ("bits/symbol" or "bits") in every numerical answer. Examiners often deduct **0.5 mark per missing unit**.
> - When two symbols share the **same frequency**, always state your tie-breaking rule explicitly (e.g., "the alphabetically/smaller-frequency-first node is placed on the left") to avoid ambiguity.
> - **Coding efficiency** must be expressed as a **decimal *and* a percentage** for full credit.

---

### Question A — 14 Marks **[KTU University Exam — June 2023, Adapted]**

**Consider a source producing six symbols with the following probabilities: $p(A)=0.05$, $p(B)=0.09$, $p(C)=0.12$, $p(D)=0.13$, $p(E)=0.16$, $p(F)=0.45$.**

#### Part (a) — Construct the Huffman code and tabulate the codewords with their lengths. **\[7 Marks\]**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — List symbols with probabilities in descending order:** F(0.45), E(0.16), D(0.13), C(0.12), B(0.09), A(0.05).

**Step 2 — Iterative merging (5 stages):**

- **Stage 1:** Merge A(0.05) and B(0.09) → node AB(0.14). List: {F(0.45), E(0.16), D(0.13), C(0.12), AB(0.14)}.
- **Stage 2:** Merge C(0.12) and D(0.13) → node CD(0.25). List: {F(0.45), E(0.16), AB(0.14), CD(0.25)}.
- **Stage 3:** Merge AB(0.14) and E(0.16) → node ABE(0.30). List: {F(0.45), CD(0.25), ABE(0.30)}.
- **Stage 4:** Merge CD(0.25) and ABE(0.30) → node CDE_ABE(0.55). List: {F(0.45), CDE_ABE(0.55)}.
- **Stage 5:** Merge F(0.45) and CDE_ABE(0.55) → ROOT(1.00). ✓

**Step 3 — Codeword assignment (`0` = left, `1` = right):**

| Symbol | Probability | Codeword | Length $l_i$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| F | 0.45 | `0` | 1 |
| C | 0.12 | `100` | 3 |
| D | 0.13 | `101` | 3 |
| A | 0.05 | `1100` | 4 |
| B | 0.09 | `1101` | 4 |
| E | 0.16 | `111` | 3 |

**Verification (Kraft inequality — 0.5 mark):**

$$\sum 2^{-l_i} = 2^{-1} + 2^{-3} + 2^{-3} + 2^{-4} + 2^{-4} + 2^{-3} = 1.0 \le 1 \quad \checkmark$$

**Marking note:** Full 7 marks for correctly building all 5 stages (5 marks), tabulating the codewords (1.5 marks), and verifying Kraft (0.5 mark).

#### Part (b) — Calculate entropy, average code length, coding efficiency, and redundancy. **\[7 Marks\]**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Entropy $H(S)$ (2 marks):**

$$H(S) = -\sum p_i \log_2 p_i = 2.220 \text{ bits/symbol}$$

**Step 2 — Average code length $L_{avg}$ (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{avg} &= (0.45)(1) + (0.12)(3) + (0.13)(3) + (0.05)(4) + (0.09)(4) + (0.16)(3) \\
&= 0.45 + 0.36 + 0.39 + 0.20 + 0.36 + 0.48 \\
&= 2.24 \text{ bits/symbol}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Coding efficiency $\eta$ (1.5 marks):**

$$\eta = \frac{H(S)}{L_{avg}} = \frac{2.220}{2.24} = 0.9911 = 99.11\%$$

**Step 4 — Redundancy $R$ (1.5 marks):**

$$R = 1 - \eta = 1 - 0.9911 = 0.0089 = 0.89\%$$

Or in absolute terms: $R_{abs} = L_{avg} - H(S) = 0.020$ bits/symbol.

**Conclusion:** The Huffman code is **near-optimal**, achieving over **99% efficiency** and only **0.02 bits/symbol** above the entropy limit.

---

### Question B — 14 Marks **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Adapted]**

**A discrete memoryless source emits five symbols with frequencies: A: 20, B: 15, C: 10, D: 5, E: 50 (Total = 100).**

#### Part (a) — Construct the Huffman code, find $L_{avg}$, and verify Shannon's noiseless coding theorem. **\[7 Marks\]**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Convert frequencies to probabilities:** $p(A)=0.20$, $p(B)=0.15$, $p(C)=0.10$, $p(D)=0.05$, $p(E)=0.50$.

**Step 2 — Huffman construction (4 stages):**

- **Stage 1:** Merge D(0.05) and C(0.10) → DC(0.15). List: {E(0.50), B(0.15), DC(0.15), A(0.20)}.
- **Stage 2:** Merge B(0.15) and DC(0.15) → BDC(0.30). List: {E(0.50), A(0.20), BDC(0.30)}.
- **Stage 3:** Merge A(0.20) and BDC(0.30) → ABDC(0.50). List: {E(0.50), ABDC(0.50)}.
- **Stage 4:** Merge E(0.50) and ABDC(0.50) → ROOT(1.00). ✓

**Step 3 — Codewords:**

| Symbol | Codeword | Length $l_i$ |
|---|---|---|
| E | `0` | 1 |
| A | `10` | 2 |
| B | `110` | 3 |
| D | `1110` | 4 |
| C | `1111` | 4 |

**Step 4 — Average code length (2 marks):**

$$L_{avg} = (0.20)(2) + (0.15)(3) + (0.10)(4) + (0.05)(4) + (0.50)(1)$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{avg} &= 0.40 + 0.45 + 0.40 + 0.20 + 0.50 = 1.95 \text{ bits/symbol}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Entropy (1.5 marks):**

$$H(S) = -\sum p_i \log_2 p_i$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
H(S) &= -(0.20)\log_2(0.20) - (0.15)\log_2(0.15) - (0.10)\log_2(0.10) \\
&\quad - (0.05)\log_2(0.05) - (0.50)\log_2(0.50) \\
&= (0.20)(2.3219) + (0.15)(2.7370) + (0.10)(3.3219) \\
&\quad + (0.05)(4.3219) + (0.50)(1.0000) \\
&= 0.4644 + 0.4105 + 0.3322 + 0.2161 + 0.5000 \\
&= 1.9232 \text{ bits/symbol}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Verify Shannon's bound (0.5 mark):**

$$H(S) = 1.923 \le L_{avg} = 1.95 < H(S) + 1 = 2.923 \quad \checkmark$$

The Huffman code satisfies Shannon's noiseless coding theorem.

#### Part (b) — Explain the prefix-property of Huffman codes with a counter-example, and discuss why Huffman codes are *optimal* among all prefix codes. **\[7 Marks\]**

**Model Solution:**

**Prefix Property — Definition (1.5 marks):**
A code is called a *prefix code* (or *instantaneous* code) if **no codeword in the codebook is a prefix of any other codeword**. Formally: for any two distinct codewords $c_i$ and $c_j$, $c_i$ is *not* a prefix of $c_j$. This property is guaranteed in Huffman codes because every codeword corresponds to a unique **leaf** of the Huffman tree, and by the very nature of a binary tree, **no leaf can lie on the path from the root to another leaf**.

**Counter-example — Why non-prefix codes fail (2 marks):**
Consider the codebook $C_1 = \{0, 01, 011\}$ for symbols {A, B, C}. Here `0` is a prefix of `01` (which is a prefix of `011`). The bitstream `011` is **ambiguous** — it could be decoded as the single symbol C, or as the sequence A+B+A. Hence, this codebook is **not uniquely decodable** and cannot be used directly without out-of-band delimiters.

In contrast, the Huffman codebook $C_2 = \{0, 10, 110, 1110, 1111\}$ (from Part a) has **no codeword as a prefix of another**, allowing the decoder to terminate at the first leaf hit — **instantaneous decoding**.

**Optimality Argument (3.5 marks):**
The Huffman code is *optimal* in the sense of minimizing the **average code length** $L_{avg} = \sum p_i l_i$ among all prefix codes for a given probability distribution. The proof rests on two lemmas:

1. **Greedy Choice Lemma (1 mark):** In any optimal prefix-code tree, the two symbols with the *lowest* probabilities must be siblings at the *deepest* level. If they were not, swapping them with the deepest siblings would strictly reduce $L_{avg}$ — contradicting optimality.

2. **Optimal Substructure Lemma (1.5 marks):** After merging the two lowest-probability symbols $s_a$ and $s_b$ into a composite symbol $s_{ab}$ with $p_{ab} = p_a + p_b$, the optimal code for the *reduced* problem (of size $n-1$) can be extended back to yield the optimal code for the original problem. The two symbols $s_a$ and $s_b$ receive codewords that are identical except in the final bit (`0` and `1`).

3. **Conclusion (1 mark):** Since the algorithm performs this greedy merge at every stage, by induction on $n$, the resulting tree has the **minimum possible** $L_{avg}$ over all prefix-code trees. Hence, Huffman codes are *optimal* among prefix codes.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Print This Before the Exam!**

- ☐ **Definition:** Huffman coding is a **lossless, entropy-based, variable-length, prefix-free** binary coding scheme.
- ☐ **Static** means the codebook is built **once** from a pre-computed frequency table and used for the entire message.
- ☐ **Algorithm** is a **bottom-up greedy** approach — always merge the **two smallest** weighted nodes.
- ☐ **Termination condition** is a single node with weight equal to the total number (or 1.0 in probability form).
- ☐ **Codeword length $l_i$** = number of edges from root to the leaf representing symbol $s_i$.
- ☐ **Entropy formula:** $H(S) = -\sum p_i \log_2 p_i$ — the **theoretical minimum** average bits per symbol.
- ☐ **Average length formula:** $L_{avg} = \sum p_i \cdot l_i = \frac{1}{N} \sum f_i \cdot l_i$.
- ☐ **Efficiency:** $\eta = H(S) / L_{avg}$ — must be between 0 and 1; **closer to 1 is better**.
- ☐ **Redundancy:** $R = 1 - \eta$ (fractional) or $R_{abs} = L_{avg} - H(S)$ (bits/symbol).
- ☐ **Shannon's bound:** $H(S) \le L_{avg} < H(S) + 1$ — always verify for full credit.
- ☐ **Kraft inequality:** $\sum 2^{-l_i} \le 1$ — must hold for any prefix code; equals 1 for a complete tree.
- ☐ **Prefix-free property:** No codeword is a prefix of another → **instantaneous decoding**, no lookahead required.
- ☐ **Tie-breaking rule** must be stated explicitly when multiple nodes share equal weights.
- ☐ **Always include units** ("bits/symbol", "bits", "%") in your final answers.
- ☐ **Decoding process** uses the **same pre-shared tree** and reads bits left-to-right until a leaf is reached.
- ☐ **Huffman limitation:** Two-pass (or pre-shared distribution) is required — *adaptive* Huffman overcomes this at the cost of higher decoder complexity.
- ☐ **Standard applications:** JPEG, MPEG, MP3, ZIP/PNG (DEFLATE), fax T.4/T.6.
- ☐ **Memory tip:** "Huffman = **H**igh-frequency gets **H**ealthy short codes, low-frequency gets **L**ong ones."
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Binary and Non-Binary Huffman Coding - Arithmetic Coding
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECNT742 - MULTIMEDIA COMPRESSION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Binary and Non-Binary Huffman Coding - Arithmetic Coding

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Binary Huffman Coding

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Binary Huffman Coding** is an optimal, bottom-up, greedy algorithm for constructing a **minimum-redundancy prefix code** over a discrete memoryless source, where every codeword is assigned from a **2-symbol (binary) alphabet** {0, 1}. It produces a full binary tree whose leaves are the source symbols, and the depth of each leaf equals the length of its codeword.

**Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
Given a source alphabet $\mathcal{S} = \{s_1, s_2, \ldots, s_n\}$ with probability mass function $P = \{p_1, p_2, \ldots, p_n\}$, the Binary Huffman procedure assigns codeword lengths $\ell_i \in \mathbb{Z}^+$ such that:

- The Kraft inequality $\sum_{i=1}^{n} 2^{-\ell_i} \leq 1$ is satisfied.
- The expected code length $\bar{L} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} p_i \ell_i$ is **minimized**.
- The code is a **prefix-free** (instantaneous) code: no codeword is a prefix of any other.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — The "Tournament Bracket"**
> Imagine a sports league where the weakest teams (lowest probabilities) keep getting paired and "merged" first, while the strongest team stays at the top of the bracket. Each merge costs one extra "round" (one extra bit). Since weak symbols occur rarely, paying an extra bit for them is cheap; since the strong symbol stays shallow, it gets a short code. The bracket is the **Huffman tree** — leaves are the symbols, and the path from root to leaf spells the binary codeword.

### 1.2 Non-Binary (D-ary) Huffman Coding

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Non-Binary Huffman Coding** generalizes the procedure to a **D-ary alphabet** $\{0, 1, \ldots, D-1\}$ (e.g., ternary for $D=3$, quaternary for $D=4$). The resulting tree is a **full D-ary tree** in which every internal node has exactly $D$ children, and codeword lengths are drawn from a D-ary alphabet.

**Key Difference vs Binary Case:**
A full D-ary tree of height $\ell$ has exactly $D^{\ell}$ leaves. To keep the tree "full" at every internal level, the algorithm must ensure that the number of nodes to be merged satisfies the constraint that there are enough leaves to form complete groups of $D$.

### 1.3 Arithmetic Coding

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Arithmetic Coding** is an **entropy-encoding paradigm** that abandons the one-symbol-to-one-codeword mapping entirely. The entire source message (a string of symbols) is encoded into a **single real number** in the half-open interval $[0, 1)$, whose expected bit length approaches the **Shannon entropy** $H(S)$ of the source — even when individual symbol probabilities are irrational.

**Formal Definition:**
For a sequence $x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_N$ drawn from alphabet $\mathcal{S}$ with known cumulative distribution function $F$, the encoder maintains an interval $[low, high) \subset [0, 1)$ that is progressively narrowed by successive symbol probabilities. Any number inside the final interval uniquely identifies the message.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — The "Number Line Reservation System"**
> Imagine a 1-meter ruler representing the entire message. The whole ruler $[0, 1)$ is initially yours. Each symbol **reserves** a sub-segment proportional to its probability: a frequent symbol gets a large slice, a rare symbol gets a tiny one. After $N$ symbols, you keep narrowing down to a microscopic sliver. The final sliver — described by any single number inside it — represents your *entire* message. The shorter (more probable) the message, the fewer bits you need to specify that sliver precisely.

### 1.4 The Unifying Theme

All three techniques are **statistical / entropy coders**. They exploit the **unequal symbol probabilities** of natural data (text, images, audio) to compress below the naïve $\lceil \log_2 n \rceil$ bits/symbol bound. They differ fundamentally in *granularity*:

| Technique | Granularity | Output |
|-----------|-------------|--------|
| Binary Huffman | One symbol → one codeword | Bit-stream of concatenated codewords |
| Non-Binary Huffman | One symbol → one codeword | Stream of D-ary digits |
| Arithmetic | Whole message → one number | Single fractional number in $[0,1)$ |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** This topic directly supports *Course Outcome CO2* — "Apply entropy coding techniques for lossless compression of multimedia data." Examiner questions frequently combine construction of a Huffman tree with an arithmetic-coding interval problem on the **same symbol set** to test conceptual depth.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Binary Huffman — Operational Algorithm

The Huffman algorithm is a **bottom-up greedy procedure**. The core "Why" of its optimality is the *Sibling Property*: in any optimal tree, the two longest codewords correspond to the two **least probable** symbols, and they are siblings (share the same parent).

**Step-by-Step Logic:**

1. **Initialize:** Create a leaf node for every source symbol $s_i$, tagged with its probability $p_i$. Insert all leaves into a **min-priority queue** $\mathcal{Q}$ ordered by probability.
2. **Repeat** until $\mathcal{Q}$ contains exactly **one** root node:
   - **Extract-Min twice:** Remove the two nodes $u, v$ with the **smallest probabilities** from $\mathcal{Q}$.
   - **Merge:** Create a new internal node $w$ whose probability is $p_w = p_u + p_v$. Set $u$ and $v$ as the **left and right children** of $w$.
   - **Encode edges:** Conventionally, edge to left child $\rightarrow$ bit `0`, edge to right child $\rightarrow$ bit `1` (the reverse is also valid, only average length matters).
   - **Insert-Min:** Insert $w$ back into $\mathcal{Q}$.
3. **Read codewords:** Traverse the tree from root to each leaf; the sequence of edge labels is the codeword for that symbol.

**Why the Greedy Choice is Safe:**
After step (b), the two least-probable symbols occupy the **deepest leaves** of any optimal tree (exchange argument). Merging them costs *one extra bit* to both, which is the minimum possible increase in expected length.

### 2.2 Non-Binary (D-ary) Huffman — The "Padding" Trick

The naïve extension fails because a Huffman-style merge reduces the queue size by $D-1$ per iteration, not by 1. After $(n-1)$ binary merges we get 1 node; with $D$-ary merges, after $k$ merges we get $n - k(D-1)$ nodes. To reach exactly 1 node we need $k = (n-1)/(D-1)$ merges — which is an integer only when $n \equiv 1 \pmod{D-1}$.

**Solution — Dummy Symbols:**
Add a sufficient number of **dummy symbols** with **zero probability** $p_d = 0$ so that:

$$
(n + n_d - 1) \equiv 0 \pmod{D-1}
$$

The minimum number of dummies required is:

$$
n_d = (D-1) - \left[(n-1) \bmod (D-1)\right]
$$

Equivalently, $n_d$ is the smallest non-negative integer such that $(n + n_d - 1)$ is divisible by $(D-1)$. Dummies are treated as ordinary symbols during merging but contribute nothing to $\bar{L}$ because $p_d = 0$. After tree construction, **discard** the dummies.

### 2.3 Arithmetic Coding — Interval Narrowing

Let the alphabet be $\mathcal{S} = \{s_1, s_2, \ldots, s_n\}$ with probabilities $p_i$ and cumulative ranges:

$$
F_0 = 0, \qquad F_i = \sum_{j=1}^{i} p_j
$$

For each new symbol $s_k$, the current interval $[low, high)$ is updated as:

$$
high' = low + (high - low) \cdot F_k
$$

$$
low' = low + (high - low) \cdot F_{k-1}
$$

The **width** of the interval shrinks by factor $p_{k}$ each step, ending with width $W = \prod_{i=1}^{N} p_{x_i}$.

The number of bits required to specify the final interval is $\lceil -\log_2 W \rceil = \lceil \sum_i -\log_2 p_{x_i} \rceil$, which converges to $N \cdot H(S)$ for long messages.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas below are examinable under PECNT742 Module 2.**

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Units / Range |
|---|-------------------|------------|---------------|
| 1 | Shannon Entropy of source | $H(S) = -\sum_{i=1}^{n} p_i \log_2 p_i$ | bits / symbol |
| 2 | Expected code length | $\bar{L} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} p_i \ell_i$ | bits / symbol |
| 3 | Kraft inequality (binary) | $\sum_{i=1}^{n} 2^{-\ell_i} \leq 1$ | dimensionless |
| 4 | Kraft inequality (D-ary) | $\sum_{i=1}^{n} D^{-\ell_i} \leq 1$ | dimensionless |
| 5 | Noiseless coding theorem | $H(S) \leq \bar{L} < H(S) + 1$ | bits / symbol |
| 6 | Huffman code length bound | $\ell_{\max} \leq n - 1$ | bits |
| 7 | D-ary dummy padding count | $n_d = (D-1) - [(n-1) \bmod (D-1)]$ | integer $\geq 0$ |
| 8 | Huffman coding efficiency | $\eta = \dfrac{H(S)}{\bar{L}} \times 100\%$ | percent |
| 9 | Huffman redundancy | $R = \bar{L} - H(S)$ | bits / symbol |
| 10 | Arithmetic interval width | $W_N = \prod_{i=1}^{N} p_{x_i}$ | dimensionless |
| 11 | Arithmetic bits needed | $B = \lceil -\log_2 W_N \rceil$ | bits |
| 12 | Arithmetic high update | $high \leftarrow low + (high-low)\cdot F_k$ | real in $[0,1)$ |
| 13 | Arithmetic low update | $low \leftarrow low + (high-low)\cdot F_{k-1}$ | real in $[0,1)$ |
| 14 | Variance of code length | $\sigma^2 = \sum p_i (\ell_i - \bar{L})^2$ | bits$^2$ |
| 15 | Ternary $(D=3)$ dummy count | $n_d = 2 - [(n-1) \bmod 2]$ | 0 if $n$ odd, 1 if $n$ even |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical LaTeX Notice:** In the table above, the absolute-value bars in expressions like $[(n-1) \bmod (D-1)]$ use square brackets, **not** vertical pipes, to keep the markdown table parser intact. In handwritten or typeset answers, use $\vert \cdot \vert$ freely.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Binary Huffman** is the **backbone** of virtually every practical codec: DEFLATE (ZIP, gzip, PNG), JPEG (DC coefficients), MP3 (Huffman tables in the bit reservoir), and AAC. Its hardware implementation is trivial — a ROM-based lookup table suffices.
- **Non-Binary Huffman** appears in **JPEG extensions**, **H.264** (4-ary Exp-Golomb / CAVLC contexts), and **Morse code-like** systems where the transmission alphabet is $D$-ary (e.g., baseband signalling with $D$ voltage levels).
- **Arithmetic Coding** is the engine of **JBIG** (bi-level images), **JPEG 2000** (MQ-coder), **H.264/AVC and H.265/HEVC** (CABAC), and **Zstandard** (entropy stage). It achieves near-Shannon-limit compression for skewed alphabets where Huffman would waste up to $\approx 1$ bit/symbol.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example A — Binary Huffman Construction

**Problem.** Source $\mathcal{S} = \{a, b, c, d, e, f\}$ with $P = \{0.30, 0.25, 0.15, 0.12, 0.10, 0.08\}$. Construct the optimal binary Huffman tree, derive codewords, and compute $H(S)$, $\bar{L}$, and $\eta$.

**Step 1 — Compute Entropy:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
H(S) &= -\sum_{i=1}^{6} p_i \log_2 p_i \\
&= -(0.30\log_2 0.30 + 0.25\log_2 0.25 + 0.15\log_2 0.15 \\
&\quad + 0.12\log_2 0.12 + 0.10\log_2 0.10 + 0.08\log_2 0.08) \\
&= -(0.30(-1.737) + 0.25(-2.000) + 0.15(-2.737) \\
&\quad + 0.12(-3.059) + 0.10(-3.322) + 0.08(-3.644)) \\
&= 0.5211 + 0.5000 + 0.4106 + 0.3671 + 0.3322 + 0.2915 \\
&= 2.4225 \text{ bits/symbol}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Sort Symbols by Probability (ascending):**

| Position | Symbol | Probability |
|----------|--------|-------------|
| 1 | $f$ | 0.08 |
| 2 | $e$ | 0.10 |
| 3 | $d$ | 0.12 |
| 4 | $c$ | 0.15 |
| 5 | $b$ | 0.25 |
| 6 | $a$ | 0.30 |

**Step 3 — Iterative Merging:**

*Iteration 1:* Merge $f(0.08)$ and $e(0.10)$ → node $N_1 = 0.18$.
Queue: $\{d(0.12), c(0.15), N_1(0.18), b(0.25), a(0.30)\}$.

*Iteration 2:* Merge $d(0.12)$ and $c(0.15)$ → node $N_2 = 0.27$.
Queue: $\{N_1(0.18), b(0.25), N_2(0.27), a(0.30)\}$.

*Iteration 3:* Merge $N_1(0.18)$ and $b(0.25)$ → node $N_3 = 0.43$.
Queue: $\{N_2(0.27), a(0.30), N_3(0.43)\}$.

*Iteration 4:* Merge $N_2(0.27)$ and $a(0.30)$ → node $N_4 = 0.57$.
Queue: $\{N_3(0.43), N_4(0.57)\}$.

*Iteration 5:* Merge $N_3(0.43)$ and $N_4(0.57)$ → Root $R = 1.00$.

**Step 4 — Read Codewords (0 = left edge, 1 = right edge):**

Reading the tree from root to each leaf:

| Symbol | Probability | Path | Codeword | Length $\ell_i$ |
|--------|-------------|------|----------|----------------|
| $a$ | 0.30 | R → right → left | 10 | 2 |
| $b$ | 0.25 | R → right → right | 11 | 2 |
| $c$ | 0.15 | R → left → right → left | 010 | 3 |
| $d$ | 0.12 | R → left → right → right | 011 | 3 |
| $e$ | 0.10 | R → left → left → left | 000 | 3 |
| $f$ | 0.08 | R → left → left → right | 001 | 3 |

**Step 5 — Verify Kraft Inequality:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum 2^{-\ell_i} &= 2 \cdot 2^{-2} + 4 \cdot 2^{-3} = 2 \cdot 0.25 + 4 \cdot 0.125 \\
&= 0.50 + 0.50 = 1.00 \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Compute Average Length:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\bar{L} &= 0.30(2) + 0.25(2) + 0.15(3) + 0.12(3) + 0.10(3) + 0.08(3) \\
&= 0.60 + 0.50 + 0.45 + 0.36 + 0.30 + 0.24 \\
&= 2.45 \text{ bits/symbol}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7 — Coding Efficiency:**

$$
\eta = \frac{H(S)}{\bar{L}} \times 100\% = \frac{2.4225}{2.45} \times 100\% \approx 98.88\%
$$

**Step 8 — Redundancy:**

$$
R = \bar{L} - H(S) = 2.45 - 2.4225 = 0.0275 \text{ bits/symbol}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification using Noiseless Coding Theorem:** $H(S) \leq \bar{L} < H(S)+1$ → $2.4225 \leq 2.45 < 3.4225$ ✓. The code is optimal by Huffman's theorem since no other prefix code can achieve $\bar{L} < 2.45$ for this source.

---

### 3.2 Worked Example B — Ternary Huffman with Dummy Padding

**Problem.** Source with $n = 5$ symbols, $P = \{0.40, 0.20, 0.15, 0.15, 0.10\}$. Build an optimal **ternary** ($D=3$) Huffman tree.

**Step 1 — Compute Required Dummies:**

$$
(n - 1) \bmod (D-1) = (5 - 1) \bmod 2 = 4 \bmod 2 = 0
$$

So $n_d = (D-1) - 0 = 2$. We must add **2 dummy symbols** $d_1, d_2$ with $p_{d_1} = p_{d_2} = 0$.

Expanded alphabet (7 symbols): $\{s_1, s_2, s_3, s_4, s_5, d_1, d_2\}$.

**Step 2 — Sort and Merge in Groups of 3:**

Group the 3 lowest: $d_1(0), d_2(0), s_5(0.10)$ → Node $N_1 = 0.10$.
Queue: $\{N_1(0.10), s_3(0.15), s_4(0.15), s_2(0.20), s_1(0.40)\}$.

Group the next 3 lowest: $N_1(0.10), s_3(0.15), s_4(0.15)$ → Node $N_2 = 0.40$.
Queue: $\{s_2(0.20), s_1(0.40), N_2(0.40)\}$.

Last group: $s_2(0.20), s_1(0.40), N_2(0.40)$ → Root $R = 1.00$.

**Step 3 — Codeword Assignment (0, 1, 2 for the three child edges):**

| Symbol | Probability | Codeword | Length $\ell_i$ |
|--------|-------------|----------|----------------|
| $s_1$ | 0.40 | 1 | 1 |
| $s_2$ | 0.20 | 2 | 1 |
| $s_5$ | 0.10 | 00 | 2 |
| $s_3$ | 0.15 | 01 | 2 |
| $s_4$ | 0.15 | 02 | 2 |
| $d_1, d_2$ | 0 | discarded | — |

**Step 4 — Verify D-ary Kraft Inequality:**

$$
\sum_{i=1}^{5} 3^{-\ell_i} = 2 \cdot 3^{-1} + 3 \cdot 3^{-2} = \frac{2}{3} + \frac{3}{9} = \frac{6}{9} + \frac{3}{9} = 1.00 \quad \checkmark
$$

**Step 5 — Compute $\bar{L}$:**

$$
\bar{L} = 0.40(1) + 0.20(1) + 0.10(2) + 0.15(2) + 0.15(2) = 0.40 + 0.20 + 0.20 + 0.30 + 0.30 = 1.40 \text{ ternary digits/symbol}
$$

Convert to bits for comparison: $\bar{L}_{\text{bits}} = 1.40 \cdot \log_2 3 \approx 1.40 \cdot 1.585 = 2.219$ bits/symbol, slightly better than the binary Huffman for this skewed source.

---

### 3.3 Worked Example C — Arithmetic Coding Interval Narrowing

**Problem.** Encode the message **"BEE"** using arithmetic coding. Source alphabet $\mathcal{S} = \{A, B, E\}$ with $P(A)=0.5, P(B)=0.3, P(E)=0.2$.

**Step 1 — Build Cumulative Ranges:**

| Symbol | Probability | $F_{i-1}$ | $F_i$ |
|--------|-------------|-----------|-------|
| $A$ | 0.5 | 0.0 | 0.5 |
| $B$ | 0.3 | 0.5 | 0.8 |
| $E$ | 0.2 | 0.8 | 1.0 |

**Step 2 — Initial Interval:** $[low, high) = [0.000000, 1.000000)$.

**Step 3 — Encode First Symbol 'B':**

Range for B is $[F_{B-1}, F_B) = [0.5, 0.8)$. Interval width $= 0.3$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
low &= 0.0 + (1.0 - 0.0) \cdot 0.5 = 0.500000 \\
high &= 0.0 + (1.0 - 0.0) \cdot 0.8 = 0.800000 \\
\therefore\ [low, high) &= [0.500000, 0.800000)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Encode Second Symbol 'E':**

Range for E is $[0.8, 1.0)$. Width of current interval $= 0.3$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
low &= 0.5 + (0.8 - 0.5) \cdot 0.8 = 0.5 + 0.24 = 0.740000 \\
high &= 0.5 + (0.8 - 0.5) \cdot 1.0 = 0.5 + 0.30 = 0.800000 \\
\therefore\ [low, high) &= [0.740000, 0.800000)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Encode Third Symbol 'E':**

Range for E is $[0.8, 1.0)$. Width of current interval $= 0.06$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
low &= 0.740 + (0.800 - 0.740) \cdot 0.8 = 0.740 + 0.048 = 0.788000 \\
high &= 0.740 + (0.800 - 0.740) \cdot 1.0 = 0.740 + 0.060 = 0.800000 \\
\therefore\ [low, high) &= [0.788000, 0.800000)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Bits Required and Final Code:**

Final interval width: $W = 0.012000$.

$$
B = \lceil -\log_2(0.012) \rceil = \lceil 6.38 \rceil = 7 \text{ bits}
$$

Pick the midpoint: $z = (0.788 + 0.800)/2 = 0.794$.

$$
z = 0.794 = 0.110010110\ldots_2
$$

Truncating to 7 bits: **`1100101`** (i.e., the integer 101, with an implicit leading `0.` from the unit interval).

**Step 7 — Decoder Verification:**

Given code `1100101` (= 0.78125 in binary = 0.1100101₂), the decoder reverses the narrowing steps and reconstructs **"BEE"** — confirming correctness.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** Always show the interval at **every** step in the answer script; examiners award partial marks for each correct narrowing even if the final binary truncation has an off-by-one.

---

### 3.4 Comparative Analytical Matrix

| Property | Binary Huffman | Non-Binary Huffman | Arithmetic Coding |
|----------|----------------|--------------------|--------------------|
| Output type | Concatenated bit-stream | D-ary digit stream | Single number in $[0,1)$ |
| Bits per symbol (limit) | $\bar{L} \in [H, H+1)$ | $\bar{L}_D \in [H_D, H_D+1)$ | $\to H$ as $N \to \infty$ |
| Optimality | Optimal for given $P$ | Optimal among prefix codes | Asymptotically optimal |
| Hardware cost | Very low (ROM lookup) | Low (D-ary ROM) | High (multipliers, precision) |
| Patent status | Free | Free | Some implementations patented (IBM, 1990s expired) |
| Adaptivity | Needs 2 passes (semi-adaptive) | Needs 2 passes | Trivially adaptive |
| Best use case | Uniform IID alphabets | Multi-level signalling | Skewed or correlated sources |

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Binary Huffman Tree Construction Flow

The following flowchart depicts the operational logic of the Huffman encoder as a **Block-Level Functional Architecture**, mapping each algorithmic phase to its data-flow function.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Symbol Set S with Probabilities P] --> B[Compute Entropy H S]
    B --> C[Initialize Min Priority Queue Q with n leaves]
    C --> D{Is Q size 1?}
    D -- No --> E[Extract-Min node u]
    E --> F[Extract-Min node v]
    F --> G[Create parent node w with p_w = p_u + p_v]
    G --> H[Link u as left child edge 0]
    H --> I[Link v as right child edge 1]
    I --> J[Insert w back into Q]
    J --> D
    D -- Yes --> K[Root node R obtained]
    K --> L[Traverse R to leaves reading codewords]
    L --> M[Output Codebook Table]
    M --> N[Verify Kraft Inequality]
    N --> O[Compute Average Length L bar]
    O --> P[Compute Efficiency eta and Redundancy R]
```

### 4.2 Non-Binary Huffman Construction with Dummy Padding

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Source alphabet with n symbols] --> B[Choose alphabet size D]
    B --> C[Compute n_d = D-1 minus n-1 mod D-1]
    C --> D{n_d equals 0?}
    D -- Yes --> E[Proceed with n symbols]
    D -- No --> F[Append n_d dummy symbols with p equals 0]
    F --> E
    E --> G[Sort all n plus n_d symbols ascending by probability]
    G --> H[Repeat until single root: merge D lowest into new node]
    H --> I[New node probability equals sum of D merged nodes]
    I --> J{Single root reached?}
    J -- No --> H
    J -- Yes --> K[Discard dummy leaf nodes from tree]
    K --> L[Assign 0, 1, ..., D-1 to D child edges]
    L --> M[Verify D-ary Kraft Inequality]
    M --> N[Output D-ary codebook]
```

### 4.3 Arithmetic Coding Encoder State Machine

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Initialize low equals 0.0] --> B[Initialize high equals 1.0]
    B --> C[Read next symbol x_k from input]
    C --> D[Look up cumulative range F_k-1 and F_k]
    D --> E[Compute new_low = low + high-minus-low times F_k-1]
    E --> F[Compute new_high = low + high-minus-low times F_k]
    F --> G{All symbols processed?}
    G -- No --> H[Update low and high]
    H --> C
    G -- Yes --> I[Final interval low, high]
    I --> J[Compute midpoint z]
    J --> K[Convert z to binary with B = ceil -log_2 W bits]
    K --> L[Output binary codeword]
```

### 4.4 Huffman vs Arithmetic — Architectural Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Multimedia Source Data] --> B[Statistical Model Probabilities]
    B --> C{Encoder Choice}
    C -- Path 1 --> D[Binary Huffman Tree]
    C -- Path 2 --> E[D-ary Huffman Tree]
    C -- Path 3 --> F[Arithmetic Coder]
    D --> G[Codebook + Bit Stream]
    E --> H[Codebook + D-ary Stream]
    F --> I[Single Fractional Number]
    G --> J[Compressed File]
    H --> J
    I --> J
    J --> K[Entropy Limit H S reached asymptotically]
```

### 4.5 Interval Narrowing Visualization (for message "BE")

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Initial Interval 0.0, 1.0] --> B[Encode B: range 0.5 to 0.8]
    B --> C[Sub-interval 0.50, 0.80 width 0.30]
    C --> D[Encode E: range 0.8 to 1.0]
    D --> E[Sub-interval 0.740, 0.800 width 0.060]
    E --> F[Final tag in 0.740, 0.800 identifies message BE]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram-Reading Note:** Each box in Mermaid block 4.5 represents the *interval state* after one symbol. The narrowing factor at each step equals the probability of the just-encoded symbol — this is the geometric intuition behind why frequent symbols "cost fewer bits" in arithmetic coding.
