> - [ ] **QMF mirror property:** $H_1(z) = H_0(-z)$
> - [ ] **Perfect reconstruction:** $Y(z) = z^{-D} X(z)$ (aliasing-free)
> - [ ] **Frequency domain transforms:** DFT, DCT, MDCT used in audio coding
> - [ ] **Parseval's theorem:** Time-energy equals frequency-energy
> - [ ] **Real-world codecs:** MP3 (polyphase + MDCT), G.711 (A/µ-Law), G.722 (QMF)
> - [ ] **Human auditory model:** Weber-Fechner logarithmic perception motivates companding
> - [ ] **Filter bank applications:** Sub-band ADPCM, audio coding, image coding (wavelets)
> - [ ] **Standard bitrate:** 8 kHz sampling × 8 bits = 64 kbps PCM
> - [ ] **Dynamic range:** A-Law provides ~24 dB improvement, µ-Law provides ~33 dB

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Basic Sub-Band Coding - MPEG Audio - Speech Compression Techniques
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECNT742 - MULTIMEDIA COMPRESSION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Basic Sub-Band Coding - MPEG Audio - Speech Compression Techniques

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 Sub-Band Coding (SBC)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus):**
> **Sub-Band Coding (SBC)** is a frequency-domain transform coding technique in which the input signal is split into several frequency sub-bands using a bank of bandpass filters, and each sub-band is independently encoded using a coding technique (typically PCM or ADPCM) matched to its statistical characteristics and perceptual importance.

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Imagine you are at a noisy railway station trying to hear an announcement. Your brain automatically "tunes in" to the human voice frequencies (roughly 300 Hz – 4 kHz) and ignores the rumble of engines and hiss of steam. Sub-Band Coding does exactly the same thing mathematically — it splits the wide audio spectrum (20 Hz – 20 kHz) into smaller slices (sub-bands) so that each slice can be compressed with a *bit budget* proportional to how much the human ear actually hears in that slice. Bands containing perceptually important sounds get more bits; bands that the ear cannot resolve well get fewer bits (or even zero bits).

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Constants & Metrics in SBC:**
> - **Number of Sub-bands:** Typically **n = 4, 8, 16, or 32**.
> - **Sampling Frequency:** CD quality uses **fs = 44.1 kHz**.
> - **Sub-band bandwidth:** For n sub-bands: **Δf = fs / (2n)** Hz.
> - **Standard:** ITU-T **G.722** uses 2 sub-bands (60–4000 Hz, 4000–8000 Hz).

---

### 1.2 MPEG Audio

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition:**
> **MPEG Audio** refers to the family of perceptual audio coding standards defined by the **Moving Picture Experts Group (MPEG)** under **ISO/IEC 11172-3 (MPEG-1)**, **ISO/IEC 13818-3 (MPEG-2)**, and **ISO/IEC 23003-3 (MPEG-4 Audio)**. The most famous implementation is **MP3 (MPEG-1 Audio Layer 3)**. The core idea is to discard audio components that the human auditory system cannot perceive (perceptual/audio-frequency masking), thereby achieving high compression ratios (typically 10:1 to 12:1) with little perceived loss in quality.

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Think of an orchestra playing very loudly. If a triangle is being softly tapped in the background, the loud violins will *mask* the triangle — your ear literally cannot hear it. MPEG Audio is like a smart engineer who, *before* writing the audio to a file, checks: "Can the human ear hear this sound given what else is playing loudly?" If not, the engineer simply throws that sound away to save space. This is called **perceptual (psychoacoustic) coding**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Standard Metrics:**
> - **Bit rates:** 32, 48, 64, 96, 128, 192, 256, 320, **384 kbps**.
> - **Sampling frequencies:** 32, 44.1, **48 kHz**.
> - **Compression ratio:** 10:1 to 12:1 (transparent CD-quality at **128 kbps** for MP3).

---

### 1.3 Speech Compression Techniques

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition:**
> **Speech Compression** refers to lossy/lossless techniques that exploit the statistical redundancy, short-term stationarity, and vocal-tract production model of human speech (bandwidth typically **300 Hz – 3400 Hz** at fs = 8 kHz). Standard techniques include **PCM, DPCM, ADPCM, APC, SBC, LPC, CELP, and MELP**.

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
A piano's sound can be perfectly reconstructed if you record the keys pressed, because the piano is a *predictable* instrument. The human voice, while varied, is also *predictable* — your throat, tongue, and mouth shape an excitation into speech-like sounds. Speech compression tries to record *only* the shape of your mouth (a few parameters) plus a thin excitation signal, instead of the full waveform. You speak → coder finds the best matching mouth shape → receiver reconstructs speech from that shape.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metrics for Speech:**
> - **Telephone (PSTN) bandwidth:** **300 Hz – 3400 Hz**.
> - **Standard sampling rate:** **fs = 8 kHz** (narrowband), **16 kHz** (wideband).
> - **MOS score (Mean Opinion Score):** 1 (bad) to 5 (excellent). Toll-quality PCM = **4.4**, CELP at 4.8 kbps ≈ **3.5–4.0**.

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 Sub-Band Coding — Operational Pipeline

The SBC encoder works in **five logical stages**:

1. **Filter Bank Decomposition** – Input signal `x(n)` is passed through a bank of `n` bandpass filters. Output of the `k`-th sub-band: `xk(n)`.
2. **Downsampling** – Each sub-band is critically downsampled by a factor `M = n` (because the bandwidth is 1/n of original). This is allowed by the **Nyquist criterion for bandpass signals** since `2·(fs/2n) = fs/n`.
3. **Quantization & Coding** – Each downsampled sub-band is quantized using a custom quantizer (e.g., 4-bit for low-energy sub-bands, 16-bit for high-energy).
4. **Multiplexing & Transmission** – Sub-band samples, side information, and bit-allocation table are packed into a bitstream.
5. **Reconstruction** – Decoder upsamples by `M`, runs synthesis filter bank, and sums the sub-bands.

**Quadrature Mirror Filter (QMF) Bank:**
> [!IMPORTANT]
> In real implementations, **QMF** or **Pseudo-QMF** banks are used so that the analysis and synthesis filters are mirror-image pairs about fs/4, eliminating **aliasing** between adjacent sub-bands. For 32 sub-bands (as in MPEG Layer 1/2), a **Polyphase Filter Bank** is used.

**Why use Sub-Band Coding?**
- Different frequency bands have different **variance** → allocate bits where variance is high.
- Exploits **frequency-masking** of the human ear.
- Provides **scalable bit-rate** quality trade-off.

---

### 2.2 MPEG Audio — Layered Architecture

MPEG Audio defines **three independent coding layers** with increasing complexity and compression:

| Layer | Bit-rate (typical stereo) | Compression Ratio | Complexity | Algorithm core |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Layer 1** | 384 kbps | 4:1 | Low | 32-band PQMF + 4-bit uniform quant + psychoacoustic model 1 |
| **Layer 2** | 192–256 kbps | 6:1 to 8:1 | Medium | 32-band PQMF + larger frame + psychoacoustic model 1 |
| **Layer 3 (MP3)** | 128 kbps | 10:1 to 12:1 | High | 32-band PQMF + MDCT (hybrid filter bank) + non-uniform quant + Huffman + psychoacoustic model 2 |

**MPEG-1 Audio Frame Structure:**
- Each frame = **1152 samples** per channel (Layer 1: 384 samples).
- Composed of: **Header (32 bits)** → **CRC (optional, 16 bits)** → **Bit Allocation** → **Scalefactors** → **Sub-band samples**.

**Psychoacoustic Model (the heart of MPEG):**
1. Compute **FFT** of the audio frame.
2. Identify **tonal** (sinusoidal) and **non-tonal** (noise) maskers.
3. Compute **masking threshold** T(z) for each critical band.
4. Compute **Signal-to-Mask Ratio (SMR)** for each sub-band:
   $$SMR_k = E_k - T_k$$
   where `Ek` = energy in sub-band k, `Tk` = masking threshold in sub-band k.
5. Bit allocation: assign more bits to sub-bands with **higher SMR**.

---

### 2.3 Speech Compression — Technique Hierarchy

**A. Waveform Coders (low compression, high quality):**
- **PCM (Pulse Code Modulation):** Each sample quantized independently.
- **DPCM (Differential PCM):** Encode prediction error `e(n) = x(n) - x̂(n)`.
- **ADPCM (Adaptive DPCM):** Step-size `Δ(n)` adapts to signal variance (ITU-T **G.721, G.726**).

**B. Parametric / Vocoder (very high compression, lower quality):**
- **LPC (Linear Predictive Coding):** Models speech as output of an **all-pole filter** of order p, excited by pitch + noise.
   $$\hat{s}(n) = \sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k s(n-k)$$
- **Residual Excited LPC (RELP), Multi-Pulse LPC (MPLPC), Code-Excited LPC (CELP)** — used at 4.8–16 kbps.

**C. Hybrid Coders (mid compression, good quality):**
- **MBE (Multi-Band Excitation)**, **STC (Sinusoidal Transform Coding)**, **MELP (Mixed Excitation Linear Prediction)** — used in military/LPWAN at 2.4–4.8 kbps.

---

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table is a KTU exam-ready cheat sheet. Memorize these equations before ESE.**

| # | Formula / Concept | Description | Units / Typical Value |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | `Δf = fs / (2n)` | Sub-band bandwidth | Hz |
| 2 | `M = n` (downsample factor) | Critical decimation per sub-band | Integer |
| 3 | `R_sub = fs / n` | Sampling rate of each sub-band | Hz |
| 4 | `B_total = Σ (Rk · bk)` | Total bit rate (bps) | bps |
| 5 | `SMRk = Ek - Tk` | Signal-to-Mask Ratio per sub-band | dB |
| 6 | `MNRk = NMRk - SNRk` | Mask-to-Noise Ratio | dB |
| 7 | `LPC prediction: ŝ(n) = Σ ak·s(n-k)` | All-pole vocal tract model | a1..ap coefficients |
| 8 | `Predictor order p: fs ≤ 8 kHz → p=10; 8kHz<fs ≤ 20kHz → p=2+fs/1000` | LPC order selection | p (integer) |
| 9 | `CR = 8000 · 16 / R_speech` | Speech compression ratio | dimensionless |
| 10 | `SQNR = 6.02·B + 1.76` (dB) | Quantization SNR (B bits) | dB |
| 11 | `G (gain) = σx / σe` | ADPCM gain adaptation | scalar |
| 12 | `MPEG Layer 1 frame = 384 samples` | Frame size at fs | samples |
| 13 | `MPEG Layer 2/3 frame = 1152 samples` | Frame size at fs | samples |
| 14 | `Masking threshold T = Pspl - Masker + SF` | Psychoacoustic masking | dB SPL |
| 15 | `ADPCM bit-rate = fs · 4` (G.726 32 kbps) | Output bit-rate | bps |

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 Derivation — Sub-Band Coding Bit-Rate Calculation

**Problem Setup:** An audio signal with `fs = 44.1 kHz` is split into `n = 32` sub-bands. Sub-band 5 receives `b5 = 6 bits` per sample, sub-band 12 receives `b12 = 4 bits`, and all others receive `b = 2 bits`. Compute the total bit rate.

**Step 1 — Sub-band sampling rate**
Each sub-band is critically downsampled by `M = n = 32`. Therefore, every sub-band's effective sampling rate is:

$$R_{\text{sub}} = \frac{f_s}{n} = \frac{44100}{32} = 1378.125 \text{ samples/sec}$$

**Step 2 — Bit-rate per sub-band**
For a generic sub-band `k` with `bk` bits per sample:

$$R_k = R_{\text{sub}} \cdot b_k = \frac{f_s}{n} \cdot b_k$$

**Step 3 — Aggregate bit-rate over all 32 sub-bands**

$$B_{\text{total}} = \sum_{k=1}^{n} R_k = \frac{f_s}{n}\sum_{k=1}^{n} b_k$$

**Step 4 — Substitute the values**
Out of 32 sub-bands, **30 sub-bands** use 2 bits, **1 sub-band** uses 6 bits, **1 sub-band** uses 4 bits. So:

$$\sum b_k = (30 \cdot 2) + 6 + 4 = 60 + 10 = 70 \text{ bits/sample-block}$$

**Step 5 — Final calculation**

$$B_{\text{total}} = \frac{44100}{32} \cdot 70 = 1378.125 \cdot 70 = 96468.75 \text{ bps} \approx 96.47 \text{ kbps}$$

**Result:** Total bit-rate ≈ **96.47 kbps per channel** (mono).

---

### 3.2 Derivation — LPC Predictor Order and Bit-Allocation

**Setup:** Speech signal sampled at `fs = 8 kHz`. Determine the LPC order `p` and estimate the unquantized prediction gain.

**Step 1 — Predictor order (Rabiner & Schafer rule)**

For fs ≤ 8 kHz, the conventional choice is `p = 8 + fs/1000`. With fs = 8 kHz:

$$p = 8 + \frac{8000}{1000} = 8 + 8 = 16$$

Wait — KTU textbooks use the simpler rule: for narrowband speech `p = 10`. Let us adopt `p = 10` for the 8 kHz case, as this is the more standard exam value.

$$p = 10$$

**Step 2 — LPC equation (autocorrelation method)**
For each lag `i = 1..p`, we solve the Yule–Walker system:

$$R(j) = \sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k R(j-k), \quad j = 1, 2, \ldots, p$$

where `R(k)` is the autocorrelation of `s(n)` at lag `k`.

**Step 3 — Solving for LPC coefficients (Levinson–Durbin recursion)**
Initialize: `E0 = R(0)`, `ki` are reflection coefficients.

For `i = 1` to `p`:

$$k_i = \frac{1}{E_{i-1}}\left[R(i) - \sum_{j=1}^{i-1} a_j^{(i-1)} R(i-j)\right]$$

$$a_i^{(i)} = k_i, \quad a_j^{(i)} = a_j^{(i-1)} - k_i a_{i-j}^{(i-1)} \text{ for } j = 1..i-1$$

$$E_i = (1 - k_i^2) E_{i-1}$$

**Step 4 — Prediction gain**
The prediction gain `G_p` in dB:

$$G_p = 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{R(0)}{E_p}\right) = 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{1}{\prod_{i=1}^{p}(1 - k_i^2)}\right)$$

For a typical voiced segment, `G_p ≈ 10–18 dB`.

---

### 3.3 Derivation — MPEG-1 Layer 2 Bit-Rate and Compression Ratio

**Setup:** Stereo CD-quality audio (fs = 44.1 kHz, 16 bits/sample) encoded at 192 kbps (Layer 2).

**Step 1 — PCM bit-rate (uncompressed)**

$$R_{\text{PCM}} = 2 \cdot f_s \cdot B = 2 \cdot 44100 \cdot 16 = 1411200 \text{ bps} = 1411.2 \text{ kbps}$$

**Step 2 — MPEG-2 Layer 2 bit-rate**

$$R_{\text{MPEG}} = 192 \text{ kbps}$$

**Step 3 — Compression ratio**

$$\text{CR} = \frac{R_{\text{PCM}}}{R_{\text{MPEG}}} = \frac{1411.2}{192} = 7.35 : 1$$

**Step 4 — Bits saved per stereo sample block (1152 samples/channel)**

$$\text{Bits saved/block} = 2 \cdot 1152 \cdot 16 - \frac{192000 \cdot 1152}{44100}$$

$$= 36864 - 5013.6 = 31850.4 \text{ bits/block}$$

---

### 3.4 Derivation — ADPCM Bit-Rate and SQNR (G.726)

**Setup:** ADPCM codec at 32 kbps with fs = 8 kHz.

**Step 1 — Bits per sample**

$$b = \frac{R}{f_s} = \frac{32000}{8000} = 4 \text{ bits/sample}$$

**Step 2 — Theoretical SQNR**

$$\text{SQNR}_{\text{dB}} = 6.02 \cdot B + 1.76 = 6.02 \cdot 4 + 1.76 = 24.08 + 1.76 = 25.84 \text{ dB}$$

> [!NOTE]
> In practice, G.726 ADPCM achieves ≈ **38 dB** SQNR due to adaptive quantization. The 25.84 dB is a *lower bound* for uniform quantizers.

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

### 4.1 Sub-Band Coding — Encoder/Decoder Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Input Audio x of n] --> B[Analysis Filter Bank]
    B --> C1[Sub-band 1]
    B --> C2[Sub-band 2]
    B --> C3[Sub-band k]
    B --> C4[Sub-band n]
    C1 --> D1[Downsample by M]
    C2 --> D2[Downsample by M]
    C3 --> D3[Downsample by M]
    C4 --> D4[Downsample by M]
    D1 --> E1[Quantize and Encode]
    D2 --> E2[Quantize and Encode]
    D3 --> E3[Quantize and Encode]
    D4 --> E4[Quantize and Encode]
    E1 --> F[Bitstream Mux]
    E2 --> F
    E3 --> F
    E4 --> F
    F --> G[Channel or Storage]
    G --> H[Bitstream Demux]
    H --> I1[Decode Sub-band 1]
    H --> I2[Decode Sub-band 2]
    H --> I3[Decode Sub-band k]
    H --> I4[Decode Sub-band n]
    I1 --> J1[Upsample by M]
    I2 --> J2[Upsample by M]
    I3 --> J3[Upsample by M]
    I4 --> J4[Upsample by M]
    J1 --> K[Synthesis Filter Bank]
    J2 --> K
    J3 --> K
    J4 --> K
    K --> L[Reconstructed Audio y of n]
```

### 4.2 MPEG Audio — Encoder Block Architecture (Layer 3 / MP3)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[PCM Audio Input 44.1 kHz] --> B[32-Band PQMF Analysis Bank]
    B --> C[MDCT Hybrid Filter Bank]
    C --> D[1024-Point FFT for Psychoacoustics]
    D --> E[Psychoacoustic Model 2]
    E --> F[Compute SMR per Sub-band]
    B --> G[Per Sub-band Quantization]
    F --> H[Bit Allocation Table]
    H --> G
    G --> I[Huffman Coding of Sub-band Samples]
    I --> J[Bitstream Formatting]
    J --> K[MP3 Encoded File 128 kbps]
```

### 4.3 LPC-based Speech Coder — Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Speech s of n 8 kHz] --> B[Pre-emphasis Filter 1 - 0.97 z^-1]
    B --> C[Frame Buffer 20 ms = 160 samples]
    C --> D[LPC Analysis Levinson Durbin p=10]
    D --> E1[Reflection Coefficients k1..kp]
    D --> E2[LPC Coefficients a1..ap]
    D --> E3[Residual Excitation e of n]
    E1 --> F[Convert to LSP for Quantization]
    F --> G[Vector Quantization of LSPs]
    G --> H[Bitstream Pack]
    E2 --> H
    E3 --> I1[Pitch Detector]
    I1 --> I2[Voiced or Unvoiced Decision]
    I2 --> H
    H --> J[Transmitted at 2.4 to 4.8 kbps]
```

### 4.4 Comparison Matrix — Speech Coding Methods

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Waveform[Waveform Coders 16 to 64 kbps]
        W1[PCM 64 kbps]
        W2[DPCM 32 to 48 kbps]
        W3[ADPCM 16 to 32 kbps G.726]
        W4[SBC and APC 16 to 24 kbps]
    end
    subgraph Hybrid[Hybrid Coders 4.8 to 16 kbps]
        H1[RELP 9.6 kbps]
        H2[MPLPC 9.6 kbps]
        H3[CELP 4.8 to 16 kbps]
    end
    subgraph Vocoder[Parametric Vocoders 1.2 to 4.8 kbps]
        V1[LPC 2.4 to 4.8 kbps]
        V2[MELP 2.4 kbps US Fed]
        V3[Channel Vocoder 1.2 kbps]
    end
```

---
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

### 5.1 Part A Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Explain the basic concept of sub-band coding with a neat block diagram. How does it exploit frequency masking of the human ear?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
> Sub-Band Coding splits the input signal into `n` frequency sub-bands using a bank of bandpass filters (typically QMF or PQMF). Each sub-band is then critically downsampled (by factor `n`) and quantized independently with a bit budget proportional to its energy and perceptual importance. By allocating **more bits to perceptually important bands** and **fewer bits to masked bands**, SBC achieves compression ratios of 4:1 to 8:1. It exploits the fact that inaudible (masked) components do not need to be transmitted, thus saving bits while preserving perceived quality. **(3 marks: 1 — block diagram, 1 — concept, 1 — masking exploitation)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
*List the three MPEG-1 audio layers in order of increasing complexity. What is the typical bit rate of MP3 for transparent CD-quality audio?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
> The three MPEG-1 audio layers in order of increasing complexity are **Layer 1 (≈ 384 kbps, complexity 1×), Layer 2 (≈ 192 kbps, complexity 3×), and Layer 3 / MP3 (≈ 128 kbps, complexity 12×)**. The typical MP3 bit rate for transparent (indistinguishable from CD) stereo audio is **128 kbps**, giving a compression ratio of about **11:1** relative to CD PCM (1411.2 kbps). **(3 marks: 1 — list, 1 — bit rate, 1 — CR)**

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (Module-3 Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **Sub-Band Coding (SBC)** encoder and decoder. Discuss the role of the **Quadrature Mirror Filter (QMF)** bank in alias cancellation. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** A wideband speech signal of bandwidth 4 kHz is sampled at fs = 8 kHz and divided into 8 sub-bands. If bits/sample allocation across sub-bands are 5, 4, 5, 3, 4, 5, 2, 4 respectively, compute: (i) sub-band sampling rate, (ii) total bit rate, (iii) compression ratio with respect to 16-bit PCM. **[7 marks]**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Sub-Band Coding Block Diagram & QMF Role (7 marks):**

**Encoder:** The input signal `x(n)` is first passed through an **analysis filter bank** consisting of `n` bandpass filters. The `k`-th filter isolates the sub-band `[k·fs/(2n), (k+1)·fs/(2n)]`. Each filtered signal is then **critically downsampled** by a factor `M = n` (since each sub-band has bandwidth `fs/(2n)` and may be sampled at `fs/n`). The downsampled sub-band signals are quantized and encoded (typically with ADPCM), and the resulting bits are **multiplexed** along with side information (bit-allocation table, scale factors) into a bitstream.

**Decoder:** The bitstream is **demultiplexed**; each sub-band bitstream is decoded and **upsampled** by `M`; a **synthesis filter bank** reconstructs the full-band signal `ŷ(n)`.

**Role of QMF:** When critical downsampling is performed, **aliasing** is introduced in each sub-band. QMF banks are designed such that the analysis filters `Hk(z)` and synthesis filters `Fk(z)` satisfy the mirror-image condition `Fk(z) = Hk(-z)`. After upsampling, the aliasing components cancel exactly during the synthesis process, allowing **perfect reconstruction** in the absence of quantization. **(7 marks: 2 — encoder, 2 — decoder, 3 — QMF / alias cancellation)**

---

**(b) Numerical Problem (7 marks):**

**Given:** `fs = 8 kHz`, `n = 8` sub-bands, bit allocations `bk = {5, 4, 5, 3, 4, 5, 2, 4}`.

**Step 1 — Sub-band sampling rate:**

$$R_{\text{sub}} = \frac{f_s}{n} = \frac{8000}{8} = 1000 \text{ samples/sec per sub-band}$$

**(2 marks)**

**Step 2 — Sum of bit allocations:**

$$\sum_{k=1}^{8} b_k = 5+4+5+3+4+5+2+4 = 32 \text{ bits/sample-block}$$

**Step 3 — Total bit rate:**

$$B_{\text{total}} = R_{\text{sub}} \cdot \sum b_k = 1000 \cdot 32 = 32000 \text{ bps} = 32 \text{ kbps}$$

**(2 marks)**

**Step 4 — Original PCM bit rate (16-bit, mono):**

$$R_{\text{PCM}} = f_s \cdot 16 = 8000 \cdot 16 = 128000 \text{ bps} = 128 \text{ kbps}$$

**Step 5 — Compression ratio:**

$$\text{CR} = \frac{128}{32} = 4 : 1$$

**(1 mark)**

> **Final Answers:** (i) `R_sub = 1000` samples/s, (ii) `B_total = 32 kbps`, (iii) **CR = 4:1**.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [ALTERNATIVE CHOICE]**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]**

**(a)** Explain the **MPEG-1 Audio coding standard** with a neat block diagram. Detail the functions of the **32-band Polyphase Quadrature Mirror Filter (PQMF)** and the **Psychoacoustic Model 2**. Discuss the role of **SMR (Signal-to-Mask Ratio)** in bit allocation. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** With a block diagram, explain the working of a **Linear Predictive Coding (LPC)** speech analyzer. For a speech signal sampled at `fs = 8 kHz`, determine the **LPC order** and compute the **unquantized prediction gain** given reflection coefficients `k1 = 0.6, k2 = 0.4, k3 = 0.3, k4 = 0.2, k5 = 0.1` (assume `p = 5`). **[7 marks]**

#### Model Solution

**(a) MPEG-1 Audio Coding (7 marks):**

**Block Diagram Flow:**
1. The PCM input (e.g., 16-bit, 44.1 kHz stereo) is fed into a **32-band PQMF analysis filter bank** with total downsampling factor of 32, producing 32 sub-band signals.
2. The same PCM input is also fed into a **1024-point FFT** block which feeds the **Psychoacoustic Model 2** (in MP3/Layer 3). Model 2 computes a much more detailed masking threshold than Model 1, considering **temporal masking** (pre- and post-masking) in addition to simultaneous frequency masking.
3. The output of the psychoacoustic model is the **SMR per sub-band**: `SMRk = Ek - Tk`.
4. **Bit allocation** assigns more bits to sub-bands with higher SMR (audible) and zero bits to fully masked sub-bands.
5. Sub-band samples are **quantized** (non-uniform in MP3) and **Huffman-coded**; the bitstream is formatted with a 32-bit header per frame (1152 samples).

**PQMF Role:** Splits the audio into 32 equal-width sub-bands of 750 Hz each (at fs = 48 kHz). Adjacent filters are mirror-image so that aliasing cancels upon reconstruction. Polyphase implementation reduces the **arithmetic cost** by ~32× compared to a direct FIR filter bank.

**SMR-based Bit Allocation:** `SMRk = (energy in sub-band k) − (masking threshold in sub-band k)`. Sub-bands with high SMR are clearly audible → assign more bits. Sub-bands with SMR < 0 dB are masked → assign 0 bits. **(7 marks: 2 — diagram, 2 — PQMF/FFT, 3 — SMR + bit alloc.)**

---

**(b) LPC Speech Analyzer (7 marks):**

**Block Diagram Flow:** Pre-emphasis → Windowed frame (20 ms) → Autocorrelation R(k) → Levinson–Durbin recursion → LPC coefficients `ak` + reflection coefficients `ki` + residual `e(n)` + pitch detection.

**Step 1 — LPC order for fs = 8 kHz:**

Using `p = 2 + fs/1000`:

$$p = 2 + \frac{8000}{1000} = 2 + 8 = 10$$

(Alternate accepted answer using `p = 8 + fs/1000` gives p = 16.) **(1 mark)**

**Step 2 — Prediction gain formula:**

$$G_p = 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{1}{\prod_{i=1}^{p}(1 - k_i^2)}\right) \text{ dB}$$

**Step 3 — Compute `1 − ki^2` for each ki:**

$$1 - 0.6^2 = 1 - 0.36 = 0.64$$
$$1 - 0.4^2 = 1 - 0.16 = 0.84$$
$$1 - 0.3^2 = 1 - 0.09 = 0.91$$
$$1 - 0.2^2 = 1 - 0.04 = 0.96$$
$$1 - 0.1^2 = 1 - 0.01 = 0.99$$

**Step 4 — Product:**

$$\prod_{i=1}^{5}(1 - k_i^2) = 0.64 \times 0.84 \times 0.91 \times 0.96 \times 0.99$$

$$= 0.64 \times 0.84 = 0.5376$$
$$0.5376 \times 0.91 = 0.4892$$
$$0.4892 \times 0.96 = 0.4696$$
$$0.4696 \times 0.99 = 0.4649$$

**Step 5 — Prediction gain:**

$$G_p = 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{1}{0.4649}\right) = 10 \log_{10}(2.151) = 10 \cdot 0.3327 = 3.33 \text{ dB}$$

> **Final Answer:** p = **10** (or 16), `Gp ≈ 3.33 dB`. **(7 marks: 1 — diagram, 1 — p, 3 — calculation, 2 — final answer + interpretation)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Marks Lost:**
> 1. **Forgetting critical downsampling factor `M = n`.** Many students wrongly take sub-band rate = fs, giving inflated bit rates and 8× the correct answer.
> 2. **Confusing MPEG-Layer 1 (384 samples/frame) with Layer 2/3 (1152 samples/frame).** This 3× error is the most common framing mistake in MP3 numericals.
> 3. **Writing SMR as `E_k / T_k`.** SMR is a **difference** in **dB**, not a ratio. Correct: `SMRk (dB) = Ek (dB) − Tk (dB)`.
> 4. **LPC order rule confusion.** Always state the rule you are using (`p = 2 + fs/1000` vs `p = 8 + fs/1000`) — KTU accepts both if justified.
> 5. **Forgetting to log10 the prediction-gain product.** Many students write the product alone and lose 1 mark.
> 6. **Drawing the LPC block diagram without the "pre-emphasis" stage.** Examiners specifically look for pre-emphasis (1 − 0.97z⁻¹) in the LPC pipeline.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Read 5 min before the exam):**

- **SBC:** Splits audio into `n` sub-bands → critical decimation by `M = n` → per-band quantization → synthesis filter bank with **alias cancellation** (QMF/PQMF).
- **Bit rate formula:** `B = (fs/n) · Σ bk`. Sub-band rate = `fs/n`.
- **MPEG Audio:** Perceptual (psychoacoustic) lossy coder; **MP3 = MPEG-1 Layer 3**; uses **32-band PQMF + 1024-pt FFT + MDCT + Huffman**.
- **SMR per sub-band:** `SMR = Energy − Masking threshold` (in dB). Higher SMR → more bits.
- **Frame sizes:** Layer 1 = **384 samples**; Layer 2 / 3 = **1152 samples**.
- **MP3 transparent stereo:** ≈ **128 kbps**, CR ≈ **11:1**.
- **LPC order rule:** `p = 2 + fs/1000` (narrowband) → p = 10 for 8 kHz speech.
- **LPC equation:** `ŝ(n) = Σ a_k · s(n−k)`; **prediction gain** `G_p = 10 log10(1/∏(1−k_i²))`.
- **Speech coders hierarchy:** PCM (64 kbps) → ADPCM (32 kbps) → CELP (4.8–16 kbps) → LPC / MELP (2.4 kbps) → Channel vocoder (1.2 kbps).
- **ADPCM bit rate:** `R = fs · b` (G.726 32 kbps uses 4 bits at 8 kHz).
- **SQNR for uniform quantization:** `6.02·B + 1.76 dB`.
- **SQNR for ADPCM in practice:** ≈ 35–38 dB (better than uniform because of adaptive step size).
- **Standard speech bandwidth:** 300 Hz – 3400 Hz; standard sample rate `fs = 8 kHz`.
- **MPEG-1 bit rates:** 32, 48, 64, 96, 128, 192, 256, 320, 384 kbps; sampling: 32, 44.1, 48 kHz.
- **MOS:** Toll-quality PCM ≈ 4.4; CELP at 4.8 kbps ≈ 3.5–4.0; LPC at 2.4 kbps ≈ 2.5–3.0.
- **MPEG key terms to write in answers:** "**Perceptual (psychoacoustic) redundancy**", "**Simultaneous vs Temporal masking**", "**Threshold in quiet**", "**Critical band rate (Bark scale)**", "**Polyphase implementation reduces ALU by ~32×**".

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Linear Predictive Coding (LPC) and Code Excited LPC
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECNT742 - MULTIMEDIA COMPRESSION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Linear Predictive Coding (LPC) and Code Excited LPC

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Linear Predictive Coding (LPC)** is a parametric time-domain speech compression technique that models the human speech production system as an all-pole linear time-varying (LTV) digital filter driven by an appropriate excitation signal. The principle is to predict each speech sample as a **linear combination of its past $p$ samples**, where the predictor coefficients $\{a_k\}$ are estimated by minimizing the mean-squared prediction error over a short, stationary analysis frame (typically **20–30 ms**).

The fundamental LPC prediction equation is:

$$
\hat{s}(n) \;=\; \sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k \, s(n-k)
$$

The error (residual) signal is the difference between the actual and predicted samples:

$$
e(n) \;=\; s(n) - \hat{s}(n) \;=\; s(n) - \sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k \, s(n-k)
$$

**Code-Excited Linear Prediction (CELP)** is the dominant class of modern analysis-by-synthesis (AbS) speech coders that retain the LPC synthesis filter of the standard vocoder, but replace the binary/simple excitation model with a **vector-quantised stochastic codebook** of Gaussian-excited candidate sequences, selected by closed-loop **perceptually weighted mean-squared error (WMSE)** minimisation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor — Module 3:** *"Speech compression — short-term and long-term predictors, LPC vocoder, residual coding, Code-Excited Linear Prediction (CELP), analysis-by-synthesis loop, perceptual weighting filter."*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Echo of the Vocal Tract"

Imagine you are shouting inside a long stairwell. The "tail" of your voice — the reverberation — depends on the **shape of the stairwell**, not on the strength of your shout. LPC exploits exactly this physics:

- **Your vocal cords** act as the *excitation source* (the shout) — a buzz for voiced sounds (`/a/`, `/i/`) or a hiss for unvoiced sounds (`/s/`, `/f/`).
- **Your mouth, tongue, and nasal cavity** act as the *vocal-tract filter* — a resonant cavity whose shape changes the spectrum of the buzz/hiss into recognisable phonemes.
- LPC mathematically builds a **digital copy of your vocal-tract filter** (the predictor) and stores *only the filter coefficients + a compact excitation code* at the receiver. The receiver then *re-synthesises* the speech by running the excitation through the filter — like feeding a small electrical buzz through a wooden model of your mouth.

The compression is dramatic: instead of sending 64 kbps of PCM samples, LPC-10 (the classic military standard) ships speech at **2.4 kbps** by transmitting just 10 reflection coefficients, gain, pitch, and a voicing bit every 22.5 ms.

## 1.3 Why This Topic is High-Yield for KTU 2024

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Distribution Tendency:** In KTU End-Semester Examinations under the 2024 NEP-aligned scheme, LPC/CELP frequently appears as a 14-mark Part-B question (with sub-parts on (i) predictor derivation and (ii) codebook search). Direct 3-mark questions test the speech production model and the AbS loop. Mastering this module is essential for `CO3 — Apply parametric models to compress speech signals`.

## 1.4 Physical / Quantitative Anchor Metrics

| Metric | Typical Value | Remarks |
|---|---|---|
| LPC order $p$ | **8 – 16** | 10 in FS1015 LPC-10, 10–12 in CELP |
| Frame length $N$ | **20 – 30 ms** | 160–240 samples @ 8 kHz |
| Sub-frame length | **5 – 7.5 ms** | 40–60 samples (for LTP search) |
| Pitch period range | **20 – 160 samples** | 50–400 Hz fundamental |
| Bit-rate of LPC-10 | **2.4 kbps** | Original DoD standard |
| Bit-rate of CELP family | **4.8 – 16 kbps** | FS1016, MELP, GSM, VSELP |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Speech Production Model — The Source-Filter Paradigm

Speech is decomposed (per Fant, 1960) into two mathematically separable components:

1. **Excitation Source $E(z)$** — Models the airflow at the glottis:
   - **Voiced** (periodic impulse train) — pitch period $T_0$ and gain $G$.
   - **Unvoiced** (white Gaussian noise) — random turbulent flow.
2. **Vocal Tract Filter $H(z)$** — An all-pole transfer function modelling resonances (formants):

$$
H(z) \;=\; \frac{G}{1 - \sum\limits_{k=1}^{p} a_k z^{-k}} \;=\; \frac{G}{A(z)}
$$

where $A(z) = 1 - \sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k z^{-k}$ is the **inverse filter (LPC analysis filter)**. Synthesis recovers speech via $S(z) = E(z) \cdot H(z)$.

## 2.2 Short-Term Prediction (STP) — Formant Removal

The **all-pole assumption** of the vocal tract gives the predictor:

$$
\hat{s}(n) \;=\; \sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k \, s(n-k) \quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad \hat{S}(z) = \left(\sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k z^{-k}\right) S(z)
$$

Minimise the energy of the residual $e(n) = s(n) - \hat{s}(n)$:

$$
E_p \;=\; \sum_{n} e^2(n) \;=\; \sum_{n} \left[s(n) - \sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k s(n-k)\right]^2
$$

Setting $\dfrac{\partial E_p}{\partial a_k} = 0$ for $k = 1, 2, \dots, p$ yields the **Yule-Walker normal equations**:

$$
\sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k \, R(\vert i - k \vert) \;=\; R(i) \quad\quad i = 1, 2, \dots, p
$$

where $R(m) = \displaystyle\sum_{n} s(n)\, s(n-m)$ is the short-term autocorrelation. In matrix form:

$$
\mathbf{R}_p \, \mathbf{a} \;=\; \mathbf{r}_p
$$

with $\mathbf{R}_p$ a $p \times p$ **Toeplitz** symmetric positive-definite autocorrelation matrix.

## 2.3 Long-Term Prediction (LTP) — Pitch Removal

Even after STP, the residual of voiced speech still contains quasi-periodic peaks spaced by the pitch period $T$. LTP removes this redundancy using a single-tap (or 3-tap) pitch predictor:

$$
\hat{e}(n) \;=\; \beta \, e(n - T)
$$

where $T$ is the integer pitch lag and $\beta$ the pitch gain. The combined predictor structure is:

$$
s(n) \;=\; \sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k s(n-k) \;+\; \beta \, e(n-T) \;+\; \underbrace{g \, c_n}_{\text{CELP innovation}}
$$

## 2.4 Levinson-Durbin Algorithm — Closed-Form Solution

The Toeplitz structure of $\mathbf{R}_p$ admits an $O(p^2)$ recursive solution:

$$
E_0 \;=\; R(0)
$$

$$
k_i \;=\; \frac{R(i) - \sum_{j=1}^{i-1} a^{(i-1)}_j \, R(i-j)}{E_{i-1}}
$$

$$
a^{(i)}_i \;=\; k_i, \qquad a^{(i)}_j \;=\; a^{(i-1)}_j - k_i \, a^{(i-1)}_{i-j} \;\;\;(1 \le j \le i-1)
$$

$$
E_i \;=\; (1 - k_i^2) \, E_{i-1}
$$

The quantities $k_i$ are the **PARCOR (reflection) coefficients** with $\vert k_i \vert < 1$ for stability.

## 2.5 CELP — Analysis-by-Synthesis (AbS) Loop

CELP eliminates the need for explicit pitch/voicing decisions. The encoder performs a **closed-loop search** over:

1. A **stochastic codebook** $\mathcal{C} = \{\mathbf{c}_m\}$ of $N$-sample Gaussian-excited vectors.
2. A **gain** $g_m \in \mathbb{R}$.

For each candidate index $m$, the synthesised speech is:

$$
\hat{\mathbf{s}}_m \;=\; g_m \, \mathbf{H} \, \mathbf{c}_m
$$

where $\mathbf{H}$ is the lower-triangular **convolution matrix** built from the LPC impulse response. The optimal pair $(m^\star, g_m^\star)$ minimises the **perceptually weighted error**:

$$
(m^\star, g_m^\star) \;=\; \arg\min_{m,\,g} \;\left\| \mathbf{x} - g \, \mathbf{H}_w \, \mathbf{c}_m \right\|^2
$$

with the **perceptual weighting filter**:

$$
W(z) \;=\; \frac{A(z)}{A(z/\gamma)} \;=\; \frac{1 - \sum a_k z^{-k}}{1 - \sum a_k \gamma^k z^{-k}}, \quad 0 < \gamma < 1
$$

The closed-form optimal gain is:

$$
g_m^\star \;=\; \frac{\mathbf{x}^{\mathsf T} \mathbf{H}_w \mathbf{c}_m}{\Vert \mathbf{H}_w \mathbf{c}_m \Vert^2}
$$

and the minimum distortion is:

$$
D_m \;=\; \Vert \mathbf{x} \Vert^2 - \frac{\left(\mathbf{x}^{\mathsf T} \mathbf{H}_w \mathbf{c}_m\right)^2}{\Vert \mathbf{H}_w \mathbf{c}_m \Vert^2}
$$

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING RULE:** Absolute-value bars inside the table are rendered as `\vert` to prevent markdown-table breakage.

| # | Concept | Formula | Variables / Conditions |
|---|---|---|---|
| F1 | LPC prediction | $\hat{s}(n) = \sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k s(n-k)$ | $p$ = predictor order |
| F2 | Residual | $e(n) = s(n) - \sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k s(n-k)$ | Quantised/transmitted in CELP |
| F3 | Synthesis filter | $H(z) = G \big/ \left(1 - \sum a_k z^{-k}\right)$ | All-pole vocal tract |
| F4 | Autocorrelation | $R(m) = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1-m} s(n) s(n+m)$ | Windowed frame, lag $m \ge 0$ |
| F5 | Yule–Walker | $\sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k R(\vert i - k \vert) = R(i)$ | $i = 1, \dots, p$ |
| F6 | MSE of residual | $E_p = R(0) - \sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k R(k)$ | Minimum residual energy |
| F7 | Levinson–Durbin | $E_i = (1 - k_i^2) E_{i-1}$ | $k_i$ = PARCOR / reflection coef |
| F8 | LTP predictor | $\hat{e}(n) = \beta e(n - T)$ | $T$ = pitch lag, $\beta$ = gain |
| F9 | Perceptual weight | $W(z) = A(z) \big/ A(z/\gamma)$ | $0 < \gamma < 1$ (typ. **0.92**) |
| F10 | CELP opt. gain | $g_m^\star = \mathbf{x}^{\mathsf T} \mathbf{H}_w \mathbf{c}_m \,/\, \Vert \mathbf{H}_w \mathbf{c}_m \Vert^2$ | Closed-form, inner product |
| F11 | CELP distortion | $D_m = \Vert \mathbf{x} \Vert^2 - (g_m^\star)^2 \Vert \mathbf{H}_w \mathbf{c}_m \Vert^2$ | Pick $m$ with smallest $D_m$ |
| F12 | LPC-10 bit-rate | $2.4$ kbps | 10 coef + pitch/voicing/gain |

## 2.7 Engineering Utility Beyond Speech

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Speech codecs (real-world):** GSM Full-Rate (VSELP, 13 kbps), GSM Half-Rate (VSELP, 5.6 kbps), MELP (2.4 kbps military), FS1016 CELP (4.8 kbps), IS-54 VSELP, AMR-WB, Opus, 3GPP EVS.
> - **Audio coding:** MDCT-based codecs (MP3, AAC) borrow the *perceptual weighting* philosophy from CELP.
> - **Biomedical:** LPC modelling of EEG/ECG envelopes for low-bitrate telemetry.
> - **Speech recognition front-ends:** Cepstral coefficients (LPCCs) derived from LPC are classical ASR features.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Yule–Walker Equations (STP Coefficients)

We minimise the residual energy over a windowed frame of length $N$:

$$
E_p \;=\; \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} \left[ s(n) - \sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k s(n-k) \right]^2
$$

**Step 1 — Expand the square:**

$$
E_p \;=\; \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} \left[ s^2(n) - 2 s(n) \sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k s(n-k) + \left(\sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k s(n-k)\right)^{\!2} \right]
$$

**Step 2 — Differentiate w.r.t. each coefficient $a_i$ (with $i = 1, \dots, p$):**

$$
\frac{\partial E_p}{\partial a_i} \;=\; -2 \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} s(n)\, s(n-i) \;+\; 2 \sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} s(n-k)\, s(n-i)
$$

**Step 3 — Define the autocorrelation function** $R(m) = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1-m} s(n)\, s(n+m)$:

$$
\frac{\partial E_p}{\partial a_i} \;=\; -2 R(i) \;+\; 2 \sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k R(i - k)
$$

**Step 4 — Set the derivative to zero for the minimum:**

$$
\sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k R(i - k) \;=\; R(i) \quad\quad i = 1, 2, \dots, p
$$

This is the **Yule–Walker system**, solvable via Levinson–Durbin.

**Step 5 — Minimum residual energy** (substitute the optimal $a_k$ back):

$$
E_p^{\min} \;=\; R(0) - \sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k R(k)
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This single equation is the **basis of the 3-mark KTU question** "*Derive the minimum residual energy of an LPC predictor of order $p$.*"

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — LPC-2 of a Toy Frame

Let a 4-sample frame be $s = [1, 2, 3, 4]$. Compute $p = 2$ LPC coefficients using autocorrelation.

**Step 1 — Compute $R(0), R(1), R(2)$:**

$$
R(0) = 1^2 + 2^2 + 3^2 + 4^2 = 30
$$

$$
R(1) = 1 \cdot 2 + 2 \cdot 3 + 3 \cdot 4 = 2 + 6 + 12 = 20
$$

$$
R(2) = 1 \cdot 3 + 2 \cdot 4 = 3 + 8 = 11
$$

**Step 2 — Set up Yule–Walker system (Levinson–Durbin, $i = 2$):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_0 &= R(0) = 30 \\[2pt]
k_1 &= \frac{R(1)}{E_0} = \frac{20}{30} = \tfrac{2}{3} \\[2pt]
a_1^{(1)} &= k_1 = \tfrac{2}{3} \\[2pt]
E_1 &= (1 - k_1^2) E_0 = \left(1 - \tfrac{4}{9}\right) \cdot 30 = \tfrac{5}{9} \cdot 30 = \tfrac{50}{3}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Second reflection coefficient:**

$$
k_2 = \frac{R(2) - a_1^{(1)} R(1)}{E_1} = \frac{11 - \tfrac{2}{3} \cdot 20}{\tfrac{50}{3}} = \frac{11 - \tfrac{40}{3}}{\tfrac{50}{3}} = \frac{\tfrac{33-40}{3}}{\tfrac{50}{3}} = \frac{-7}{50} = -0.14
$$

$$
a_2^{(2)} = k_2 = -0.14, \qquad a_1^{(2)} = a_1^{(1)} - k_2\, a_1^{(1)} = \tfrac{2}{3}\,(1 - (-0.14)) = \tfrac{2}{3} \cdot 1.14 = 0.76
$$

**Step 4 — Final LPC coefficients of order 2:**

$$
\boxed{a_1 = 0.76, \quad a_2 = -0.14, \quad E_2 = (1 - k_2^2) E_1 = (1 - 0.0196) \cdot \tfrac{50}{3} \approx 16.39}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The stability check $\vert k_1 \vert = 0.67 < 1$ and $\vert k_2 \vert = 0.14 < 1$ confirms the all-pole filter is stable.

## 3.3 Derivation — CELP Closed-Form Optimal Gain

The perceptually weighted error vector is $\mathbf{e}_m = \mathbf{x} - g \, \mathbf{y}_m$ where $\mathbf{y}_m = \mathbf{H}_w \mathbf{c}_m$.

**Step 1 — Distortion (squared error):**

$$
D_m(g) \;=\; \Vert \mathbf{x} - g\, \mathbf{y}_m \Vert^2 \;=\; \mathbf{x}^{\mathsf T}\mathbf{x} - 2g\,\mathbf{x}^{\mathsf T}\mathbf{y}_m + g^2 \mathbf{y}_m^{\mathsf T}\mathbf{y}_m
$$

**Step 2 — Differentiate and set to zero:**

$$
\frac{d D_m}{d g} \;=\; -2 \mathbf{x}^{\mathsf T}\mathbf{y}_m + 2 g\, \mathbf{y}_m^{\mathsf T}\mathbf{y}_m \;=\; 0
$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $g$:**

$$
g_m^\star \;=\; \frac{\mathbf{x}^{\mathsf T}\mathbf{y}_m}{\mathbf{y}_m^{\mathsf T}\mathbf{y}_m} \;=\; \frac{\mathbf{x}^{\mathsf T}\mathbf{H}_w \mathbf{c}_m}{\Vert \mathbf{H}_w \mathbf{c}_m \Vert^2}
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute back to get the minimum distortion:**

$$
D_m^{\min} \;=\; \Vert \mathbf{x} \Vert^2 - \frac{\left(\mathbf{x}^{\mathsf T}\mathbf{H}_w \mathbf{c}_m\right)^2}{\Vert \mathbf{H}_w \mathbf{c}_m \Vert^2}
$$

**Step 5 — Codebook search complexity:** Picking the best of $M$ codewords costs $O(MN)$ multiplications, which is the dominant computational bottleneck of CELP. This is why **algebraic codebooks** (ACELP in 3GPP) replaced **stochastic codebooks** in modern standards.

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — When to Use What

> [!NOTE]
> The following decision matrix is a high-yield KTU revision summary.

| Codec Family | Bit-rate | Excitation Model | Analysis Style | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| LPC-10 (FS1015) | **2.4 kbps** | Binary voiced/unvoiced + pitch | Open-loop | Military secure voice |
| MELP | **2.4 kbps** | Mixed pulse + noise, aperiodic flag | Open-loop + mixed | NATO STANAG 4591 |
| FS1016 CELP | **4.8 kbps** | Stochastic codebook, 256 entries | Closed-loop AbS | Federal std secure voice |
| VSELP | **5.6 – 13 kbps** | 3 basis codebooks | Closed-loop AbS | GSM, IS-54 |
| ACELP (AMR, EVS) | **4.75 – 24 kbps** | Algebraic 40-pulse codebook | Closed-loop AbS | 3GPP, 4G/5G voice |
| Wideband CELP | **16 – 32 kbps** | Multi-pulse / CELP | Closed-loop AbS | VoIP, wideband speech |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 LPC Vocoder — Encoder & Decoder Block Diagram

The figure below shows the classical LPC-10 architecture: the analysis filter $A(z)$ removes short-term correlation, the residual is parameterised by gain/pitch/voicing, and the synthesis filter $1/A(z)$ rebuilds speech at the receiver.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Speech Input s of n] --> B[Pre-emphasis Filter 1 - alpha z^-1]
    B --> C[Frame Buffer plus Hamming Window N=240]
    C --> D[LPC Analysis Order 10 via Levinson Durbin]
    D --> D1[Quantise a k to LSP or PARCOR]
    D1 --> E[Residual e of n equals s of n minus prediction]
    E --> F[Pitch plus Voicing Detector T and V UV]
    F --> F1[Quantise Gain G]
    D1 --> TX[Channel Encoder 2.4 kbps Multiplex]
    F1 --> TX
    F --> TX
    TX --> CH((Digital Channel))
    CH --> RX[Channel Decoder De-multiplex]
    RX --> G1[Reconstruct a k]
    RX --> H1[Reconstruct G T V UV]
    G1 --> I[LPC Synthesis Filter 1 over A of z]
    H1 --> J[Excitation Generator Pulse Train if V White Noise if UV]
    J --> I
    I --> K[De-emphasis plus De-emphasis Filter]
    K --> L[Synthesised Speech s hat of n]
```

## 4.2 CELP — Analysis-by-Synthesis (AbS) Closed-Loop Architecture

The defining innovation of CELP: the encoder *embeds a copy of the decoder* and searches the codebook so that the *synthesised* (not the analysed) speech minimises **perceptually weighted** error.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    X[Input Speech Vector x of length N] --> Diff((Subtract))
    Diff --> Err[Weighted Error Vector e w of n]
    Err --> MSE[Compute Distortion D m]
    MSE --> Min[Find m star with min D m]
    Min --> OutIndex[Transmit Codebook Index m star]
    Min --> OutGain[Transmit Gain g m star]
    X --> PerW[Perceptual Weighting Filter W of z]
    PerW --> Sum
    CB[Stochastic Codebook C of size M equals 256] --> Pick[Pick Candidate c m]
    Pick --> GainMult[Multiply by Gain g]
    GainMult --> Synth[LPC Synthesis 1 over A of z]
    Synth --> Hw[Convolve with H w]
    Hw --> Sum((Add))
    Sum --> Diff
    OutIndex --> TX[(Transmit)]
    OutGain --> TX
```

## 4.3 Speech Production Model — Source/Filter Decomposition

This block diagram formalises the Fant source-filter model that underpins every LPC-family codec. The glottal source is mathematically isolated from the time-varying vocal-tract filter.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Exc[Excitation Source E of z] -->|Voiced Impulse Train OR Noise| Prod((Convolve))
    H[All-pole Vocal Tract H of z equals G over 1 minus sum a k z to minus k] --> Prod
    Prod --> Rad[Radiation Load R of z] --> Out[s of n]
    subgraph Source_Block
        Exc
    end
    subgraph Filter_Block
        H
        Rad
    end
```

## 4.4 Hierarchical Prediction Architecture (STP + LTP + Codebook)

This figure captures the **three-tier redundancy-removal structure** common to CELP-family codecs: short-term (formant), long-term (pitch), and stochastic innovation.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[s of n Original Speech] --> STP[Short-Term Predictor sum a k s of n minus k]
    STP --> E1[Residual 1 e 1 of n]
    E1 --> LTP[Long-Term Predictor beta e of n minus T]
    LTP --> E2[Residual 2 e 2 of n]
    E2 --> CB[Codebook Innovation g c n]
    CB --> E3[Final Excitation to be Quantised]
    STP -. "LPC coeffs a k" .-> TX[Transmitted Parameters]
    LTP -. "Lag T and Gain beta" .-> TX
    CB -. "Index m and Gain g" .-> TX
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->
