# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper PECNT742]`
**State and explain the linear prediction model of speech production. Why is the all-pole assumption justified?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The linear prediction model represents speech $s(n)$ as the output of an all-pole filter $H(z) = G \big/ A(z)$ excited by either a periodic pulse train (voiced) or white noise (unvoiced):

$$
s(n) = \sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k s(n-k) + G \cdot e(n)
$$

The all-pole assumption is justified because (i) for nasal and vowel sounds the vocal tract behaves predominantly as a resonant cavity (poles dominate), (ii) the order $p = 10$–$12$ is sufficient to capture the four formant pairs of adult speech, and (iii) it leads to a tractable linear least-squares problem (Yule–Walker) with a fast $O(p^2)$ solution via Levinson–Durbin. **(3 marks)**

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**What is the perceptual weighting filter in CELP? Why is it used instead of plain MSE?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The perceptual weighting filter is $W(z) = A(z)/A(z/\gamma)$ where $0 < \gamma < 1$ (typically $\gamma = 0.92$). It shapes the error spectrum so that the codec spends its bit-budget on **frequency regions of high auditory masking** (formant peaks) and de-emphasises spectral valleys where noise is easily heard. Plain MSE would distribute errors uniformly in frequency, wasting bits on perceptually unimportant regions. By minimising the weighted MSE, CELP achieves transparent quality at much lower bit-rates. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, PECNT742]`

**(a)** Derive the autocorrelation-based Yule–Walker equations for the optimum $p$-th order linear predictor. Show that the minimum mean-squared prediction error is $E_p = R(0) - \sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k R(k)$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the Levinson–Durbin recursive algorithm for solving the Yule–Walker equations. Why is the stability condition $\vert k_i \vert < 1$ for all $i$ essential? **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Derivation (7 marks):**

The predictor output is $\hat{s}(n) = \sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k s(n-k)$, and the prediction error is $e(n) = s(n) - \sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k s(n-k)$. The mean-squared error over an $N$-sample windowed frame is:

$$
E_p = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1-p} e^2(n) = \sum_{n} \left[s(n) - \sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k s(n-k)\right]^2
$$

Differentiate with respect to $a_i$ and set to zero:

$$
\frac{\partial E_p}{\partial a_i} = -2 \sum_n s(n) s(n-i) + 2 \sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k \sum_n s(n-k) s(n-i) = 0
$$

Recognising $R(m) = \sum_n s(n) s(n-m)$:

$$
\sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k R(i - k) = R(i), \quad i = 1, \dots, p
$$

These are the **Yule–Walker normal equations**. Substituting the optimal solution back into the error expression and using the Yule–Walker identity $\sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k R(i-k) = R(i)$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_p^{\min} &= \sum_n s^2(n) - 2 \sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k \sum_n s(n) s(n-k) + \sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k \sum_{i=1}^{p} a_i \sum_n s(n-k) s(n-i) \\
&= R(0) - 2 \sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k R(k) + \sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k R(k) \\
&= R(0) - \sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k R(k) \quad \text{(after applying the Yule-Walker relations)}
\end{aligned}
$$

Hence $\boxed{E_p^{\min} = R(0) - \sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k R(k)}$. **(7 marks — full marks require: setup 2, differentiation 2, recognition 2, final expression 1.)**

**(b) Levinson–Durbin + stability (7 marks):**

The autocorrelation matrix $\mathbf{R}_p$ is **Toeplitz** (constant along each diagonal) and **Hermitian positive-definite** (for a non-zero signal). Levinson–Durbin exploits this structure to recursively build the order-$i$ solution from the order-$(i-1)$ solution in $O(p^2)$ operations (vs. $O(p^3)$ for general Gaussian elimination):

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_0 &= R(0) \\
k_i &= \frac{R(i) - \sum_{j=1}^{i-1} a^{(i-1)}_j R(i-j)}{E_{i-1}} \\
a^{(i)}_i &= k_i \\
a^{(i)}_j &= a^{(i-1)}_j - k_i\, a^{(i-1)}_{i-j}, \quad 1 \le j \le i-1 \\
E_i &= (1 - k_i^2)\, E_{i-1}
\end{aligned}
$$

The quantities $k_i$ are called **PARCOR (partial correlation) coefficients** and represent the reflection coefficients of an equivalent acoustic-tube model of the vocal tract.

**Stability condition:** The all-pole filter $1/A(z)$ is **stable** (all poles inside the unit circle) if and only if $\vert k_i \vert < 1$ for every $i = 1, \dots, p$. This is because each $k_i$ is a reflection coefficient of a lossless tube; if $\vert k_i \vert \ge 1$ the tube would require non-passive (active) elements, implying poles on/outside the unit circle. Since $E_i = (1 - k_i^2) E_{i-1}$ and $E_i$ must remain **non-negative** (it is a residual energy), $\vert k_i \vert < 1$ is mathematically necessary and sufficient. **(7 marks — algorithm 3, PARCOR interpretation 2, stability 2.)**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam — May 2024, PECNT742, Internal Choice B]`

**(a)** Describe the **analysis-by-synthesis (AbS) loop** of a Code-Excited Linear Prediction (CELP) coder with the aid of a block diagram. Explain the role of the perceptual weighting filter $W(z)$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a CELP coder, the perceptually weighted error for codeword $m$ is $\mathbf{e}_m = \mathbf{x} - g\, \mathbf{H}_w \mathbf{c}_m$. Derive the closed-form expressions for the optimum gain $g_m^\star$ and the resulting minimum distortion $D_m^{\min}$. Comment on the computational complexity of the codebook search. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) AbS loop + perceptual weighting (7 marks):**

In a CELP encoder, the analysis-by-synthesis principle means the encoder **synthesises candidate outputs internally** and picks the one closest to the original. The loop is:

1. Estimate LPC coefficients $\{a_k\}$ from the input frame.
2. Quantise them to LSP/LSF indices and transmit.
3. For the current sub-frame, **search the stochastic codebook**: try every codeword $\mathbf{c}_m$ from the $M$-entry Gaussian codebook.
4. For each candidate, compute the **synthesised** speech $\hat{\mathbf{s}}_m$ by passing $g_m \mathbf{c}_m$ through the LPC synthesis filter $1/A(z)$, then through $W(z)$ to get $\mathbf{H}_w \mathbf{c}_m$.
5. Compute the weighted distortion $D_m = \Vert \mathbf{x} - g_m \mathbf{H}_w \mathbf{c}_m \Vert^2$ and pick the index $m^\star$ that minimises it.
6. Transmit $(m^\star, g_m^\star, a_k)$ to the receiver.

**Perceptual weighting filter role:** $W(z) = A(z)/A(z/\gamma)$ deliberately **tilts the error spectrum** to be inversely proportional to the short-term speech spectrum (concentrated near formants). Quantisation noise that overlaps formant peaks is masked by the speech energy itself, so $W(z)$ effectively allows the codec to "spend" bits where the ear cannot hear the error and to "save" bits in spectral valleys where noise is conspicuous. The parameter $\gamma \in (0,1)$ controls the trade-off: $\gamma = 0$ reduces to plain MSE, $\gamma \to 1$ gives aggressive spectral shaping. **(7 marks — AbS steps 4, role of W(z) 3.)**

**(b) Closed-form gain + distortion + complexity (7 marks):**

Start with $D_m(g) = \Vert \mathbf{x} - g\, \mathbf{y}_m \Vert^2$ where $\mathbf{y}_m = \mathbf{H}_w \mathbf{c}_m$.

Expand the squared norm:

$$
D_m(g) = \Vert \mathbf{x} \Vert^2 - 2 g\, \mathbf{x}^{\mathsf T}\mathbf{y}_m + g^2 \Vert \mathbf{y}_m \Vert^2
$$

Differentiate with respect to $g$ and set to zero:

$$
\frac{d D_m}{d g} = -2 \mathbf{x}^{\mathsf T}\mathbf{y}_m + 2 g \Vert \mathbf{y}_m \Vert^2 = 0
$$

Solve for the optimum gain:

$$
\boxed{g_m^\star = \frac{\mathbf{x}^{\mathsf T}\mathbf{y}_m}{\Vert \mathbf{y}_m \Vert^2} = \frac{\mathbf{x}^{\mathsf T}\mathbf{H}_w \mathbf{c}_m}{\Vert \mathbf{H}_w \mathbf{c}_m \Vert^2}}
$$

Substitute back into $D_m(g)$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_m^{\min} &= \Vert \mathbf{x} \Vert^2 - 2 g_m^\star \mathbf{x}^{\mathsf T}\mathbf{y}_m + (g_m^\star)^2 \Vert \mathbf{y}_m \Vert^2 \\
&= \Vert \mathbf{x} \Vert^2 - 2 \frac{(\mathbf{x}^{\mathsf T}\mathbf{y}_m)^2}{\Vert \mathbf{y}_m \Vert^2} + \frac{(\mathbf{x}^{\mathsf T}\mathbf{y}_m)^2}{\Vert \mathbf{y}_m \Vert^2} \\
&= \Vert \mathbf{x} \Vert^2 - \frac{(\mathbf{x}^{\mathsf T}\mathbf{y}_m)^2}{\Vert \mathbf{y}_m \Vert^2}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\boxed{D_m^{\min} = \Vert \mathbf{x} \Vert^2 - \frac{\left(\mathbf{x}^{\mathsf T}\mathbf{H}_w \mathbf{c}_m\right)^2}{\Vert \mathbf{H}_w \mathbf{c}_m \Vert^2}}
$$

**Complexity comment:** Searching the $M$-entry stochastic codebook requires $O(MN)$ multiply-adds per sub-frame, where $N$ is the sub-frame length. For $M = 256$–$1024$ and $N = 40$–$60$, this was historically the bottleneck — a major reason modern codecs (3GPP AMR, EVS) use **algebraic codebooks** with sparse, structured codewords that admit a much faster focused search. **(7 marks — expansion 1, differentiation 1, gain 2, distortion 2, complexity 1.)**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls — LPC & CELP Problems:**
> 1. **Sign error in residual:** Many students write $e(n) = \hat{s}(n) - s(n)$ instead of $s(n) - \hat{s}(n)$. This flips the sign of every LPC coefficient in the answer. Examiner deducts **1–2 marks**.
> 2. **Forgetting the window:** Autocorrelation $R(m)$ must use a **windowed** frame (Hamming/Hann). Plain un-windowed $R(m)$ over the full buffer produces unstable LPC filters at frame boundaries. **−1 mark**.
> 3. **Confusing STP and LTP:** Short-term prediction removes **formants** (order $p \approx 10$), long-term prediction removes **pitch periodicity** (1–3 tap delay). Mixing the two roles = **−2 marks**.
> 4. **Skipping the stability check:** Always end a Levinson–Durbin derivation with "**$\vert k_i \vert < 1$ ensures a stable all-pole synthesis filter.**" Omitting this is a **−1 mark** penalty.
> 5. **CELP: forgetting the $W(z)$ filter:** The closed-form gain uses the *weighted* $\mathbf{H}_w$, not raw $\mathbf{H}$. Writing the unweighted formula loses **2 marks** in part (b).
> 6. **Units / bit-rate mismatch:** Stating LPC-10 is 4.8 kbps (it is 2.4) or that MELP uses a stochastic codebook (it does not) = **−1 mark**.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — LPC & CELP (Module 3):**
> - **Speech production model** = **excitation** $E(z) \;\times\;$ **all-pole vocal-tract filter** $H(z) = G/A(z)$. The glottal source is **voiced** (impulse train, period $T$) or **unvoiced** (white noise).
> - **LPC equation:** $\hat{s}(n) = \sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k s(n-k)$; residual $e(n) = s(n) - \hat{s}(n)$. Predictor order $p \approx 10$ for 8 kHz speech.
> - **Yule–Walker** equations: $\sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k R(\vert i - k \vert) = R(i)$ — derived by minimising $E_p$ w.r.t. $a_k$.
> - **Minimum residual energy:** $E_p = R(0) - \sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k R(k)$.
> - **Levinson–Durbin** solves Yule–Walker in $O(p^2)$ using the Toeplitz structure; produces PARCOR $k_i$ with stability $\vert k_i \vert < 1$.
> - **Lattice filter** equivalence: $k_i$ are the reflection coefficients of an equivalent lossless acoustic tube.
> - **Long-Term Prediction (LTP):** $\hat{e}(n) = \beta e(n-T)$ removes residual pitch periodicity. Lag $T$ typically searched over **20–160** samples.
> - **LPC-10 (FS1015):** **2.4 kbps**; uses PARCOR/LSP quantisation + binary voicing + pitch period. Classic military standard.
> - **CELP (FS1016):** **4.8 kbps**; closed-loop AbS search over a 256-entry Gaussian stochastic codebook; perceptually weighted MSE.
> - **Perceptual weight:** $W(z) = A(z)/A(z/\gamma)$, $0 < \gamma < 1$ (typ. **0.92**). Exploits auditory masking.
> - **CELP closed-form gain:** $g_m^\star = \mathbf{x}^{\mathsf T}\mathbf{H}_w \mathbf{c}_m \,/\, \Vert \mathbf{H}_w \mathbf{c}_m \Vert^2$.
> - **CELP minimum distortion:** $D_m^{\min} = \Vert \mathbf{x} \Vert^2 - \dfrac{(\mathbf{x}^{\mathsf T}\mathbf{H}_w \mathbf{c}_m)^2}{\Vert \mathbf{H}_w \mathbf{c}_m \Vert^2}$.
> - **Modern variants:** VSELP (GSM, IS-54), ACELP (AMR, 3GPP), MELP (mixed-excitation 2.4 kbps).
> - **Real-world bit-rate ladder:** LPC-10 (2.4) < MELP (2.4) < FS1016 CELP (4.8) < GSM HR (5.6) < VSELP (13) ≈ AMR (4.75–12.2) < Opus (6–510).
> - **Pre-emphasis** filter $1 - \alpha z^{-1}$ (typ. $\alpha = 0.95$) is applied before LPC analysis to flatten the spectrum and reduce dynamic range; **de-emphasis** $1/(1 - \alpha z^{-1})$ is applied at synthesis.
> - **Windowing:** Hamming/Hann window of length $N = 20$–$30$ ms is applied per frame to ensure stationarity.
> - **Decoder is embedded in encoder:** This is the essence of AbS — the encoder *simulates* the decoder for every candidate excitation.
> - **Computational hot-spot:** Stochastic codebook search ($O(MN)$) → replaced by **algebraic codebooks** in modern 3GPP/AMR-WB/EVS codecs.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fundamentals - Compression Standards - DCT
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECNT742 - MULTIMEDIA COMPRESSION > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Fundamentals - Compression Standards - DCT # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Compression Standards in Multimedia Multimedia compression standards are internationally accepted technical specifications that define the algorithms, syntax, and decoding rules for representing audio, image, and video data in a compact digital form. They ensure interoperability between devices from different manufacturers and provide a common framework for storage and transmission. The two principal standardization bodies are: - **ITU-T (International Telecommunication Union – Telecommunication Standardization Sector)** – responsible for the **H.26x** family (e.g., H.261, H.263, H.264/AVC, H.265/HEVC). - **ISO/IEC** – responsible for the **MPEG-x** family (e.g., MPEG-1, MPEG-2, MPEG-4, JPEG, JPEG-2000). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Most modern hybrid video codecs (H.261 → H.265, MPEG-1 → MPEG-4) are based on a **block-based hybrid DPCM/DCT framework**. Hence, the **Discrete Cosine Transform (DCT)** is the single most important mathematical tool in this module. ## 1.2 The Discrete Cosine Transform (DCT) – Formal Definition The **Discrete Cosine Transform (DCT)** is a real-valued, orthogonal, linear transformation that converts a finite sequence of spatial-domain samples (pixel intensities or audio amplitudes) into a sequence of frequency-domain coefficients. It is mathematically equivalent to a **Discrete Fourier Transform (DFT)** of approximately twice the length, with even symmetry extension. For an input sequence $x(n)$ of length $N$, the **forward DCT (Type-II, the most commonly used in compression)** is defined as: $$X(k) = \sqrt{\frac{2}{N}} \, c(k) \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x(n) \cos\left[\frac{(2n+1)k\pi}{2N}\right], \quad k = 0, 1, \ldots, N-1$$ where the normalization constant is $$c(k) = \begin{cases} \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} & \text{for } k = 0 \\ 1 & \text{for } k = 1, 2, \ldots, N-1 \end{cases}$$ The corresponding **Inverse DCT (IDCT)** is identical in form due to orthogonality. > [!NOTE] > The DCT is a **lossless, invertible linear transform**. Compression arises not from the DCT itself, but from what we do with the resulting coefficients (quantization, entropy coding). ## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy: Reading a Photograph with "Different Glasses" Imagine you are looking at a photograph through different pairs of glasses: - **Regular glasses (Spatial Domain):** You see the picture as it is – millions of tiny colored dots (pixels), each carrying a brightness value. It is hard to compress because every dot looks unique. - **"Pattern glasses" (Frequency Domain via DCT):** Now the same image is decomposed into a small set of **building-block patterns** (horizontal waves, vertical waves, diagonal ripples, and a flat average brightness). Real pictures are mostly **smooth or slowly changing**; only the edges have sharp variation. The DCT concentrates the "energy" of the image into just a few of these patterns. We can now throw away the high-frequency, hard-to-see patterns with little visible loss. > [!NOTE] > **Real-world insight:** This is precisely why the DCT is the backbone of JPEG, MPEG, and H.26x – human perception is **insensitive to fine high-frequency detail**, so we can discard those coefficients aggressively. ## 1.4 Why DCT and not DFT? | Property | DFT | DCT | |---|---|---| | Output coefficients | Complex (real + imaginary) | **Purely real** | | Imaginary component handling | Requires extra storage | Not required – half the memory | | Boundary discontinuities | Severe (Gibbs phenomenon) | None (implicit even extension) | | Energy compaction | Moderate | **Excellent** – near-optimal for correlated data | | Computational complexity | $O(N^2)$ (FFT: $O(N \log N)$) | $O(N \log N)$ via fast algorithms | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KLT (Karhunen–Loève Transform)** is theoretically optimal for energy compaction, but it is **data-dependent** and has no fast algorithm. The DCT is the closest fixed, signal-independent approximation to the KLT for first-order Markov sources (e.g., natural images with correlation $\rho \approx 0.95$). This is the famous **"DCT is the next-best practical substitute for KLT"** result. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Compression Standards Classification Modern multimedia compression standards follow two broad paradigms: 1. **Predictive Coding (DPCM-based):** Used in speech/audio (e.g., ADPCM in G.726, LPC in CELP codecs). 2. **Transform Coding (DCT/DWT-based):** Used in still images (JPEG) and video (H.26x, MPEG-x). > [!NOTE] > **Video codecs are hybrid:** they use **motion-compensated prediction (DPCM)** in the *temporal* direction and **DCT-based transform coding** in the *spatial* direction. This is the **"Hybrid MC-DPCM/DCT"** architecture mandated by H.261, H.263, H.264, MPEG-1, MPEG-2, and MPEG-4. ## 2.2 The JPEG Standard – DCT in Action **JPEG (Joint Photographic Experts Group – ISO/IEC 10918)** operates on **8×8 pixel blocks** through the following pipeline: 1. **Level shift:** Subtract **128** from each pixel so that values lie in $[-128, +127]$. 2. **Forward 2D-DCT** on each 8×8 block → 64 frequency coefficients. 3. **Quantization:** Divide each coefficient by an entry of an 8×8 **quantization table** (Q-table) and round. This is the **primary lossy step**. 4. **Entropy coding:** Zig-zag scan + Run-Length Encoding (RLE) + **Huffman coding** (or arithmetic coding in baseline JPEG). The **DCT coefficient matrix layout** for an 8×8 block is: $$ \begin{pmatrix} DC & AC_{01} & AC_{02} & AC_{03} & AC_{04} & AC_{05} & AC_{06} & AC_{07} \\ AC_{10} & AC_{11} & \cdots & \cdots & \cdots & \cdots & \cdots & AC_{17} \\ AC_{20} & \cdots & \cdots & \cdots & \cdots & \cdots & \cdots & \cdots \\ \vdots & \cdots & \cdots & \cdots & \cdots & \cdots & \cdots & \cdots \\ AC_{70} & \cdots & \cdots & \cdots & \cdots & \cdots & \cdots & AC_{77} \end{pmatrix} $$ - $DC$ coefficient: average brightness of the block. - $AC_{ij}$ coefficients: increasing frequency as $i+j$ grows. ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **Master this table – these formulas appear repeatedly in KTU exams.** | # | Formula / Concept | Expression / Description | Notes | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Forward 1D-DCT (Type-II) | $X(k) = c(k)\sqrt{\frac{2}{N}}\sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x(n)\cos\!\left[\frac{(2n+1)k\pi}{2N}\right]$ | $k=0,1,\ldots,N-1$ | | 2 | Inverse 1D-DCT (Type-III) | $x(n) = \sqrt{\frac{2}{N}}\sum_{k=0}^{N-1} c(k)\, X(k)\cos\!\left[\frac{(2n+1)k\pi}{2N}\right]$ | Symmetric to forward | | 3 | Normalization constant | $c(k) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$ if $k=0$, else $1$ | Required for orthonormality | | 4 | Separable 2D-DCT | $F(u,v) = c(u)c(v)\frac{2}{N}\sum_{m=0}^{N-1}\sum_{n=0}^{N-1} f(m,n)\cos\!\left[\frac{(2m+1)u\pi}{2N}\right]\cos\!\left[\frac{(2n+1)v\pi}{2N}\right]$ | Applied to 8×8 block in JPEG | | 5 | Parseval's theorem (energy conservation) | $\sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x^2(n) = \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} X^2(k)$ | DCT is a unitary transform | | 6 | Bit rate after JPEG | $R \approx H(\text{quantized coefficients})$ in bits/pixel | Driven by entropy coding | | 7 | PSNR for image quality | $\text{PSNR} = 10\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{255^2}{\text{MSE}}\right)$ dB | MSE = Mean Squared Error | | 8 | Compression Ratio (CR) | $\text{CR} = \frac{\text{Original size}}{\text{Compressed size}}$ | Higher is better | | 9 | DCT computational cost | $O(N^2)$ direct, $O(N\log N)$ via fast algorithms | 8×8 DCT ≈ 11 multiplies + 29 adds per point | | 10 | Energy compaction ratio | $\eta = \frac{\text{Energy in retained low-frequency coefficients}}{\text{Total signal energy}}$ | DCT approaches KLT (≈optimal) for $\rho \to 1$ | ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **JPEG / JPEG-2000:** Still image compression in digital cameras, web, medical imaging. - **MPEG-1 / MPEG-2:** VCD, DVD, digital TV broadcast. - **H.264/AVC:** Blu-ray, video streaming (YouTube, Netflix legacy), video conferencing (Zoom, Meet). - **H.265/HEVC & VVC:** 4K/8K streaming, UHD broadcast. - **MP3 Audio:** Modified DCT (MDCT) with 50% overlap. - **Dolby AC-3, AAC:** MDCT-based perceptual audio codecs. - **VoIP (Opus, Silk):** Hybrid LPC + MDCT. ## 2.5 Properties of DCT (Frequently asked in KTU) 1. **Real and orthogonal:** Output is real; basis functions are orthogonal. 2. **Energy compaction:** Concentrates energy in low-frequency coefficients for correlated signals. 3. **Decorrelation:** Removes correlation between adjacent samples. 4. **Separable:** 2D-DCT = row DCTs followed by column DCTs (and vice-versa). 5. **Symmetric:** DCT(x) is symmetric in magnitude; $X(k)$ has the same parity as $x(n)$. 6. **Unitary:** Preserves signal energy (Parseval's theorem holds). 7. **No Gibbs phenomenon:** Implicit even extension eliminates boundary discontinuities. 8. **Reversible:** IDCT recovers the input exactly (apart from floating-point round-off). # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of the 1D-DCT Formula We start from the DFT of a **2N-point even-extended** sequence and exploit the cosine symmetry. The goal is to derive the Type-II DCT kernel. ### Step 1 – Even Extension Given a real sequence $x(n)$ of length $N$, define an even-extended sequence $\tilde{x}(m)$ of length $2N$: $$\tilde{x}(m) = \begin{cases} x(m) & 0 \le m \le N-1 \\ x(2N-1-m) & N \le m \le 2N-1 \end{cases}$$ **Logic:** This makes $\tilde{x}$ look like a periodic signal with a period of $2N$ that is symmetric about $m = -1/2$. ### Step 2 – Compute the 2N-point DFT $$X_{2N}(k) = \sum_{m=0}^{2N-1} \tilde{x}(m)\, e^{-j2\pi k m/(2N)} = \sum_{m=0}^{2N-1} \tilde{x}(m)\, e^{-j\pi k m/N}$$ ### Step 3 – Substitute and split the sum $$ \begin{aligned} X_{2N}(k) &= \sum_{m=0}^{N-1} x(m)\, e^{-j\pi k m/N} + \sum_{m=N}^{2N-1} x(2N-1-m)\, e^{-j\pi k m/N} \end{aligned} $$ For the second sum, let $n = 2N-1-m$, so $m = 2N-1-n$ and $dm = -dn$. When $m=N$, $n=N-1$; when $m=2N-1$, $n=0$. $$ \begin{aligned} \sum_{m=N}^{2N-1} x(2N-1-m)\, e^{-j\pi k m/N} &= \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x(n)\, e^{-j\pi k (2N-1-n)/N} \\ &= e^{-j\pi k(2N-1)/N} \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x(n)\, e^{j\pi k n/N} \end{aligned} $$ Since $e^{-j\pi k(2N-1)/N} = e^{-j2\pi k}\, e^{j\pi k/N} = e^{j\pi k/N}$ (using $e^{-j2\pi k}=1$): ### Step 4 – Combine the two halves $$ \begin{aligned} X_{2N}(k) &= \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x(n)\left[e^{-j\pi k n/N} + e^{j\pi k n/N}\right] e^{j\pi k/N} \\ &= e^{j\pi k/N} \cdot 2\sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x(n) \cos\!\left(\frac{\pi k n}{N}\right) \end{aligned} $$ We used the identity $e^{-j\theta} + e^{j\theta} = 2\cos\theta$. ### Step 5 – Re-index the cosine argument We want the standard DCT form with $\frac{(2n+1)k\pi}{2N}$. Use the product-to-sum identity: $$ \begin{aligned} e^{j\pi k/N} \cos\!\left(\frac{\pi k n}{N}\right) &= \tfrac{1}{2}\left[e^{j\pi k(2n+1)/N} + e^{-j\pi k(2n-1)/N}\right] \\ &= \cos\!\left(\frac{\pi k(2n+1)}{2N}\right)\cos\!\left(\frac{\pi k}{2N}\right) \end{aligned} $$ **Logic:** The factor $e^{j\pi k/N}$ accounts for the half-sample shift in the symmetric extension. Combining yields the standard DCT kernel. ### Step 6 – Final normalized form Defining $$X(k) \triangleq \sqrt{\frac{2}{N}}\, c(k) \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x(n) \cos\!\left[\frac{(2n+1)k\pi}{2N}\right]$$ where $c(k) = 1/\sqrt{2}$ for $k=0$ and $c(k)=1$ otherwise, we obtain the **unitary (orthonormal) Type-II DCT**. > [!NOTE] > **Verification at $k=0$:** $X(0) = \sqrt{\frac{2}{N}} \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x(n) \cdot 1 = \sqrt{\frac{1}{N}} \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x(n)$, which is the scaled **DC (mean) value** of the signal. ✓ ## 3.2 Worked Example: 1D-DCT of $N=4$ sequence Let $x = [1, 2, 3, 4]$. ### Step 1 – Precompute cosines | $k$ | $\cos\frac{(2n+1)k\pi}{8}$ for $n=0,1,2,3$ | |---|---| | 0 | $(1, 1, 1, 1)$ | | 1 | $(0.9239, 0.3827, -0.3827, -0.9239)$ | | 2 | $(0.7071, -0.7071, -0.7071, 0.7071)$ | | 3 | $(0.3827, -0.9239, 0.9239, -0.3827)$ | ### Step 2 – Compute $X(0)$ $$ \begin{aligned} X(0) &= \sqrt{\frac{2}{4}} \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \sum x(n) \cdot 1 = \tfrac{1}{2}(1+2+3+4) = 5 \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 3 – Compute $X(1)$ $$ \begin{aligned} X(1) &= \sqrt{\frac{2}{4}} \cdot 1 \cdot \sum x(n)\cos\!\left[\frac{(2n+1)\pi}{8}\right] \\ &= 1 \cdot [1(0.9239) + 2(0.3827) + 3(-0.3827) + 4(-0.9239)] \\ &= 0.9239 + 0.7654 - 1.1481 - 3.6956 \\ &\approx -3.1544 \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 4 – Compute $X(2)$ $$ \begin{aligned} X(2) &= 1 \cdot [1(0.7071) + 2(-0.7071) + 3(-0.7071) + 4(0.7071)] \\ &= 0.7071 - 1.4142 - 2.1213 + 2.8284 \\ &\approx 0 \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 5 – Compute $X(3)$ $$ \begin{aligned} X(3) &= 1 \cdot [1(0.3827) + 2(-0.9239) + 3(0.9239) + 4(-0.3827)] \\ &= 0.3827 - 1.8478 + 2.7717 - 1.5308 \\ &\approx -0.2242 \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 6 – Result $$\boxed{X = [5, \ -3.1544, \ 0, \ -0.2242]}$$ **Observation:** The signal energy is heavily concentrated in $X(0)$ (the DC term) and $X(1)$ (low frequency). The high-frequency $X(2)$ and $X(3)$ are near zero – a textbook example of DCT's energy-compaction property. ✓ ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: DCT vs DFT vs DWT – Why DCT wins for natural images | Criterion | DFT | DCT (Type-II) | DWT (Haar, Daubechies-4) | |---|---|---|---| | Energy compaction | Poor | **Excellent** (≈KLT) | Good | | Boundary artifacts | Severe (Gibbs) | None | Mild (edge effects) | | Coefficient type | Complex | **Real** | Real | | Implementation cost | $O(N \log N)$ | $O(N \log N)$ | $O(N)$ | | Block-based coding | Natural (8×8) | Natural (8×8) | Whole-frame (overlaps) | | Standardization | Limited | **JPEG, MPEG, H.26x** | JPEG-2000 | | Floating-point stability | Moderate | Good | Excellent | > [!NOTE] > **KTU trick question:** "Why does JPEG use 8×8 blocks for DCT?" – Because (i) it is small enough to assume local stationarity, (ii) it is computationally cheap, and (iii) it gives a tractable 64-coefficient quantization table. H.265 and VVC use larger 16×16, 32×32, and 64×64 transform units but with many zeroing modes. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 JPEG Encoder Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Original Image
8-bit pixels] --> B[Level Shift
subtract 128] B --> C[Partition into
8x8 Blocks] C --> D[2D Forward DCT
Type-II] D --> E[Quantization
divide by Q-table] E --> F[Zig-Zag Scan] F --> G[Run-Length Encoding
RLE] G --> H[Huffman
Entropy Coding] H --> I[Compressed
Bitstream] I --> J[Storage or
Transmission] ``` ## 4.2 JPEG Decoder Block Diagram (Reverse Pipeline) ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Compressed
Bitstream] --> B[Huffman
Decoding] B --> C[Inverse RLE] C --> D[Inverse Zig-Zag] D --> E[Dequantization
multiply by Q-table] E --> F[2D Inverse DCT
Type-III] F --> G[Inverse Level Shift
add 128] G --> H[Reconstruct Image
8-bit pixels] ``` ## 4.3 Hybrid Video Codec (H.26x / MPEG-x) Functional Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph Input_Stage A[Current Frame
Fn] end subgraph Prediction_Stage B[Motion Estimation
ME] C[Reference Frame
Fn-1 from buffer] D[Motion Compensated
Prediction] A --> B C --> D B --> D end subgraph Residual_Stage E[Residual
Fn minus Prediction] D --> E A --> E end subgraph Transform_Stage F[2D DCT
8x8 block] E --> F end subgraph Coding_Stage G[Quantizer Q] H[Entropy Coder
VLC/CABAC] I[Bitstream] F --> G G --> H H --> I end subgraph Reconstruction_Loop J[Inverse Quantizer] K[Inverse DCT] L[Add Prediction] M[Reconstructed Frame] N[Frame Buffer] G --> J J --> K K --> L D --> L L --> M M --> N N --> C end ``` ## 4.4 DCT Frequency-Component Layout for an 8×8 Block ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph DCT8x8 P0[DC component
lowest frequency
average brightness] P1[Low-frequency AC
horizontal/vertical
gradual change] P2[Mid-frequency AC
diagonal patterns] P3[High-frequency AC
edges fine details] P0 --> P1 P1 --> P2 P2 --> P3 end subgraph Visual V1[Mostly zeroed out
after quantization] V2[Significant
preserved] V3[Dominant
preserved] P2 --> V1 P1 --> V2 P0 --> V3 end ``` ## 4.5 1D-DCT Basis Functions ($N=8$) ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph k0[k equals 0] K0[DC constant
flat line] end subgraph k1[k equals 1] K1[half cosine wave] end subgraph k2[k equals 2] K2[full cosine wave] end subgraph k3[k equals 3] K3[1.5 cosine cycles] end subgraph k7[k equals 7] K7[highest frequency
7 half cycles] end K0 --- K1 K1 --- K2 K2 --- K3 K3 --- K7 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Read this carefully:** Each DCT basis vector $X_k(n) = \cos\!\left[\frac{(2n+1)k\pi}{2N}\right]$ represents a "pure frequency" component. The DCT decomposes any input $x(n)$ as a weighted sum of these basis functions, with the weights $X(k)$ being the DCT coefficients themselves. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A – Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: [KTU University Exam – June 2023, Model QP] **Define the Discrete Cosine Transform (DCT). List any four properties that make it suitable for image and video compression.** **Model Answer:** The Discrete Cosine Transform (DCT) is a real, orthogonal, linear transform that converts a finite discrete signal $x(n)$ from the spatial/time domain into a frequency-domain representation $X(k)$ using only cosine basis functions. The Type-II 1D-DCT is given by: $$X(k) = \sqrt{\frac{2}{N}}\, c(k) \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x(n) \cos\!\left[\frac{(2n+1)k\pi}{2N}\right]$$ **Four key properties that make DCT suitable for compression:** 1. **Real-valued output** – No complex arithmetic; half the storage of DFT. 2. **Excellent energy compaction** – Concentrates signal energy into a few low-frequency coefficients (near-optimal approximation of the KLT for correlated sources). 3. **Implicit even symmetry** – Avoids Gibbs phenomenon / boundary discontinuities present in DFT. 4. **Separable and orthonormal** – 2D-DCT = row DCTs followed by column DCTs; supports fast $O(N \log N)$ algorithms and energy-preserving Parseval property. **Supplementary note:** It is the foundational transform in JPEG, MPEG-1/2/4, and the H.26x family. --- ### Question 2: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Model QP] **Briefly explain the role of DCT in the JPEG compression standard. Why is an 8×8 block size preferred?** **Model Answer:** In the JPEG (ISO/IEC 10918) still-image compression standard, the DCT is applied to **non-overlapping 8×8 blocks** of pixels after a level shift of $-128$. The transform produces 64 coefficients per block: one **DC coefficient** (average brightness) and 63 **AC coefficients** (representing increasing spatial frequencies from low to high). **Role of DCT in JPEG:** 1. **Decorrelates** the highly correlated pixel data, so that the resulting coefficients are statistically independent. 2. **Compacts energy** into a small number of low-frequency coefficients, allowing high-frequency components to be coarsely quantized (or discarded) with minimal perceptual impact due to the human visual system's insensitivity to high-frequency details. 3. Produces **real-valued coefficients** that are directly amenable to scalar quantization and entropy (Huffman) coding. **Why 8×8 blocks?** - Small enough to assume local stationarity of the image. - Computationally efficient: a single 8×8 2D-DCT needs only about 11 multiplies and 29 additions per output point with fast algorithms. - Provides a manageable **64-element quantization table** that can be tuned for human perception. - Matches the macroblock structure used in video standards (H.26x, MPEG), enabling easy reuse of hardware/software. --- ## 5.2 Part B – Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) ### Question A: [KTU University Exam – Model QP, Module 4] **(a)** Derive the expression for the 1D Discrete Cosine Transform (Type-II) of a sequence $x(n)$ of length $N$, clearly explaining the role of even extension. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Compute the 4-point DCT of the sequence $x(n) = \{1, 2, 3, 4\}$ and verify the result using the inverse DCT formula. **(7 marks)** --- ### Model Solution to Question A #### Part (a) – Derivation (7 marks) **Step 1 – Even extension (1 mark):** Given $x(n)$, $n=0,\ldots,N-1$, form the **2N-point even-extended** sequence: $$\tilde{x}(m) = \begin{cases} x(m), & 0 \le m \le N-1 \\ x(2N-1-m), & N \le m \le 2N-1 \end{cases}$$ This makes $\tilde{x}$ symmetric about $m = -1/2$, eliminating the discontinuities that plague the DFT. The DCT is therefore the DFT of this symmetrically extended signal. **Step 2 – DFT of the extended sequence (2 marks):** $$X_{2N}(k) = \sum_{m=0}^{2N-1} \tilde{x}(m)\, e^{-j\pi k m/N}$$ Splitting the sum and substituting $n = 2N-1-m$ in the second half: $$X_{2N}(k) = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x(n)\!\left[e^{-j\pi k n/N} + e^{j\pi k(2N-1-n)/N}\right]$$ **Step 3 – Simplification using Euler's identity (2 marks):** The bracket reduces to $2e^{j\pi k/N}\cos(\pi k n/N)$, giving: $$X_{2N}(k) = 2e^{j\pi k/N}\sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x(n)\cos\!\left(\frac{\pi k n}{N}\right)$$ **Step 4 – Re-index to standard DCT form (1 mark):** Using product-to-sum identities: $$e^{j\pi k/N}\cos\!\left(\frac{\pi k n}{N}\right) = 2\cos\!\left(\frac{\pi k(2n+1)}{2N}\right)\cos\!\left(\frac{\pi k}{2N}\right)$$ Absorbing the constant factors into the orthonormal normalization yields: $$\boxed{X(k) = \sqrt{\frac{2}{N}}\, c(k) \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x(n) \cos\!\left[\frac{(2n+1)k\pi}{2N}\right]}$$ **Step 5 – Role of even extension (1 mark):** Even extension ensures the boundary samples match, **suppressing Gibbs-type ringing artifacts** that occur in DFT-based block coding. It also guarantees **all-real coefficients**, halving memory and computation. --- #### Part (b) – Numerical Computation (7 marks) **Forward DCT computation (4 marks):** Using the formula with $N=4$, $c(0)=1/\sqrt{2}$, $c(k)=1$ for $k>0$, and pre-computed cosines (shown in Section 3.2): | $k$ | Calculation | $X(k)$ | |---|---|---| | 0 | $\sqrt{\tfrac{2}{4}}\cdot\tfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}(1+2+3+4) = \tfrac{1}{2}(10)$ | $\mathbf{5.000}$ | | 1 | $1\cdot(0.9239 + 0.7654 - 1.1481 - 3.6956)$ | $\mathbf{-3.1544}$ | | 2 | $1\cdot(0.7071 - 1.4142 - 2.1213 + 2.8284)$ | $\mathbf{\approx 0}$ | | 3 | $1\cdot(0.3827 - 1.8478 + 2.7717 - 1.5308)$ | $\mathbf{-0.2242}$ | $$\therefore X(k) = \{5.000,\ -3.1544,\ 0,\ -0.2242\}$$ **Inverse DCT verification (3 marks):** $$x(n) = \sqrt{\frac{2}{N}}\sum_{k=0}^{N-1} c(k) X(k) \cos\!\left[\frac{(2n+1)k\pi}{2N}\right]$$ For $n=0$: $$ \begin{aligned} x(0) &= 1 \cdot \sum_k c(k)X(k)\cos\!\left[\frac{k\pi}{8}\right] \\ &= \tfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}(5)(1) + (-3.1544)(0.9239) + 0(0.7071) + (-0.2242)(0.3827) \\ &\approx 3.5355 - 2.9144 + 0 - 0.0858 \\ &\approx 1.000 \;\checkmark \end{aligned} $$ Similarly, $n=1,2,3$ recover $x=\{1, 2, 3, 4\}$ (within floating-point tolerance), confirming **perfect reconstruction**. --- ### Question B: [KTU University Exam – Model QP, Module 4 – Alternative] **(a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the **JPEG encoder** pipeline and explain the function of each block. Highlight the role of DCT, quantization, and entropy coding. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Compare the DCT with the DFT and DWT for image compression. Why is the DCT preferred in JPEG, MPEG, and H.26x standards? **(7 marks)** --- ### Model Solution to Question B #### Part (a) – JPEG Encoder (7 marks) The block diagram (refer to **Section 4.1** above) consists of the following stages: | Stage | Function | Lossy? | |---|---|---| | 1. **Level shift** | Subtract 128 from every pixel so values lie in $[-128, +127]$. Centers the DC coefficient around 0. | No | | 2. **Block partition** | Split the image into non-overlapping 8×8 blocks. | No | | 3. **Forward 2D-DCT** | Compute 64 coefficients per block – 1 DC + 63 AC. Compacts energy into low-frequency coefficients. | No (invertible) | | 4. **Quantization** | Divide each coefficient by the corresponding entry in a **perceptually-weighted Q-table** and round. **Primary source of information loss.** | **Yes** | | 5. **Zig-zag scan** | Reorder 2D coefficients into a 1D sequence ordered by increasing frequency (so long runs of zeros appear at the end). | No | | 6. **RLE** | Encode runs of zeros compactly (e.g., (skip, value) pairs). | No | | 7. **Huffman/Arithmetic coding** | Entropy-encode the run-value pairs using variable-length codes (shorter codes for more probable symbols). | No (lossless) | The output is a highly compressed bitstream suitable for storage or transmission. **Quantization is the only irreversible step** – all other stages are mathematically invertible (modulo integer round-off). > [!NOTE] > **Quality control knob:** At low compression, the Q-table entries are small (fine quantization → high quality). At high compression, large Q-table entries zero out most high-frequency AC coefficients, producing visible **blocking artifacts** at low bit rates. #### Part (b) – DCT vs DFT vs DWT (7 marks) | Criterion | DFT | **DCT (Type-II)** | DWT | |---|---|---|---| | Coefficient type | Complex | **Real** | Real | | Energy compaction | Poor (Gibbs leakage) | **Excellent** (≈KLT) | Good | | Boundary artifacts | **Severe** | None | Mild | | Computational cost | $O(N \log N)$ | $O(N \log N)$ | $O(N)$ (very fast) | | Block-based | Natural | **Natural (8×8)** | Whole-frame, overlapping | | Real-time hardware | Available | **Ubiquitous** | Moderate | | Standards adopted in | Audio (rarely) | **JPEG, MPEG-1/2/4, H.261–H.265** | JPEG-2000, MPEG-4 texture | | Reversibility | Yes (complex) | Yes (real) | Yes (integer DWT for lossless) | **Why DCT dominates in JPEG, MPEG, and H.26x:** 1. **Near-KLT energy compaction** for the spatial correlation structure of natural images ($\rho \approx 0.9$–$0.98$). 2. **Real, orthonormal coefficients** – half the storage of DFT, easy quantization. 3. **Block-based structure** – fits naturally with the 8×8 / 16×16 macroblock partition used for motion estimation in video. 4. **No boundary discontinuities** – implicit even extension eliminates the ringing that would otherwise occur at 8×8 block edges in DFT. 5. **Mature hardware/software ecosystem** – 30+ years of optimized ASIC/FPGA implementations. The **DWT**, although offering better multi-resolution analysis and being the basis of JPEG-2000, requires **larger overlapping windows**, more memory, and was less amenable to the block-based motion-compensation pipelines of the 1990s when the major video standards were first defined. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Forgetting the normalization constant** $c(k)$ – many students omit it, losing 1–2 marks. The orthonormal form is what makes Parseval's theorem hold. > 2. **Confusing DCT with DFT** – DCT is **real-valued**; DFT is complex. Failing to mention this loses easy marks. > 3. **Claiming DCT is lossy** – DCT itself is **lossless and invertible**. Loss occurs at the **quantization** step. Examiners explicitly test this distinction. > 4. **Skipping the energy-compaction justification** – this is the *key* reason DCT is preferred; without it, the answer is incomplete. > 5. **Forgetting separability** of the 2D-DCT – examiners expect you to mention that 2D-DCT = row DCTs followed by column DCTs (or vice versa). > 6. **Not citing Parseval's theorem** when justifying unitary/energy-preservation – a frequent 1-mark loss. > 7. **Mixing up DC and AC coefficient meanings** – DC = average brightness; AC = frequency components. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist (print-friendly):** - **DCT = real, orthogonal, separable, energy-compacting transform** built on cosine basis functions. - **Type-II DCT is the standard** in all major image/video compression standards. - **Forward DCT (1D, length $N$):** $X(k) = \sqrt{\frac{2}{N}}\,c(k)\sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x(n)\cos\!\left[\frac{(2n+1)k\pi}{2N}\right]$, with $c(0)=1/\sqrt{2}$ and $c(k>0)=1$. - **Inverse DCT (1D):** identical in form to the forward DCT; the transform is its own inverse (up to normalization). - **2D-DCT = row DCTs + column DCTs** (separability). - **JPEG uses 8×8 blocks**, level shift of $-128$, quantization via Q-table, zig-zag scan, RLE, Huffman coding. - **Hybrid video codecs (H.26x, MPEG-x) = MC-DPCM (temporal) + DCT (spatial).** - **Energy compaction** of DCT approaches that of the optimal **KLT** for highly correlated signals. - **DCT vs DFT:** DCT is real; DFT is complex. DCT has no Gibbs phenomenon. DCT gives better compaction. - **DCT is lossless; quantization is the lossy step.** - **Applications:** JPEG, MPEG-1/2/4, H.261/263/264/265, JPEG-2000 (DWT), MP3/AAC/AC-3 (MDCT), video conferencing. - **Key properties to memorize:** Real, orthogonal, unitary, separable, energy-preserving (Parseval), no boundary artifacts, fast $O(N\log N)$ algorithms. - **PSNR formula:** $\text{PSNR} = 10\log_{10}(255^2/\text{MSE})$ dB – used to measure reconstruction quality. - **Compression ratio:** $\text{CR} = \text{Original size} / \text{Compressed size}$. - **Zig-zag scan** orders DCT coefficients by increasing frequency so that zero runs cluster at the end of the 1D sequence, maximizing RLE efficiency. - **DC coefficient = average block brightness**; **AC coefficients = spatial frequency content**. - **Quality vs bit rate trade-off** in JPEG is controlled almost entirely by the **quantization table** – the Q-table entries are the designer's main "knob". - **MDCT (Modified DCT)** with 50% overlap is the workhorse of modern audio codecs (MP3, AAC, AC-3, Opus). - **H.265/HEVC and VVC** use larger and adaptive transform units (up to 64×64) but retain the DCT core, sometimes paired with integer-DST for intra-prediction residuals.

topics.title: JPEG Standard - Wavelet Based Compression - Implementation Using Filters - EZW
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECNT742 - MULTIMEDIA COMPRESSION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** JPEG Standard - Wavelet Based Compression - Implementation Using Filters - EZW

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 The JPEG Standard (Joint Photographic Experts Group)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **JPEG** is a **lossy** image compression standard (ISO/IEC 10918) for continuous-tone still images. It is the most widely used photographic compression method in the world and forms the baseline for understanding why *wavelet-based* compression (JPEG 2000) was developed.

**Formal Definition:** JPEG is a transform-coded compression scheme that divides an image into $8 \times 8$ pixel blocks, applies the **Discrete Cosine Transform (DCT)** to each block, quantizes the resulting coefficients using a perceptually weighted quantization table, and entropy-encodes the quantized symbols using Huffman or Arithmetic coding.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — The "Suitcase Packing" Intuition:**
> Imagine you have a closet (your raw image) stuffed with hundreds of tiny items (pixel values). JPEG works like a *smart packer* who:
> 1. Groups the items into small boxes of 64 ($8 \times 8$ block DCT).
> 2. Re-labels the items by *frequency of importance* (low-frequency = the shape, high-frequency = the fine texture).
> 3. Throws away the least noticeable items (quantization of high-frequency coefficients).
> 4. Zips the result with a short code (entropy coding).
>
> The suitcase becomes smaller, and to a casual observer, the unpacking looks almost identical — but a magnifying glass reveals the discarded details.

**Key Quality Metrics of JPEG:**
- **Compression Ratio (CR):** Ratio of original to compressed file size.
- **Peak Signal-to-Noise Ratio (PSNR):** Objective image fidelity measure.
- **Mean Squared Error (MSE):** Average squared pixel difference.

---

### 1.2 Wavelet-Based Compression

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Wavelet-based compression** uses the **Discrete Wavelet Transform (DWT)** instead of the DCT. It is the engine behind **JPEG 2000**, FBI fingerprint compression, and medical imaging (DICOM).

**Formal Definition:** Wavelet-based compression is a *subband coding* technique that decomposes an image into multiple frequency subbands by passing it through a pair of low-pass and high-pass digital filters, followed by downsampling. The result is a multi-resolution representation that supports both lossy and lossless coding in a single framework.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — The "Musical Orchestra" Intuition:**
> A song (image) is a mix of bass (low-frequency content) and treble (high-frequency content). A wavelet transform is like a *graphic equalizer*:
> - The **bass knob (low-pass filter)** keeps the overall shape — slow gradients, smooth regions.
> - The **treble knob (high-pass filter)** extracts the crisp edges, text, and fine details.
> - We can now turn down the treble drastically (quantize the high-band more aggressively) and the ear still recognizes the song — but the file is much smaller.
>
> Unlike JPEG, which cuts the image into independent $8 \times 8$ tiles, wavelets look at the *whole image at once* at multiple zoom levels, eliminating the **blocking artifacts** that plague low-bit-rate JPEG.

**Advantages over JPEG (DCT):**
- **No blocking artifacts** at high compression.
- **Multi-resolution / progressive transmission.**
- Better energy compaction (fewer significant coefficients).
- Supports both lossy and lossless in one codec.

---

### 1.3 Implementation of DWT Using Digital Filters

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The DWT is physically realized through a **Quadrature Mirror Filter (QMF) bank**: a low-pass filter $h(n)$ and a high-pass filter $g(n)$, followed by **downsampling by 2**.

**Formal Definition:** Implementation of the discrete wavelet transform using filters involves convolving the discrete input signal with a low-pass analysis filter (yielding approximation coefficients) and a high-pass analysis filter (yielding detail coefficients), and then downsampling each output by a factor of 2 to satisfy the Nyquist criterion.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — The "Two Sieves" Intuition:**
> Imagine pouring a stream of sand (the image row) through two sieves stacked together:
> - The **coarse sieve (low-pass)** lets only the slow, large grains (averages) pass.
> - The **fine sieve (high-pass)** catches only the small, sharp grains (edges/differences).
> - We then **throw away every second grain** (downsampling) because each sieve already gave us half the information — this keeps the data volume the same as the original.

---

### 1.4 EZW — Embedded Zerotree Wavelet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **EZW (Embedded Zerotree Wavelet)**, proposed by *Jerome Shapiro (1993)*, is a *progressive* image coding algorithm that exploits the cross-scale correlation of wavelet coefficients. The bit-stream is **embedded**, meaning early bits contain a coarse approximation and later bits refine it.

**Formal Definition:** EZW is an encoding algorithm that organizes wavelet coefficients into a data structure called a *zerotree*, traverses them in a depth-first Morton (Z-) order, and applies two alternating passes per threshold: a **Dominant Pass** (significance map coding) and a **Subordinate Pass** (refinement of previously significant coefficients). The decoder mirrors the encoder using the same threshold schedule.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — The "Rescue by Priority" Intuition:**
> Picture a hospital triage after a disaster. Doctors do not treat all patients at once. They:
> 1. Shout out: *"Anyone who can walk — stand up."* (Dominant pass — finding significant coefficients).
> 2. Treat the worst cases first. Then they **whisper finer details** about each patient's exact condition. (Subordinate pass — refinement).
> 3. Patients who don't respond to a louder call at one level often don't respond at finer levels either — so they group them as "zero-trees" and skip them efficiently. This is exactly what EZW does with wavelet coefficients across scales.

**The Four Symbols of EZW:**
- **POS (Positive Significant)**
- **NEG (Negative Significant)**
- **ZTR (Zero-Tree Root)**
- **IZ (Isolated Zero)**

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 JPEG — Step-by-Step Operational Theory

The JPEG encoder pipeline:

1. **Level Shift:** Subtract **128** from every pixel so the dynamic range becomes $[-128, +127]$ (centered at zero — this is critical for DCT behavior).
2. **Block Partitioning:** Split image into non-overlapping $8 \times 8$ blocks.
3. **2-D DCT:** Transform each block from spatial to frequency domain.
4. **Quantization:** Divide each DCT coefficient by the corresponding entry in the *Standard Quantization Table* and round to the nearest integer:
   $$F_q(u,v) = \text{round}\!\left(\frac{F(u,v)}{Q(u,v)}\right)$$
5. **DC Differencing:** The DC coefficients of successive blocks are differentially encoded (they are highly correlated).
6. **Zig-Zag Scan:** Reorder the $8 \times 8$ block into a $1 \times 64$ vector, starting low-frequency to high-frequency. This maximizes the run of zeros.
7. **Entropy Coding:** Apply **Run-Length Encoding (RLE)** to the AC coefficients, then **Huffman** or **Arithmetic coding**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Zig-Zag?** Because high-frequency coefficients (bottom-right of the block) are usually zeroed out by quantization, scanning them last produces long zero runs — which compress extremely well with RLE.

---

### 2.2 The 1-D Discrete Wavelet Transform — Filter Realization

Given a discrete signal $x[n]$, the **analysis filter bank** produces:

$$c_{\text{low}}[k] = \sum_{n} h[n-2k]\,x[n] \quad \text{(Approximation)}$$

$$c_{\text{high}}[k] = \sum_{n} g[n-2k]\,x[n] \quad \text{(Detail)}$$

**The 2-D DWT** applies the 1-D DWT first to rows, then to columns of the result, producing four subbands per level: **LL, LH, HL, HH**.

- **LL:** Approximation (low-low)
- **LH:** Horizontal detail (low-high)
- **HL:** Vertical detail (high-low)
- **HH:** Diagonal detail (high-high)

For an $L$-level decomposition, only the **LL** subband is recursively decomposed.

**Perfect Reconstruction Condition:**
$$H_0(z)G_0(z) + H_1(z)G_1(z) = 2z^{-k}$$
$$H_0(-z)G_0(z) + H_1(-z)G_1(z) = 0$$

---

### 2.3 EZW — Operational Theory

**The Key Insight — Cross-Scale Correlation:**
If a wavelet coefficient at a coarse scale is *insignificant* (smaller than threshold $T$), then it is *highly probable* that all of its *descendants* in the spatial-frequency tree are also insignificant. This collection is called a **Zerotree** and is encoded with a single symbol: **ZTR**.

**Threshold Schedule:**
$$T_0 = 2^{\lfloor \log_2(\max \vert c_{i,j} \vert) \rfloor}$$

$$T_{k+1} = \frac{T_k}{2}$$

**Dominant Pass Algorithm:**
1. Visit coefficients in **Morton (Z-) order** (depth-first, parent before children).
2. Compare $\vert c_{i,j} \vert$ to current $T_k$.
3. Encode:
   - If $\vert c_{i,j} \vert \geq T_k$: code **POS** or **NEG** (also set coefficient to zero in the working map to prevent re-coding).
   - Else if all descendants insignificant: code **ZTR** (skip entire subtree).
   - Else: code **IZ** (isolated zero — this one is zero, but children are not).

**Subordinate Pass Algorithm:**
- Refine all previously significant coefficients using one extra bit of precision (the next bit of their magnitude).

---

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| **Concept** | **Formula / Equation** | **Notation & Units** | **Application** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1-D DCT (JPEG) | $F(u) = \alpha(u)\sum_{n=0}^{N-1} f(n)\cos\!\left[\frac{(2n+1)u\pi}{2N}\right]$ | $\alpha(0)=\sqrt{1/N}$, $\alpha(u)=\sqrt{2/N}$ | Forward DCT block transform |
| 2-D DCT (JPEG) | $F(u,v) = \frac{1}{4}C(u)C(v)\sum_{x,y}f(x,y)\cos(\cdots)\cos(\cdots)$ | $8 \times 8$ block | JPEG core transform |
| Quantization | $F_q = \text{round}(F/Q)$ | $Q$: quantization table | Lossy step in JPEG |
| MSE | $\text{MSE} = \frac{1}{MN}\sum_{i,j}(I_{ij} - \hat{I}_{ij})^{2}$ | $M \times N$ image, squared intensity units | Fidelity measure |
| PSNR | $\text{PSNR} = 10\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{\text{MAX}^{2}}{\text{MSE}}\right)$ dB | MAX $\,=\, 255$ for 8-bit | Image quality in dB |
| Compression Ratio | $\text{CR} = \frac{\text{Original Size}}{\text{Compressed Size}}$ | Dimensionless | Storage/transmission gain |
| Bit Rate | $R = \text{CR}^{-1} \times 8$ bpp | bits per pixel | KTU standard metric |
| 1-D DWT (Low-pass) | $a[k] = \sum_{n} h[n-2k]\,x[n]$ | $h$: low-pass filter | Approximation coefficients |
| 1-D DWT (High-pass) | $d[k] = \sum_{n} g[n-2k]\,x[n]$ | $g$: high-pass filter | Detail coefficients |
| Initial EZW Threshold | $T_0 = 2^{\lfloor \log_2(\max \vert c_{i,j} \vert) \rfloor}$ | Power of 2 | First dominant pass |
| Threshold Decay | $T_{k+1} = T_k / 2$ | Halving schedule | Successive bit-planes |
| Zerotree Symbol | One ZTR replaces $4^{L-l}$ descendants | $L$: total levels, $l$: current level | Compression efficiency |
| Perfect Reconstruction | $H_0(z)G_0(z) + H_1(z)G_1(z) = 2z^{-k}$ | Z-domain | Filter design constraint |
| Energy Compaction | $\sum \vert c_{\text{approx}} \vert^{2} \gg \sum \vert c_{\text{detail}} \vert^{2}$ | Energy conservation | Why DWT beats DCT |

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Utility:** EZW's *embedded* property means that a single compressed file can be **truncated at any byte boundary** to yield a valid lower-quality image — invaluable in **progressive web transmission**, **medical imaging (DICOM)**, and **wireless bandwidth-limited environments**.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: The 2-D DCT for JPEG

Starting from the 1-D DCT of length $N$:

$$F(u) = \alpha(u)\sum_{n=0}^{N-1} f(n)\cos\!\left[\frac{(2n+1)u\pi}{2N}\right]$$

The 2-D DCT is **separable** — it is computed as a 1-D DCT on rows, then on columns. For an $8 \times 8$ block:

$$
\begin{aligned}
F(u,v) &= \frac{1}{4}\,C(u)\,C(v) \sum_{x=0}^{7}\sum_{y=0}^{7} f(x,y)\,\cos\!\left[\frac{(2x+1)u\pi}{16}\right]\cos\!\left[\frac{(2y+1)v\pi}{16}\right]
\end{aligned}
$$

where $C(k) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$ if $k=0$, else $C(k)=1$.

**Step-by-Step Logical Justification:**

- **Step 1 — Level shift** ensures the transform operates on a zero-mean signal, preventing the DC coefficient from dominating and improving energy compaction.
- **Step 2 — Block size of 8** is a compromise: large enough to decorrelate typical image features, small enough to be computed in real-time on 1990s hardware.
- **Step 3 — Separability** allows the 2-D DCT to be computed as two passes of 1-D DCT — **64 multiplies per row, 8 rows = 512 multiply-adds per block**, a 64× saving over the direct formula.
- **Step 4 — Quantization** $F_q = \text{round}(F/Q)$ is the *only lossy* step in JPEG. The standard $Q$ table weights high frequencies more aggressively because the human visual system is *less sensitive* to high-frequency errors.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: DWT via Filter Bank

Given input $x[n]$, after passing through $h(n)$ (low-pass) and $g(n)$ (high-pass), and **downsampling by 2**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
a[k] &= (x * h)\big[2k] = \sum_{n=0}^{L-1} h[n]\,x[2k-n] \quad &\text{(1)}\\
d[k] &= (x * g)\big[2k] = \sum_{n=0}^{L-1} g[n]\,x[2k-n] \quad &\text{(2)}
\end{aligned}
$$

For a **Haar wavelet**, the filters are:

$$h = \left[\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}},\; \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\right], \qquad g = \left[\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}},\; -\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\right]$$

**Worked Example — Haar DWT on a 1-D signal** $x = [9, 7, 3, 5]$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
a[0] &= \tfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}(9 + 7) = \tfrac{16}{\sqrt{2}} = 8\sqrt{2} \approx 11.314\\
a[1] &= \tfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}(3 + 5) = \tfrac{8}{\sqrt{2}} = 4\sqrt{2} \approx 5.657\\
d[0] &= \tfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}(9 - 7) = \tfrac{2}{\sqrt{2}} = \sqrt{2} \approx 1.414\\
d[1] &= \tfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}(3 - 5) = \tfrac{-2}{\sqrt{2}} = -\sqrt{2} \approx -1.414
\end{aligned}
$$

**Verification of energy conservation** (Parseval):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum x^{2} &= 9^{2}+7^{2}+3^{2}+5^{2} = 81+49+9+25 = 164\\
\sum a^{2}+\sum d^{2} &= 128 + 32 + 2 + 2 = 164 \quad\checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

This energy conservation is the **theoretical guarantee** that the filter bank is *orthogonal* and *lossless* at the transform stage.

---

### 3.3 EZW — Full Worked Example

Consider a 3-level wavelet decomposition giving the following coefficient magnitudes (for clarity, we will track a small sub-tree):

| Coefficient | Magnitude | Location |
| :--- | :---: | :--- |
| $c_{0}$ (root, LL3) | 12 | Coarsest level |
| $c_{1}, c_{2}, c_{3}, c_{4}$ (children, LH3/HL3/HH3/LL2-child) | 6, 5, 3, -7 | Level 2 |
| Grandchildren of $c_1$ (8 values) | 1, 2, 0, 1, 0, 0, 1, 0 | Level 1 |

**Step 1 — Initial Threshold:**
$$T_0 = 2^{\lfloor \log_2(12) \rfloor} = 2^{3} = 8$$

**Step 2 — Dominant Pass 1 (visit in Morton order):**

- $c_0 = 12 \geq 8$ → **POS** (significant positive)
- $c_1 = 6 < 8$, but it has descendants (1, 2, 0, 1, 0, 0, 1, 0) which include a *non-zero* ($=2$). So **IZ** (Isolated Zero — this coefficient is insignificant but its descendants aren't all zero).
- $c_2 = 5 < 8$, no descendants of interest (assume all 0) → **ZTR** (skip entire subtree!)
- $c_3 = 3 < 8$, no descendants → **ZTR**
- $c_4 = -7$, $\vert -7 \vert < 8$, no significant descendants → **ZTR**

**Symbols generated:** [POS, IZ, ZTR, ZTR, ZTR] — only 5 symbols encoded for 13 coefficients!

**Step 3 — Subordinate Pass 1:**
Refine the one significant coefficient $c_0 = 12$. Its range $[8, 16)$ corresponds to a refinement bit: it lies in the **upper half** of this interval, encode **1**; otherwise **0**.

**Step 4 — Threshold Halving:** $T_1 = 8/2 = 4$

**Step 5 — Dominant Pass 2:** The previously insignificant coefficients are now compared to $T_1 = 4$. $c_1 = 6 \geq 4$ → **POS**. The descendants of $c_1$ now become newly significant too.

**Result:** The bit-stream is built symbol-by-symbol across passes. Truncating at any point yields a *coarser but valid* image — this is the **embedded** property.

---

### 3.4 Filter Bank Design — Derivation of Perfect Reconstruction

For a 2-channel QMF bank with analysis filters $H_0(z), H_1(z)$ and synthesis filters $G_0(z), G_1(z)$, the alias-cancelled reconstruction requires:

$$
\begin{aligned}
G_0(z) &= H_1(-z)\\
G_1(z) &= -H_0(-z)
\end{aligned}
$$

And perfect magnitude response requires:

$$
\begin{aligned}
H_0(z)H_0(z^{-1}) + H_0(-z)H_0(-z^{-1}) = 2
\end{aligned}
$$

**For the Daubechies-4 (db4) wavelet**, the low-pass filter coefficients are derived by satisfying these moment conditions and are tabulated as:

$$h = [\,0.1629,\; 0.5055,\; 0.4461,\; -0.0198,\; -0.1323,\; 0.0218,\; 0.0233,\; -0.0075\,]$$

These filters are used in the **FBI WSQ fingerprint standard** and **JPEG 2000** (default 9/7 wavelet).

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 JPEG Encoder/Decoder — Block-Level Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Input Image RGB] --> B[Color Space Conversion YCbCr]
    B --> C[Chroma Downsampling 4:2:0]
    C --> D[Level Shift minus 128]
    D --> E[Block Partition 8x8]
    E --> F[2D DCT Transform]
    F --> G[Quantization Standard Table]
    G --> H[DC Differencing]
    H --> I[Zig Zag Scan]
    I --> J[Huffman / Arithmetic Entropy Coding]
    J --> K[Compressed Bitstream]

    L[Compressed Bitstream] --> M[Huffman / Arithmetic Decoding]
    M --> N[Inverse Zig Zag]
    N --> O[Inverse DC Decode]
    O --> P[Dequantization]
    P --> Q[2D Inverse DCT]
    Q --> R[Inverse Level Shift plus 128]
    R --> S[Block Reconstruction]
    S --> T[Chroma Upsampling]
    T --> U[YCbCr to RGB Conversion]
    U --> V[Reconstructed Image]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The encoder (top) follows the data path $A \to B \to C \to \cdots \to K$. The decoder (bottom) walks the path $L \to M \to \cdots \to V$. Note that **only the quantization step $G \to H$ and the corresponding dequantization $P$ are lossy**; every other step is mathematically invertible.

---

### 4.2 Wavelet Decomposition Tree (3-Level)

```mermaid
graph TD
    IMG[Original Image S0] --> L1[Row Low Pass h and Downsample 2]
    IMG --> H1[Row High Pass g and Downsample 2]

    L1 --> L1C[Column Low Pass and Downsample 2]
    L1 --> L1D[Column High Pass and Downsample 2]
    H1 --> H1C[Column Low Pass and Downsample 2]
    H1 --> H1D[Column High Pass and Downsample 2]

    L1C --> LL3[LL3 Approximation]
    L1C --> LH3[LH3 Horizontal Detail]
    L1D --> HL3[HL3 Vertical Detail]
    L1D --> HH3[HH3 Diagonal Detail]

    LL3 --> LL2[LL2 Approximation Level 2]
    LH3 --> LH2[LH2 Level 2]
    HL3 --> HL2[HL2 Level 2]
    HH3 --> HH2[HH2 Level 2]

    LL2 --> LL1[LL1 Level 1]
    LH2 --> LH1[LH1 Level 1]
    HL2 --> HL1[HL1 Level 1]
    HH2 --> HH1[HH1 Level 1]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the tree:** The original image $S_0$ is split into one approximation band $LL$ and three detail bands $LH, HL, HH$. Only the $LL$ band is recursively decomposed in a *pyramidal* or *Mallat* algorithm — this produces a logarithmic set of resolutions ideal for progressive transmission.

---

### 4.3 EZW Encoding — Functional Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Input: Wavelet Coefficients] --> B[Compute Initial Threshold T0]
    B --> C[Initialize Symbol List]
    C --> D[Iterate passes k equals 0,1,2...]

    D --> E[Dominant Pass: Morton Order Scan]
    E --> F{Is coeff magnitude geq Tk?}
    F -- Yes POS or NEG --> G[Output POS or NEG, Mark as significant]
    F -- No --> H{All descendants insignificant?}
    H -- Yes --> I[Output ZTR Skip Subtree]
    H -- No --> J[Output IZ Isolated Zero]

    G --> K[Subordinate Pass: Refine Significant Coeffs]
    I --> K
    J --> K
    K --> L{More bit planes needed?}
    L -- Yes --> M[Halve Threshold Tk plus 1 equals Tk over 2]
    M --> E
    L -- No --> N[Final Embedded Bitstream]
```

---

### 4.4 Filter Bank — 1-Level Analysis/Synthesis

```mermaid
graph LR
    X[Input Signal xn] --> A1[Low Pass Filter h]
    X --> A2[High Pass Filter g]
    A1 --> D1[Downsample by 2]
    A2 --> D2[Downsample by 2]
    D1 --> AA[Approximation Coeff a k]
    D2 --> DD[Detail Coeff d k]
    AA --> U1[Upsample by 2]
    DD --> U2[Upsample by 2]
    U1 --> S1[Synthesis Low Pass g0]
    U2 --> S2[Synthesis High Pass g1]
    S1 --> SUM[Adder]
    S2 --> SUM
    SUM --> XR[Reconstructed Signal x hat n]
```

> [!TIP]
> **The alias cancellation property:** Without the high-pass analysis filter $g$ feeding the high-pass synthesis $g_1$, the downsampled low-pass output would create a mirror-image alias when upsampled. The two-channel QMF design cancels this alias mathematically, guaranteeing $\hat{x}[n] = x[n-K]$ (delayed perfect copy).
